subject: Math
description: Partition shapes into parts with equal areas. Express the area of each part as a unit fraction of the whole.
description_plain: Your child can divide shapes into equal parts and describe each part as a fraction of the whole shape.

subject: Math
description: Understand that shapes in different categories (e.g., rhombuses, rectangles, and others) may share attributes (e.g., having four sides), and that the shared attributes can define a larger category (e.g., quadrilaterals). Recognize rhombuses, rectangles, and squares as examples of quadrilaterals, and draw examples of quadrilaterals that do not belong to any of these subcategories.
description_plain: Your child can recognize that shapes like rhombuses, rectangles, and squares share features like having four sides, and can sort and compare shapes by their sides and angles, including drawing quadrilaterals that don't fit into those groups.

subject: Math
description: Reason with shapes and their attributes.
description_plain: Your child can think about shapes and their features.

subject: Math
description: Solve word problems involving pennies, nickels, dimes, quarters, and bills greater than one dollar, using the dollar and cent symbols appropriately.
description_plain: Your child can solve word problems using pennies, nickels, dimes, quarters, and bills over one dollar, using the correct symbols for dollars and cents.

subject: Math
description: Work with money
description_plain: Work with money

subject: Math
description: Solve real-world and mathematical problems involving perimeters of polygons, including finding the perimeter given the side lengths, finding an unknown side length, and exhibiting rectangles with the same perimeter and different areas or with the same area and different perimeters.
description_plain: Your child can solve problems about the perimeter of shapes by adding side lengths, finding missing sides, and exploring how shapes with the same perimeter can have different areas.

subject: Math
description: Geometric measurement: recognize perimeter as an attribute of plane figures and distinguish between linear and area measures.
description_plain: Your child understands that perimeter measures the distance around a shape and can tell the difference between length and area measurements.

subject: Math
description: Use tiling to show in a concrete case that the area of a rectangle with whole-number side lengths a and b + c is the sum of a × b and a × c. Use area models to represent the distributive property in mathematical reasoning.
description_plain: Your child can use small squares to show that the area of a rectangle with sides a and b + c is the same as adding the areas of rectangles with sides a and b, and a and c.

subject: Math
description: Multiply side lengths to find areas of rectangles with whole-number side lengths in the context of solving real-world and mathematical problems, and represent whole-number products as rectangular areas in mathematical reasoning.
description_plain: Your child multiplies side lengths to find the area of rectangles and uses this to solve problems.

subject: Math
description: Find the area of a rectangle with whole-number side lengths by tiling it, and show that the area is the same as would be found by multiplying the side lengths.
description_plain: Your child finds the area of a rectangle by counting squares or multiplying side lengths.

subject: Math
description: Relate area to the operations of multiplication and addition.
description_plain: Your child understands that area relates to multiplication and addition.

subject: Math
description: Measure areas by counting unit squares (square cm, square m, square in, square ft, and improvised units).
description_plain: Your child can measure area by counting square units like square centimeters or square inches.

subject: Math
description: A plane figure that can be covered without gaps or overlaps by n unit squares is said to have an area of n square units.
description_plain: Your child understands that the area of a flat shape is the number of unit squares that cover it without gaps or overlaps.

subject: Math
description: A square with side length 1 unit, called "a unit square," is said to have "one square unit" of area, and can be used to measure area.
description_plain: Your child understands that a square with sides one unit long is called a unit square and measures one square unit of area.

subject: Math
description: Recognize area as an attribute of plane figures and understand concepts of area measurement.
description_plain: Your child knows that area is a property of flat shapes and understands what it means to measure area.

subject: Math
description: Geometric measurement: understand concepts of area and relate area to multiplication and to addition.
description_plain: Your child understands what area means and how it connects to multiplication and addition.

subject: Math
description: Generate measurement data by measuring lengths using rulers marked with halves and fourths of an inch. Show the data by making a line plot, where the horizontal scale is marked off in appropriate units—whole numbers, halves, or quarters.
description_plain: Your child measures lengths using rulers marked in halves and quarters of an inch and shows the results on a line plot.

subject: Math
description: Draw a scaled picture graph and a scaled bar graph to represent a data set with several categories. Solve one- and two-step "how many more" and "how many less" problems using information presented in scaled bar graphs.
description_plain: Your child can draw picture and bar graphs with scales to show data and solve questions about how much more or less one category has compared to another.

subject: Math
description: Represent and interpret data.
description_plain: Represent and interpret data.

subject: Math
description: Measure and estimate liquid volumes and masses of objects using standard units of grams (g), kilograms (kg), and liters (l). Add, subtract, multiply, or divide to solve one-step word problems involving masses or volumes that are given in the same units, e.g., by using drawings (such as a beaker with a measurement scale) to represent the problem.
description_plain: Your child can measure and estimate how much liquids and objects weigh or hold using grams, kilograms, and liters, and solve simple word problems using drawings.

subject: Math
description: Solve word problems involving addition and subtraction of time intervals in minutes, e.g., by representing the problem on a number line diagram.
description_plain: Your child can solve word problems about adding and subtracting time in minutes by using a number line.

subject: Math
description: Calculate elapsed time greater than 60 minutes to the nearest quarter and half hour on a number line diagram.
description_plain: Your child can find elapsed time longer than 60 minutes to the nearest quarter or half hour using a number line.

subject: Math
description: Tell and write time to the nearest minute and measure time intervals in minutes, within 60 minutes, on an analog and digital clock.
description_plain: Your child can tell and write time to the nearest minute and measure time intervals up to 60 minutes on both analog and digital clocks.

subject: Math
description: Understand time to the nearest minute.
description_plain: Your child understands how to tell time to the nearest minute.

subject: Math
description: Solve problems involving measurement and estimation of intervals of time, liquid volumes, and masses of objects.
description_plain: Your child can solve problems involving measuring and estimating time, liquid amounts, and weights.

subject: Math
description: Compare two fractions with the same numerator or the same denominator by reasoning about their size. Recognize that comparisons are valid only when the two fractions refer to the same whole. Record the results of comparisons with the symbols >, =, or <, and justify the conclusions, e.g., by using a visual fraction model.
description_plain: Your child compares two fractions with the same top or bottom number by thinking about their size and uses >, =, or < to show which is bigger or if they are equal.

subject: Math
description: Express whole numbers as fractions, and recognize fractions that are equivalent to whole numbers.
description_plain: Your child can write whole numbers as fractions and recognize when fractions are equal to whole numbers.

subject: Math
description: Recognize and generate simple equivalent fractions, e.g., ½ = 2/4, 4/6 = 2/3. Explain why the fractions are equivalent, e.g., by using a visual fraction model.
description_plain: Your child can recognize and create simple equivalent fractions like 1/2 = 2/4 and explain why they are equal using pictures.

subject: Math
description: Understand two fractions as equivalent (equal) if they are the same size, or the same point on a number line.
description_plain: Your child understands that two fractions are equal if they represent the same amount or point on a number line.

subject: Math
description: Explain equivalence of fractions with denominators 2, 3, 4, 6, and 8 in special cases, and compare fractions by reasoning about their size.
description_plain: Your child can explain when fractions with denominators 2, 3, 4, 6, and 8 are equal and compare fractions by thinking about their size.

subject: Math
description: Represent a fraction a/b on a number line diagram by marking off a lengths 1/b from 0. Recognize that the resulting interval has size a/b and that its endpoint locates the number a/b on the number line.
description_plain: Your child can show a fraction on a number line by marking equal parts and understands where the fraction fits on the line.

subject: Math
description: Represent a fraction 1/b on a number line diagram by defining the interval from 0 to 1 as the whole and partitioning it into b equal parts. Recognize that each part has size 1/b and that the endpoint of the part based at 0 locates the number 1/b on the number line.
description_plain: Your child can show the fraction 1/b on a number line by dividing the space from 0 to 1 into equal parts and identifying one part.

subject: Math
description: Understand a fraction with denominators 2, 3, 4, 6, and 8 as a number on a number line diagram.
description_plain: Your child understands fractions with denominators 2, 3, 4, 6, and 8 as numbers on a number line.

subject: Math
description: Understand a fraction 1/b, with denominators 2, 3, 4, 6, and 8, as the quantity formed by 1 part when a whole is partitioned into b equal parts; understand a fraction a/b as the quantity formed by a parts of size 1/b.
description_plain: Your child understands that a fraction like 1/b means one part when a whole is split into b equal parts, and a/b means a parts of size 1/b.

subject: Math
description: Develop understanding of fractions as numbers.
description_plain: Your child learns that fractions are numbers and how they work.

subject: Math
description: Multiply one-digit whole numbers by multiples of 10 in the range 10–90 (e.g., 9 × 80, 5 × 60) using strategies based on place value and properties of operations.
description_plain: Your child can multiply one-digit numbers by multiples of 10 (like 9 × 80) using place value and math rules.

subject: Math
description: Fluently add and subtract within 1000 using strategies and algorithms based on place value, properties of operations, and/or the relationship between addition and subtraction.
description_plain: Your child can quickly add and subtract numbers up to 1000 using strategies based on place value and math facts.

subject: Math
description: Use place value understanding to round whole numbers to the nearest 10 or 100.
description_plain: Your child can round whole numbers to the nearest 10 or 100 using place value.

subject: Math
description: Use place value understanding and properties of operations to perform multi-digit arithmetic.
description_plain: Your child uses their understanding of place value and math rules to solve problems with bigger numbers.

subject: Math
description: Identify arithmetic patterns (including patterns in the addition table or multiplication table), and explain them using properties of operations.
description_plain: Your child can find patterns in addition and multiplication and explain them using math rules.

subject: Math
description: Solve two-step word problems using the four operations. Represent these problems using equations with a letter standing for the unknown quantity. Assess the reasonableness of answers using mental computation and estimation strategies including rounding.
description_plain: Your child can solve two-step word problems using addition, subtraction, multiplication, or division and check their answers by estimating.

subject: Math
description: Solve problems involving the four operations, and identify and explain patterns in arithmetic.
description_plain: Your child can solve math problems using addition, subtraction, multiplication, and division, and can find and explain patterns in numbers.

subject: Math
description: Fluently multiply and divide within 100, using strategies such as the relationship between multiplication and division (e.g., knowing that 8 × 5 = 40, one knows 40 ÷ 5 = 8) or properties of operations. By the end of Grade 3, know from memory all products of two one-digit numbers.
description_plain: Your child can quickly multiply and divide numbers up to 100 using strategies like knowing related facts and math properties, and knows all multiplication and division facts for single-digit numbers by memory.

subject: Math
description: Multiply and divide within 100.
description_plain: Your child can multiply and divide numbers up to 100.

subject: Math
description: Understand division as an unknown-factor problem.
description_plain: Your child understands division as finding an unknown number that, when multiplied, gives the original number.

subject: Math
description: Apply properties of operations as strategies to multiply and divide.
description_plain: Your child uses rules about how numbers work to help solve multiplication and division problems.

subject: Math
description: Understand properties of multiplication and the relationship between multiplication and division.
description_plain: Your child understands how multiplication works and how it is connected to division.

subject: Math
description: Determine the unknown whole number in a multiplication or division equation relating three whole numbers.
description_plain: Your child can find the missing number in multiplication or division problems without needing special math words.

subject: Math
description: Use multiplication and division within 100 to solve word problems in situations involving equal groups, arrays, and measurement quantities, e.g., by using drawings and equations with a symbol for the unknown number to represent the problem.
description_plain: Your child can solve word problems about equal groups, arrays, or measurements using multiplication and division, and write equations with a letter for the unknown.

subject: Math
description: Interpret whole-number quotients of whole numbers, e.g., interpret 56 ÷ 8 as the number of objects in each share when 56 objects are partitioned equally into 8 shares, or as a number of shares when 56 objects are partitioned into equal shares of 8 objects each.
description_plain: Your child can explain division problems like 56 ÷ 8 by showing how many objects are in each group or how many groups there are.

subject: Math
description: Interpret products of whole numbers, e.g., interpret 5 × 7 as the total number of objects in 5 groups of 7 objects each.
description_plain: Your child can understand multiplication as putting equal groups together, like seeing 5 × 7 as 5 groups of 7 objects each.

subject: Math
description: Represent and solve problems involving multiplication and division.
description_plain: Your child can solve problems using multiplication and division.

subject: Math
description: Look for and express regularity in repeated reasoning.
description_plain: Your child can notice and explain patterns or repeated steps when solving problems.

subject: Math
description: Look for and make use of structure.
description_plain: Your child can notice and use the way math problems are put together to help solve them.

subject: Math
description: Attend to precision.
description_plain: Attend to precision.

subject: Math
description: Use appropriate tools strategically.
description_plain: Your child can choose and use the right tools, like calculators, rulers, or graphs, to help solve math problems.

subject: Math
description: Model with mathematics.
description_plain: Model with mathematics.

subject: Math
description: Construct viable arguments and critique the reasoning of others.
description_plain: Your child can explain their math thinking and listen to and question the ideas of others.

subject: Math
description: Reason abstractly and quantitatively.
description_plain: Your child can think about math problems in different ways, using numbers and real-life examples.

subject: Math
description: Make sense of problems and persevere in solving them.
description_plain: Your child can understand what a math problem is asking and keep working until they find a solution.

subject: Math
description: Classify quadrilaterals in a hierarchy based on properties. (Students will define a trapezoid as a quadrilateral with at least one pair of parallel sides.)
description_plain: Your child can sort four-sided shapes by their features and knows that a trapezoid has at least one pair of parallel sides.

subject: Math
description: Understand that attributes belonging to a category of two-dimensional figures also belong to all subcategories of that category.
description_plain: Your child understands that if a shape has certain features, then all shapes in smaller groups within that category also have those features.

subject: Math
description: Classify two-dimensional figures into categories based on their properties.
description_plain: Your child can group flat shapes into categories based on their features.

subject: Math
description: Represent real-world and mathematical problems by graphing points in the first quadrant of the coordinate plane, and interpret coordinate values of points in the context of the situation.
description_plain: Your child can plot points on a graph in the first quadrant and understand what the points mean in real-life or math problems.

subject: Math
description: Use a pair of perpendicular number lines, called axes, to define a coordinate system, with the intersection of the lines (the origin) arranged to coincide with the 0 on each line and a given point in the plane located by using an ordered pair of numbers, called its coordinates. Understand that the first number in the ordered pair indicates how far to travel from the origin in the direction of one axis, and the second number in the ordered pair indicates how far to travel in the direction of the second axis, with the convention that the names of the two axes and the coordinates correspond (e.g., x-axis and x-coordinate, y-axis and y-coordinate).
description_plain: Your child can use two number lines that cross at zero to find points on a grid by using pairs of numbers that tell how far to go along each line.

subject: Math
description: Graph points on the coordinate plane to solve real-world and mathematical problems.
description_plain: Your child can put points on a graph to help solve real-world and math problems.

subject: Math
description: Recognize volume as additive. Find volumes of solid figures composed of two non-overlapping right rectangular prisms by adding the volumes of the non-overlapping parts, applying this technique to solve real-world problems.
description_plain: Your child knows that the volume of a solid made of two rectangular boxes is the sum of their volumes and can use this to solve real problems.

subject: Math
description: Apply the formulas V = l × w × h and V = b × h for rectangular prisms to find volumes of right rectangular prisms with whole-number edge lengths in the context of solving real-world and mathematical problems.
description_plain: Your child can use formulas to find the volume of box-shaped objects by multiplying their length, width, and height.

subject: Math
description: Find the volume of a right rectangular prism with whole-number side lengths by packing it with unit cubes, and show that the volume is the same as would be found by multiplying the edge lengths, equivalently by multiplying the height by the area of the base. Represent threefold whole-number products as volumes, e.g., to represent the associative property of multiplication.
description_plain: Your child can find the volume of a rectangular box by filling it with unit cubes and show that multiplying the edges or the base area by height gives the same volume.

subject: Math
description: Relate volume to the operations of multiplication and addition and solve real-world and mathematical problems involving volume.
description_plain: Your child understands how volume relates to multiplication and addition and can solve problems about the volume of rectangular boxes.

subject: Math
description: Measure volumes by counting unit cubes, using cubic cm, cubic in, cubic ft, and improvised units.
description_plain: Your child can measure volume by counting unit cubes and using units like cubic centimeters, inches, feet, or other units.

subject: Math
description: A solid figure that can be packed without gaps or overlaps using n unit cubes is said to have a volume of n cubic units.
description_plain: Your child understands that the volume of a solid shape is the number of unit cubes that fit inside it without gaps or overlaps.

subject: Math
description: A cube with side length 1 unit, called a "unit cube," is said to have "one cubic unit" of volume, and can be used to measure volume.
description_plain: Your child knows that a cube with sides of one unit has a volume of one cubic unit and can use it to measure volume.

subject: Math
description: Recognize volume as an attribute of solid figures and understand concepts of volume measurement.
description_plain: Your child understands that volume is a property of solid shapes and learns how to measure it.

subject: Math
description: Geometric measurement: understand concepts of volume and relate volume to multiplication and to addition.
description_plain: Your child learns about volume and how it connects to multiplication and addition.

subject: Math
description: Make a line plot to display a data set of measurements in fractions of a unit (½, ¼, 1/8). Use operations on fractions for this grade to solve problems involving information presented in line plots.
description_plain: Your child can make a line plot to show measurements in fractions like ½, ¼, or 1/8 and use fraction operations to solve problems from the plot.

subject: Math
description: Represent and interpret data.
description_plain: Represent and interpret data.

subject: Math
description: Convert among different-sized standard measurement units within a given measurement system, and use these conversions in solving multi-step, real-world problems (e.g., convert 5 cm to 0.05 m; 9 ft to 108 in).
description_plain: Your child can change measurements from one unit to another within the same system (like cm to m) and use these changes to solve multi-step real-world problems.

subject: Math
description: Convert like measurement units within a given measurement system.
description_plain: Your child can convert between units that measure the same thing within one measurement system.

subject: Math
description: Solve real-world problems involving division of unit fractions by non-zero whole numbers and division of whole numbers by unit fractions, e.g., by using visual fraction models and equations to represent the problem.
description_plain: Your child can solve real-life problems by dividing fractions by whole numbers and whole numbers by fractions, using pictures or equations to show their work.

subject: Math
description: Interpret division of a whole number by a unit fraction, and compute such quotients.
description_plain: Your child can understand and calculate what happens when a whole number is divided by a unit fraction.

subject: Math
description: Interpret division of a unit fraction by a non-zero whole number, and compute such quotients.
description_plain: Your child can understand and calculate what happens when a fraction with 1 in the numerator is divided by a whole number.

subject: Math
description: Apply and extend previous understandings of division to divide unit fractions by whole numbers and whole numbers by unit fractions.
description_plain: Your child can divide fractions by whole numbers and whole numbers by fractions, and solve real-life problems using these skills.

subject: Math
description: Solve real-world problems involving multiplication of fractions and mixed numbers, e.g., by using visual fraction models or equations to represent the problem.
description_plain: Your child can solve real-life problems by multiplying fractions and mixed numbers, using pictures or equations to explain their thinking.

subject: Math
description: Relating the principle of fraction equivalence a/b = (n × a)/(n × b) to the effect of multiplying a/b by 1.
description_plain: Your child understands that multiplying the top and bottom of a fraction by the same number doesn’t change its value, like multiplying by 1.

subject: Math
description: Explaining why multiplying a given number by a fraction less than 1 results in a product smaller than the given number.
description_plain: Your child can explain why multiplying a number by a fraction less than one makes the result smaller than the original number.

subject: Math
description: Explaining why multiplying a given number by a fraction greater than 1 results in a product greater than the given number (recognizing multiplication by whole numbers greater than 1 as a familiar case).
description_plain: Your child can explain why multiplying by a fraction greater than 1 makes a number bigger and why multiplying by a fraction less than 1 makes it smaller, and understand how multiplying by 1 keeps a fraction the same.

subject: Math
description: Comparing the size of a product to the size of one factor on the basis of the size of the other factor, without performing the indicated multiplication.
description_plain: Your child can compare the size of a multiplication result to one of the numbers being multiplied by looking at the other number, without actually multiplying.

subject: Math
description: Interpret multiplication as scaling (resizing), by:
description_plain: Your child understands multiplication as resizing or scaling a number.

subject: Math
description: Multiply fractional side lengths to find areas of rectangles, and represent fraction products as rectangular areas.
description_plain: Your child can find the area of rectangles by multiplying fractional side lengths and show this as a picture of the rectangle.

subject: Math
description: Find the area of a rectangle with fractional side lengths by tiling it with unit squares of the appropriate unit fraction side lengths, and show that the area is the same as would be found by multiplying the side lengths.
description_plain: Your child can find the area of a rectangle with fractional sides by covering it with small squares and show that multiplying the side lengths gives the same area.

subject: Math
description: Construct a model to develop understanding of the concept of multiplying two fractions and create a story context for the equation.
description_plain: Your child can build models to understand multiplying two fractions and create a story that matches the multiplication.

subject: Math
description: Interpret the product (m/n) × q as m parts of a partition of q into n equal parts; equivalently, as the result of a sequence of operations, m × q ÷ n.
description_plain: Your child understands multiplying a fraction by a whole number as taking parts of that whole number or doing multiplication and division steps.

subject: Math
description: Apply and extend previous understandings of multiplication to multiply a fraction or whole number by a fraction.
description_plain: Your child can multiply fractions and whole numbers by fractions, building on what they already know about multiplication.

subject: Math
description: Interpret a fraction as division of the numerator by the denominator (a/b = a ÷ b). Solve word problems involving division of whole numbers leading to answers in the form of fractions or mixed numbers, e.g., by using visual fraction models or equations to represent the problem.
description_plain: Your child understands that a fraction means dividing the top number by the bottom number and can solve word problems that result in fractions or mixed numbers using pictures or equations.

subject: Math
description: Apply and extend previous understandings of multiplication and division to multiply and divide fractions.
description_plain: Your child can multiply and divide fractions by building on what they already know about multiplication and division.

subject: Math
description: Use benchmark fractions and number sense of fractions to estimate mentally and justify the reasonableness of answers.
description_plain: Your child can estimate answers with fractions using familiar fractions and explain why the estimate makes sense.

subject: Math
description: Solve word problems involving addition and subtraction of fractions referring to the same whole, including cases of unlike denominators, e.g., by using visual fraction models or equations to represent the problem.
description_plain: Your child can solve word problems adding and subtracting fractions with different denominators using pictures or equations.

subject: Math
description: Solve word problems involving addition and subtraction of fractions.
description_plain: Your child can solve word problems that involve adding and subtracting fractions.

subject: Math
description: Add and subtract fractions with unlike denominators (including mixed numbers) by replacing given fractions with equivalent fractions in such a way as to produce an equivalent sum or difference of fractions with like denominators.
description_plain: Your child can add and subtract fractions with different bottom numbers (including mixed numbers) by changing them to equivalent fractions with the same bottom number.

subject: Math
description: Use equivalent fractions as a strategy to add and subtract fractions.
description_plain: Your child can use equivalent fractions to help add and subtract fractions.

subject: Math
description: Add, subtract, multiply, and divide decimals to hundredths, using concrete models or drawings and strategies based on place value, properties of operations, and/or the relationship between addition and subtraction; justify the reasoning used with a written explanation.
description_plain: Your child can add, subtract, multiply, and divide decimals to two decimal places using drawings or place value strategies, and can check if their answers make sense by estimating.

subject: Math
description: Find whole-number quotients of whole numbers with up to four-digit dividends and two-digit divisors, using strategies based on place value, the properties of operations, subtracting multiples of the divisor, and/or the relationship between multiplication and division. Illustrate and/or explain the calculation by using equations, rectangular arrays, area models, or other strategies based on place value.
description_plain: Your child can divide large whole numbers (up to four digits) by two-digit numbers using place value and other strategies, and can explain their work with equations or pictures.

subject: Math
description: Fluently multiply multi-digit whole numbers using the standard algorithm.
description_plain: Your child can multiply multi-digit whole numbers quickly and accurately using the standard method.

subject: Math
description: Perform operations with multi-digit whole numbers and with decimals to hundredths.
description_plain: Your child can work with large whole numbers and decimals to the hundredths place using addition, subtraction, multiplication, and division.

subject: Math
description: Use place value understanding to round decimals to any place.
description_plain: Your child can round decimals to any place value.

subject: Math
description: Compare two decimals to thousandths based on meanings of the digits in each place, using >, =, and < symbols to record the results of comparisons.
description_plain: Your child can compare two decimals to the thousandths place using >, =, and < symbols.

subject: Math
description: Read and write decimals to thousandths using base-ten numerals, number names, and expanded form, e.g., 347.392 = 3 × 100 + 4 × 10 + 7 × 1 + 3 × (1/10) + 9 × (1/100) + 2 × (1/1000).
description_plain: Your child can read and write decimals to the thousandths place using numbers, words, and expanded form to show the value of each digit.

subject: Math
description: Read, write, and compare decimals to thousandths.
description_plain: Your child can read, write, and compare decimals up to the thousandths place.

subject: Math
description: Explain and apply patterns in the number of zeros of the product when multiplying a number by powers of 10. Explain and apply patterns in the values of the digits in the product or the quotient, when a decimal is multiplied or divided by a power of 10. Use whole-number exponents to denote powers of 10.
description_plain: Your child can explain and use patterns when multiplying or dividing numbers by 10, 100, 1000, and understand how decimals move.

subject: Math
description: Recognize that in a multi-digit number, a digit in one place represents 10 times as much as it represents in the place to its right and 1/10 of what it represents in the place to its left.
description_plain: Your child understands that each place in a number is ten times bigger than the place to its right and one-tenth the place to its left.

subject: Math
description: Understand the place value system.
description_plain: Your child understands how place value works in numbers.

subject: Math
description: Generate two numerical patterns using two given rules. Identify apparent relationships between corresponding terms. Form ordered pairs consisting of corresponding terms from the two patterns, and graph the ordered pairs on a coordinate plane.
description_plain: Your child can create two number patterns from two rules, find relationships between the numbers in the patterns, make pairs from matching numbers, and graph these pairs on a coordinate plane.

subject: Math
description: Analyze patterns and relationships.
description_plain: Your child can find and understand patterns and relationships in numbers.

subject: Math
description: Write simple expressions that record calculations with whole numbers, fractions, and decimals, and interpret numerical expressions without evaluating them.
description_plain: Your child can write simple math expressions with whole numbers, fractions, and decimals, and understand what they mean without solving them.

subject: Math
description: Use parentheses or brackets in numerical expressions, and evaluate expressions with these symbols.
description_plain: Your child can use parentheses or brackets in math expressions and solve problems that include them.

subject: Math
description: Write and interpret numerical expressions.
description_plain: Your child can write and understand math expressions.

subject: Math
description: Look for and express regularity in repeated reasoning.
description_plain: Your child can notice and explain patterns or repeated steps when solving problems.

subject: Math
description: Look for and make use of structure.
description_plain: Your child can notice and use the way math problems are put together to help solve them.

subject: Math
description: Attend to precision.
description_plain: Attend to precision.

subject: Math
description: Use appropriate tools strategically.
description_plain: Your child can choose and use the right tools, like calculators, rulers, or graphs, to help solve math problems.

subject: Math
description: Model with mathematics.
description_plain: Model with mathematics.

subject: Math
description: Construct viable arguments and critique the reasoning of others.
description_plain: Your child can explain their math thinking and listen to and question the ideas of others.

subject: Math
description: Reason abstractly and quantitatively.
description_plain: Your child can think about math problems in different ways, using numbers and real-life examples.

subject: Math
description: Make sense of problems and persevere in solving them.
description_plain: Your child can understand what a math problem is asking and keep working until they find a solution.

subject: Math
description: Partition circles and rectangles into two, three, or four equal shares, describe the shares using the words halves, thirds, half of, a third of, etc., and describe the whole as two halves, three thirds, four fourths. Recognize that equal shares of identical wholes need not have the same shape.
description_plain: Your child can divide circles and rectangles into equal parts like halves, thirds, or fourths and describe these parts and the whole.

subject: Math
description: Partition a rectangle into rows and columns of same-size squares and count to find the total number of them.
description_plain: Your child can divide a rectangle into equal squares by making rows and columns and count how many squares there are.

subject: Math
description: Recognize and draw shapes having specified attributes, such as a given number of angles or a given number of equal faces. Identify triangles, quadrilaterals, pentagons, hexagons, and cubes.
description_plain: Your child can recognize and draw shapes by their features, like number of sides or equal faces, and name shapes like triangles and cubes.

subject: Math
description: Reason with shapes and their attributes.
description_plain: Your child understands shapes and their features and can think about how they are alike and different.

subject: Math
description: Draw a picture graph and a bar graph (with single-unit scale) to represent a data set with up to four categories. Solve simple put-together, take-apart, and compare problems using information presented in a bar graph.
description_plain: Your child can make picture and bar graphs with up to four categories and use them to solve simple problems.

subject: Math
description: Generate measurement data by measuring lengths of several objects to the nearest whole unit, or by making repeated measurements of the same object. Show the measurements by making a line plot, where the horizontal scale is marked off in whole-number units.
description_plain: Your child can measure several objects, record the lengths on a line plot, and organize the data.

subject: Math
description: Represent and interpret data.
description_plain: Represent and interpret data.

subject: Math
description: Solve word problems involving dollar bills, quarters, dimes, nickels, and pennies, using $ and ¢ symbols appropriately.
description_plain: Your child can solve money problems using coins and dollar bills and write amounts with $ and ¢ symbols.

subject: Math
description: Tell and write time from analog and digital clocks to the nearest five minutes, using a.m. and p.m.
description_plain: Your child can tell and write time to the nearest five minutes on analog and digital clocks, using a.m. and p.m.

subject: Math
description: Work with time and money.
description_plain: Work with time and money.

subject: Math
description: Represent whole numbers as lengths from 0 on a number line diagram with equally spaced points corresponding to the numbers 0, 1, 2, …, and represent whole-number sums and differences within 100 on a number line diagram.
description_plain: Your child can show numbers on a number line and use it to add and subtract numbers up to 100.

subject: Math
description: Use addition and subtraction within 100 to solve word problems involving lengths that are given in the same units, e.g., by using drawings (such as drawings of rulers) and equations with a symbol for the unknown number to represent the problem.
description_plain: Your child can solve word problems about lengths using addition and subtraction within 100, with drawings or equations.

subject: Math
description: Relate addition and subtraction to length.
description_plain: Your child understands how addition and subtraction relate to measuring length.

subject: Math
description: Measure to determine how much longer one object is than another, expressing the length difference in terms of a standard length unit.
description_plain: Your child can measure how much longer one object is than another using standard units.

subject: Math
description: Estimate lengths using units of inches, feet, centimeters, and meters.
description_plain: Your child can estimate lengths using inches, feet, centimeters, and meters.

subject: Math
description: Measure the length of an object twice, using length units of different lengths for the two measurements; describe how the two measurements relate to the size of the unit chosen.
description_plain: Your child can measure something twice using different units and explain how the measurements change with the unit size.

subject: Math
description: Measure the length of an object by selecting and using appropriate tools such as rulers, yardsticks, meter sticks, and measuring tapes.
description_plain: Your child can measure how long things are using tools like rulers or measuring tapes.

subject: Math
description: Measure and estimate lengths in standard units.
description_plain: Your child can measure and estimate lengths using standard units.

subject: Math
description: Explain why addition and subtraction strategies work, using place value and the properties of operations.
description_plain: Your child can explain why their ways of adding and subtracting work by using place value and math rules.

subject: Math
description: Mentally add 10 or 100 to a given number 100–900, and mentally subtract 10 or 100 from a given number 100–900.
description_plain: Your child can add or subtract 10 or 100 in their head to numbers between 100 and 900.

subject: Math
description: Add and subtract within 1000 using concrete models or drawings and strategies based on place value, properties of operations, and/or the relationship between addition and subtraction; justify the reasoning used with a written explanation. Understand that in adding or subtracting three-digit numbers, one adds or subtracts hundreds and hundreds, tens and tens, ones and ones; and sometimes it is necessary to compose or decompose tens or hundreds.
description_plain: Your child can add and subtract numbers up to 1000 using drawings or place value ideas and explain how they solved the problem.

subject: Math
description: Add up to four two-digit numbers using strategies based on place value and properties of operations.
description_plain: Your child can add up to four two-digit numbers using place value and math properties.

subject: Math
description: Fluently add and subtract within 100 using strategies based on place value, properties of operations, and/or the relationship between addition and subtraction.
description_plain: Your child can quickly add and subtract numbers within 100 using place value and math rules.

subject: Math
description: Use place value understanding and properties of operations to add and subtract.
description_plain: Your child can use their understanding of place value to add and subtract numbers.

subject: Math
description: Compare two three-digit numbers based on meanings of the hundreds, tens, and ones digits, using >, =, and < symbols to record the results of comparisons.
description_plain: Your child can compare two three-digit numbers by looking at the hundreds, tens, and ones, and use the symbols >, =, and < to show which is bigger or smaller.

subject: Math
description: Read and write numbers to 1000 using base-ten numerals, number names, and expanded form.
description_plain: Your child can read and write numbers up to 1000 in different ways, like with digits, words, or expanded form.

subject: Math
description: Count within 1000; skip-count by 5s, 10s, and 100s.
description_plain: Your child can count to 1000 and skip count by 5s, 10s, and 100s.

subject: Math
description: The numbers 100, 200, 300, 400, 500, 600, 700, 800, 900 refer to one, two, three, four, five, six, seven, eight, or nine hundreds (and 0 tens and 0 ones).
description_plain: Your child understands that numbers like 100, 200, 300, etc., mean one, two, three, and more hundreds with zero tens and ones.

subject: Math
description: 100 can be thought of as a bundle of ten tens—called a "hundred."
description_plain: Your child understands that 100 is made by grouping ten tens together, which is called a 'hundred.'

subject: Math
description: Understand that the three digits of a three-digit number represent amounts of hundreds, tens, and ones; e.g., 706 equals 7 hundreds, 0 tens, and 6 ones. Understand the following as special cases:
description_plain: Your child understands that a three-digit number shows how many hundreds, tens, and ones it has, like 706 means 7 hundreds, 0 tens, and 6 ones.

subject: Math
description: Understand place value.
description_plain: Understand place value.

subject: Math
description: Use addition to find the total number of objects arranged in rectangular arrays with up to 5 rows and up to 5 columns; write an equation to express the total as a sum of equal addends.
description_plain: Your child can use addition to find out how many objects are in a rectangle with up to 5 rows and 5 columns, and can write an equation to show this.

subject: Math
description: Determine whether a group of objects (up to 20) has an odd or even number of members, e.g., by pairing objects or counting them by 2s; write an equation to express an even number as a sum of two equal addends.
description_plain: Your child can tell if a group of up to 20 objects has an odd or even number by pairing or counting by twos and write an equation showing even numbers as two equal parts.

subject: Math
description: Work with equal groups of objects to gain foundations for multiplication.
description_plain: Your child works with equal groups of objects to start learning about multiplication.

subject: Math
description: Fluently add and subtract within 20 using mental strategies. By the end of Grade 2, know from memory all sums of two one-digit numbers.
description_plain: Your child can quickly add and subtract numbers within 20 in their head and knows all sums of two one-digit numbers by memory.

subject: Math
description: Add and subtract within 20.
description_plain: Add and subtract within 20.

subject: Math
description: Use addition and subtraction within 100 to solve one- and two-step word problems involving situations of adding to, taking from, putting together, taking apart, and comparing, with unknowns in all positions, e.g., by using drawings and equations with a symbol for the unknown number to represent the problem.
description_plain: Your child can solve one- and two-step word problems using addition and subtraction within 100, drawing pictures and writing equations with unknown numbers.

subject: Math
description: Represent and solve problems involving addition and subtraction.
description_plain: Your child can use addition and subtraction to solve different kinds of problems.

subject: Math
description: Look for and express regularity in repeated reasoning.
description_plain: Your child can notice and explain patterns or repeated steps when solving problems.

subject: Math
description: Look for and make use of structure.
description_plain: Your child can notice and use the way math problems are put together to help solve them.

subject: Math
description: Attend to precision.
description_plain: Attend to precision.

subject: Math
description: Use appropriate tools strategically.
description_plain: Your child can choose and use the right tools, like calculators, rulers, or graphs, to help solve math problems.

subject: Math
description: Model with mathematics.
description_plain: Model with mathematics.

subject: Math
description: Construct viable arguments and critique the reasoning of others.
description_plain: Your child can explain their math thinking and listen to and question the ideas of others.

subject: Math
description: Reason abstractly and quantitatively.
description_plain: Your child can think about math problems in different ways, using numbers and real-life examples.

subject: Math
description: Make sense of problems and persevere in solving them.
description_plain: Your child can understand what a math problem is asking and keep working until they find a solution.

subject: Math
description: Design and use a simulation to generate frequencies for compound events.
description_plain: Your child can create and use simulations to find how often combined events happen.

subject: Math
description: Represent sample spaces for compound events using methods such as organized lists, tables and tree diagrams. For an event described in everyday language (e.g., "rolling double sixes"), identify the outcomes in the sample space that compose the event.
description_plain: Your child can list all possible outcomes for combined chance events using lists, tables, or tree diagrams and identify which outcomes match a described event.

subject: Math
description: Understand that, just as with simple events, the probability of a compound event is the fraction of outcomes in the sample space for which the compound event occurs.
description_plain: Your child understands that the chance of combined events happening is the part of all possible outcomes where those events happen.

subject: Math
description: Find probabilities of compound events using organized lists, tables, tree diagrams, and simulation.
description_plain: Your child can find the chances of combined events happening by using lists, tables, tree diagrams, or simulations.

subject: Math
description: Develop a probability model (which may not be uniform) by observing frequencies in data generated from a chance process.
description_plain: Your child creates a probability model based on how often things happen in real-life chance events.

subject: Math
description: Develop a uniform probability model by assigning equal probability to all outcomes, and use the model to determine probabilities of events.
description_plain: Your child makes a simple probability model where all outcomes are equally likely and uses it to find chances of events.

subject: Math
description: Develop a probability model and use it to find probabilities of events. Compare probabilities from a model to observed frequencies; if the agreement is not good, explain possible sources of the discrepancy.
description_plain: Your child builds probability models to find chances of events and compares these to actual results, explaining differences.

subject: Math
description: Approximate the probability of a chance event by collecting data on the chance process that produces it and observing its long-run relative frequency, and predict the approximate relative frequency given the probability.
description_plain: Your child estimates the chance of an event by collecting data and watching how often it happens over time.

subject: Math
description: Understand that the probability of a chance event is a number between 0 and 1 that expresses the likelihood of the event occurring. Larger numbers indicate greater likelihood. A probability near 0 indicates an unlikely event, a probability around ½ indicates an event that is neither unlikely nor likely, and a probability near 1 indicates a likely event.
description_plain: Your child understands that probability is a number from 0 to 1 showing how likely something is to happen, with bigger numbers meaning more likely.

subject: Math
description: Investigate chance processes and develop, use, and evaluate probability models.
description_plain: Your child explores chance events and learns how to create, use, and check probability models.

subject: Math
description: Use measures of center and measures of variability for numerical data from random samples to draw informal comparative inferences about two populations.
description_plain: Your child uses averages and variation from random samples to compare two groups and make guesses about them.

subject: Math
description: Informally assess the degree of visual overlap of two numerical data distributions with similar variabilities using quantitative measures of center (median and/or mean) and variability (interquartile range and/or mean absolute deviation), as well as describing any overall pattern and any striking deviations from the overall pattern with reference to the context in which the data were gathered.
description_plain: Your child looks at two sets of data to see how much they overlap and compares their centers using measures of spread.

subject: Math
description: Draw informal comparative inferences about two populations.
description_plain: Your child makes informal comparisons between two groups using data.

subject: Math
description: Use data from a random sample to draw inferences about a population with an unknown characteristic of interest. Generate multiple samples (or simulated samples) of the same size to gauge the variation in estimates or predictions.
description_plain: Your child uses data from random samples to make guesses about a larger group and tries multiple samples to see how results can change.

subject: Math
description: Understand that statistics can be used to gain information about a population by examining a sample of the population; generalizations about a population from a sample are valid only if the sample is representative of that population. Understand that random sampling tends to produce representative samples and support valid inferences.
description_plain: Your child understands that looking at a small, random part of a group can give good information about the whole group if the sample is fair.

subject: Math
description: Use random sampling to draw inferences about a population.
description_plain: Your child uses random samples to learn about larger groups.

subject: Math
description: Solve word problems leading to inequalities of the form px + q > r, px + q ≥ r, px + q < r or px + q ≤ r, where p, q, and r are specific rational numbers. Graph the solution set of the inequality and interpret it in the context of the problem.
description_plain: Your child can solve word problems that lead to inequalities, graph the solutions, and explain what the answers mean in the problem.

subject: Math
description: Solve word problems leading to equations of the form px + q = r and p(x + q) = r, where p, q, and r are specific rational numbers. Solve equations of these forms fluently. Compare an algebraic solution to an arithmetic solution, identifying the sequence of the operations used in each approach.
description_plain: Your child solves word problems that lead to equations, solves them easily, and compares algebraic and arithmetic methods.

subject: Math
description: Use variables to represent quantities in a real-world or mathematical problem, and construct simple equations and inequalities to solve problems by reasoning about the quantities.
description_plain: Your child can use letters to stand for numbers in real problems and write simple equations or inequalities to solve them.

subject: Math
description: Solve multi-step real-life and mathematical problems posed with positive and negative rational numbers in any form (whole numbers, fractions, and decimals), using tools strategically. Apply properties of operations to calculate with numbers in any form; convert between forms as appropriate; and assess the reasonableness of answers using mental computation and estimation strategies.
description_plain: Your child can solve multi-step problems with positive and negative numbers in different forms, use math tools wisely, and check if answers make sense.

subject: Math
description: Solve real-life and mathematical problems using numerical and algebraic expressions and equations.
description_plain: Your child can solve real-life and math problems using number and algebra expressions and equations.

subject: Math
description: Understand that rewriting an expression in different forms in a problem context can shed light on the problem and how the quantities in it are related.
description_plain: Your child understands that changing how an expression looks can help explain a problem and how the numbers relate.

subject: Math
description: Apply properties of operations as strategies to add, subtract, factor, and expand linear expressions with rational coefficients to include multiple grouping symbols (e.g., parentheses, brackets, and braces).
description_plain: Your child uses math rules to add, subtract, factor, and expand expressions with rational numbers.

subject: Math
description: Use properties of operations to generate equivalent expressions.
description_plain: Your child uses math properties to create expressions that mean the same thing.

subject: Math
description: Solve real-world and mathematical problems involving the four operations with rational numbers.
description_plain: Your child can solve real-life and math problems using addition, subtraction, multiplication, and division with rational numbers.

subject: Math
description: Convert a rational number to a decimal using long division; know that the decimal form of a rational number terminates in 0s or eventually repeats.
description_plain: Your child can change a fraction or rational number into a decimal by dividing and knows decimals either end or repeat.

subject: Math
description: Apply properties of operations as strategies to multiply and divide rational numbers.
description_plain: Your child uses math rules to multiply and divide rational numbers.

subject: Math
description: Understand that integers can be divided, provided that the divisor is not zero, and every quotient of integers (with non-zero divisor) is a rational number. If p and q are integers, then –(p/q) = (–p)/q = p/(–q). Interpret quotients of rational numbers by describing real-world contexts.
description_plain: Your child understands dividing integers works when the divisor isn't zero and explains division of rational numbers using real-life examples.

subject: Math
description: Understand that multiplication is extended from fractions to rational numbers by requiring that operations continue to satisfy the properties of operations, particularly the distributive property, leading to products such as (–1)(–1) = 1 and the rules for multiplying signed numbers. Interpret products of rational numbers by describing real-world contexts.
description_plain: Your child understands multiplying rational numbers follows math rules, including multiplying negatives, and explains these products with real-world examples.

subject: Math
description: Apply and extend previous understandings of multiplication and division and of fractions to multiply and divide rational numbers.
description_plain: Your child can multiply and divide positive and negative numbers, including fractions, and use these skills to solve real-world problems.

subject: Math
description: Apply properties of operations as strategies to add and subtract rational numbers.
description_plain: Your child can use rules about adding and subtracting numbers to help solve problems with fractions and decimals.

subject: Math
description: Understand subtraction of rational numbers as adding the additive inverse, p – q = p + (–q). Show that the distance between two rational numbers on the number line is the absolute value of their difference, and apply this principle in real-world contexts.
description_plain: Your child understands that subtracting a number is the same as adding its opposite and can find the distance between two numbers on a number line.

subject: Math
description: Understand p + q as the number located a distance |q| from p, in the positive or negative direction depending on whether q is positive or negative. Show that a number and its opposite have a sum of 0 (are additive inverses). Interpret sums of rational numbers by describing real-world contexts.
description_plain: Your child understands adding positive or negative numbers as moving along a number line and knows that a number plus its opposite equals zero.

subject: Math
description: Describe situations in which opposite quantities combine to make 0.
description_plain: Your child can explain situations where two opposite amounts combine to make zero.

subject: Math
description: Apply and extend previous understandings of addition and subtraction to add and subtract rational numbers; represent addition and subtraction on a horizontal or vertical number line diagram.
description_plain: Your child can add and subtract rational numbers and show these on a number line going left-right or up-down.

subject: Math
description: Apply and extend previous understandings of operations with fractions to add, subtract, multiply, and divide rational numbers.
description_plain: Your child can add, subtract, multiply, and divide rational numbers using what they know about fractions.

subject: Math
description: Use proportional relationships to solve multi-step ratio and percent problems of simple interest, tax, markups and markdowns, gratuities and commissions, fees, percent increase and decrease, and percent error.
description_plain: Your child can use ratios and percentages to solve multi-step problems about interest, tax, discounts, tips, fees, and percent changes.

subject: Math
description: Explain what a point (x, y) on the graph of a proportional relationship means in terms of the situation, with special attention to the points (0, 0) and (1, r) where r is the unit rate.
description_plain: Your child can explain what points on a graph mean in a proportional relationship, especially the starting point and the unit rate.

subject: Math
description: Represent proportional relationships by equations.
description_plain: Your child can write equations that show proportional relationships between quantities.

subject: Math
description: Identify the constant of proportionality (unit rate) in tables, graphs, equations, diagrams, and verbal descriptions of proportional relationships.
description_plain: Your child can find the unit rate (constant ratio) in tables, graphs, equations, pictures, and words.

subject: Math
description: Decide whether two quantities are in a proportional relationship, e.g., by testing for equivalent ratios in a table or graphing on a coordinate plane and observing whether the graph is a straight line through the origin.
description_plain: Your child can decide if two quantities are proportional by checking if their ratios are equal or if their graph is a straight line through the origin.

subject: Math
description: Recognize and represent proportional relationships between quantities.
description_plain: Your child can recognize and show proportional relationships between amounts.

subject: Math
description: Compute unit rates associated with ratios of fractions, including ratios of lengths, areas, and other quantities measured in like or different units.
description_plain: Your child can find unit rates from ratios, including those with fractions and different units like length or area.

subject: Math
description: Analyze proportional relationships and use them to solve real-world and mathematical problems.
description_plain: Your child can understand and use ratios to solve problems in everyday life and math.

subject: Math
description: Solve real-world and mathematical problems involving area, volume and surface area of two- and three-dimensional objects composed of triangles, quadrilaterals, polygons, cubes, and right prisms. (Pyramids limited to surface area only.)
description_plain: Your child can solve problems about area, volume, and surface area of shapes like triangles, rectangles, and cubes.

subject: Math
description: Use facts about supplementary, complementary, vertical, and adjacent angles in a multi-step problem to write and use them to solve simple equations for an unknown angle in a figure.
description_plain: Your child can use angle facts to write and solve simple equations to find missing angles.

subject: Math
description: Know the formulas for the area and circumference of a circle and solve problems; give an informal derivation of the relationship between the circumference and area of a circle.
description_plain: Your child knows the formulas for the area and circumference of a circle and can solve problems using them, including understanding how circumference and area relate.

subject: Math
description: Solve real-life and mathematical problems involving angle measure, area, surface area, and volume.
description_plain: Your child can solve real and math problems about angles, area, surface area, and volume.

subject: Math
description: Describe the two-dimensional figures that result from slicing three-dimensional figures, as in plane sections of right rectangular prisms and right rectangular pyramids.
description_plain: Your child can describe the flat shapes made when slicing 3D shapes like boxes and pyramids.

subject: Math
description: Draw (freehand, with ruler and protractor, or with technology) geometric shapes with given conditions. (Focus is on triangles from three measures of angles or sides, noticing when the conditions determine one and only one triangle, more than one triangle, or no triangle.)
description_plain: Your child can draw triangles using given angle or side measurements and understand when these measurements make one, more than one, or no triangle.

subject: Math
description: Solve problems involving scale drawings of geometric figures, such as computing actual lengths and areas from a scale drawing and reproducing a scale drawing at a different scale.
description_plain: Your child solves problems using scale drawings by finding actual lengths and areas or making drawings at different scales.

subject: Math
description: Draw, construct, and describe geometrical figures and describe the relationships between them.
description_plain: Your child can draw and build shapes and explain how they relate to each other.

subject: Math
description: Look for and express regularity in repeated reasoning.
description_plain: Your child can notice and explain patterns or repeated steps when solving problems.

subject: Math
description: Look for and make use of structure.
description_plain: Your child can notice and use the way math problems are put together to help solve them.

subject: Math
description: Attend to precision.
description_plain: Attend to precision.

subject: Math
description: Use appropriate tools strategically.
description_plain: Your child can choose and use the right tools, like calculators, rulers, or graphs, to help solve math problems.

subject: Math
description: Model with mathematics.
description_plain: Model with mathematics.

subject: Math
description: Construct viable arguments and critique the reasoning of others.
description_plain: Your child can explain their math thinking and listen to and question the ideas of others.

subject: Math
description: Reason abstractly and quantitatively.
description_plain: Your child can think about math problems in different ways, using numbers and real-life examples.

subject: Math
description: Make sense of problems and persevere in solving them.
description_plain: Your child can understand what a math problem is asking and keep working until they find a solution.

subject: Math
description: Evaluate reports based on data.
description_plain: Your child can look at reports based on data and decide if the information makes sense.

subject: Math
description: Evaluate reports based on data.
description_plain: Your child can look at reports based on data and decide if the information makes sense.

subject: Math
description: Evaluate reports based on data.
description_plain: Your child can look at reports based on data and decide if the information makes sense.

subject: Math
description: Evaluate reports based on data.
description_plain: Your child can look at reports based on data and decide if the information makes sense.

subject: Math
description: Use data from a randomized experiment to compare two treatments; use simulations to decide if differences between parameters are significant.
description_plain: Your child can use data from experiments to compare two treatments and use simulations to check if differences are meaningful.

subject: Math
description: Use data from a randomized experiment to compare two treatments; use simulations to decide if differences between parameters are significant.
description_plain: Your child can use data from experiments to compare two treatments and use simulations to check if differences are meaningful.

subject: Math
description: Use data from a randomized experiment to compare two treatments; use simulations to decide if differences between parameters are significant.
description_plain: Your child can use data from experiments to compare two treatments and use simulations to check if differences are meaningful.

subject: Math
description: Use data from a randomized experiment to compare two treatments; use simulations to decide if differences between parameters are significant.
description_plain: Your child can use data from experiments to compare two treatments and use simulations to check if differences are meaningful.

subject: Math
description: Use data from a sample survey to estimate a population mean or proportion; develop a margin of error through the use of simulation models for random sampling.
description_plain: Your child can use survey data to estimate averages or proportions for a whole group and understand how much error might be in the estimate.

subject: Math
description: Use data from a sample survey to estimate a population mean or proportion; develop a margin of error through the use of simulation models for random sampling.
description_plain: Your child can use survey data to estimate averages or proportions for a whole group and understand how much error might be in the estimate.

subject: Math
description: Use data from a sample survey to estimate a population mean or proportion; develop a margin of error through the use of simulation models for random sampling.
description_plain: Your child can use survey data to estimate averages or proportions for a whole group and understand how much error might be in the estimate.

subject: Math
description: Use data from a sample survey to estimate a population mean or proportion; develop a margin of error through the use of simulation models for random sampling.
description_plain: Your child can use survey data to estimate averages or proportions for a whole group and understand how much error might be in the estimate.

subject: Math
description: Recognize the purposes of and differences among sample surveys, experiments, and observational studies; explain how randomization relates to each.
description_plain: Your child understands the differences between surveys, experiments, and observational studies and how random choices are used in each.

subject: Math
description: Recognize the purposes of and differences among sample surveys, experiments, and observational studies; explain how randomization relates to each.
description_plain: Your child understands the differences between surveys, experiments, and observational studies and how random choices are used in each.

subject: Math
description: Recognize the purposes of and differences among sample surveys, experiments, and observational studies; explain how randomization relates to each.
description_plain: Your child understands the differences between surveys, experiments, and observational studies and how random choices are used in each.

subject: Math
description: Recognize the purposes of and differences among sample surveys, experiments, and observational studies; explain how randomization relates to each.
description_plain: Your child understands the differences between surveys, experiments, and observational studies and how random choices are used in each.

subject: Math
description: Make inferences and justify conclusions from sample surveys, experiments, and observational studies.
description_plain: Your child can make guesses and explain conclusions from surveys, experiments, and observations.

subject: Math
description: Make inferences and justify conclusions from sample surveys, experiments, and observational studies.
description_plain: Your child can make guesses and explain conclusions from surveys, experiments, and observations.

subject: Math
description: Make inferences and justify conclusions from sample surveys, experiments, and observational studies.
description_plain: Your child can make guesses and explain conclusions from surveys, experiments, and observations.

subject: Math
description: Make inferences and justify conclusions from sample surveys, experiments, and observational studies.
description_plain: Your child can make guesses and explain conclusions from surveys, experiments, and observations.

subject: Math
description: Decide if a specified model is consistent with results from a given data-generating process, e.g., using simulation.
description_plain: Your child can decide if a model fits the results from data, sometimes by using simulations.

subject: Math
description: Decide if a specified model is consistent with results from a given data-generating process, e.g., using simulation.
description_plain: Your child can decide if a model fits the results from data, sometimes by using simulations.

subject: Math
description: Decide if a specified model is consistent with results from a given data-generating process, e.g., using simulation.
description_plain: Your child can decide if a model fits the results from data, sometimes by using simulations.

subject: Math
description: Decide if a specified model is consistent with results from a given data-generating process, e.g., using simulation.
description_plain: Your child can decide if a model fits the results from data, sometimes by using simulations.

subject: Math
description: Understand statistics as a process for making inferences to be made about population parameters based on a random sample from that population.
description_plain: Your child understands that statistics help us learn about a whole group by studying a random sample from it.

subject: Math
description: Understand statistics as a process for making inferences to be made about population parameters based on a random sample from that population.
description_plain: Your child understands that statistics help us learn about a whole group by studying a random sample from it.

subject: Math
description: Understand statistics as a process for making inferences to be made about population parameters based on a random sample from that population.
description_plain: Your child understands that statistics help us learn about a whole group by studying a random sample from it.

subject: Math
description: Understand statistics as a process for making inferences to be made about population parameters based on a random sample from that population.
description_plain: Your child understands that statistics help us learn about a whole group by studying a random sample from it.

subject: Math
description: Understand and evaluate random processes underlying statistical experiments.
description_plain: Your child understands and checks the random processes behind statistical experiments.

subject: Math
description: Understand and evaluate random processes underlying statistical experiments.

description: Understand that a function from one set (called the domain) to another set (called the range) assigns to each element of the domain exactly one element of the range. subject: Math
description: Partition circles and rectangles into two and four equal shares, describe the shares using the words halves, fourths, and quarters, and use the phrases half of, fourth of, and quarter of. Describe the whole as two of, or four of the shares. Understand for these examples that decomposing into more equal shares creates smaller shares.
description_plain: Your child can divide circles and rectangles into two or four equal parts and use words like halves and quarters to describe them.

subject: Math
description: Compose two-dimensional shapes (rectangles, squares, trapezoids, triangles, half-circles, and quarter-circles) and three-dimensional shapes (cubes, right rectangular prisms, right circular cones, and right circular cylinders) to create a composite shape, and compose new shapes from the composite shape.
description_plain: Your child can put together flat shapes or solid shapes to make new shapes and can also break apart shapes to make others.

subject: Math
description: Distinguish between defining attributes (e.g., triangles are closed and three-sided) versus non-defining attributes (e.g., color, orientation, overall size); build and draw shapes that possess defining attributes.
description_plain: Your child can tell the important features that define shapes, like a triangle having three sides, and can build and draw shapes with those features.

subject: Math
description: Reason with shapes and their attributes.
description_plain: Your child can think about shapes and their features.

subject: Math
description: Determine the value of a collection of coins up to 50 cents. (Pennies, nickels, dimes, and quarters in isolation; not to include a combination of different coins.)
description_plain: Your child can figure out the value of a group of the same kind of coins up to 50 cents.

subject: Math
description: Work with money.
description_plain: Work with money.

subject: Math
description: Organize, represent, and interpret data with up to three categories; ask and answer questions about the total number of data points, how many in each category, and how many more or less are in one category than in another.
description_plain: Your child can collect and organize data into up to three groups and ask and answer questions about how many are in each group and how the groups compare.

subject: Math
description: Represent and interpret data.
description_plain: Represent and interpret data.

subject: Math
description: Tell and write time in hours and half-hours using analog and digital clocks.
description_plain: Your child can tell and write time to the hour and half-hour using both analog and digital clocks.

subject: Math
description: Tell and write time.
description_plain: Tell and write time.

subject: Math
description: Express the length of an object as a whole number of length units, by laying multiple copies of a shorter object (the length unit) end to end; understand that the length measurement of an object is the number of same-size length units that span it with no gaps or overlaps.
description_plain: Your child can measure how long something is by lining up smaller, same-sized objects end to end without gaps or overlaps.
