subject: Math
description: Order three objects by length; compare the lengths of two objects indirectly by using a third object.
description_plain: Your child can put three objects in order from shortest to longest and compare two objects by using a third object.

subject: Math
description: Measure lengths indirectly and by iterating length units.
description_plain: Your child can measure lengths by using smaller units repeatedly or by measuring indirectly.

subject: Math
description: Subtract multiples of 10 in the range 10–90 from multiples of 10 in the range 10–90 (positive or zero differences), using concrete models or drawings and strategies based on place value, properties of operations, and/or the relationship between addition and subtraction; relate the strategy to a written method and explain the reasoning used.
description_plain: Your child can subtract multiples of 10 from each other using objects or drawings and explain how they solved it.

subject: Math
description: Given a two-digit number, mentally find 10 more or 10 less than the number, without having to count; explain the reasoning used.
description_plain: Your child can quickly find 10 more or 10 less than a two-digit number in their head and explain how they know.

subject: Math
description: Understand that in adding two-digit numbers, one adds tens and tens, ones and ones; and sometimes it is necessary to compose a ten.
description_plain: Your child understands that when adding two-digit numbers, they add the tens and ones separately and sometimes need to carry over ten.

subject: Math
description: Use concrete models or drawings and strategies based on place value, properties of operations, and/or the relationship between addition and subtraction; relate the strategy to a number sentence; justify the reasoning used with a written explanation.
description_plain: Your child can use objects or drawings and place value ideas to add and subtract, explain their method, and connect it to number sentences.

subject: Math
description: Add within 100, including adding a two-digit number and a one-digit number, and adding a two-digit number and a multiple of 10.
description_plain: Your child can add numbers up to 100, including adding two-digit and one-digit numbers or multiples of 10, using objects or drawings and explain their thinking.

subject: Math
description: Use place value understanding and properties of operations to add and subtract.
description_plain: Your child can use their understanding of tens and ones to add and subtract numbers.

subject: Math
description: Compare two two-digit numbers based on meanings of the tens and ones digits, recording the results of comparisons with the symbols >, =, and <.
description_plain: Your child can compare two two-digit numbers by looking at the tens and ones, and use symbols like >, =, and < to show which is bigger or smaller.

subject: Math
description: The numbers 10, 20, 30, 40, 50, 60, 70, 80, 90 refer to one, two, three, four, five, six, seven, eight, or nine tens (and 0 ones).
description_plain: Your child understands that numbers like 10, 20, 30, etc., mean groups of tens and zero ones.

subject: Math
description: The numbers from 11 to 19 are composed of a ten and one, two, three, four, five, six, seven, eight, or nine ones.
description_plain: Your child knows that numbers from 11 to 19 are made of one ten and some ones.

subject: Math
description: 10 can be thought of as a bundle of ten ones—called a "ten."
description_plain: Your child understands that ten ones can be grouped together to make one 'ten.'

subject: Math
description: Understand that the two digits of a two-digit number represent amounts of tens and ones. Understand the following as special cases:
description_plain: Your child understands that a two-digit number shows how many tens and how many ones it has.

subject: Math
description: Understand place value.
description_plain: Understand place value.

subject: Math
description: Count to 120, starting at any number less than 120. In this range, read and write numerals and represent a number of objects with a written numeral.
description_plain: Your child can count, read, and write numbers up to 120, starting from any number less than 120, and match numbers to groups of objects.

subject: Math
description: Extend the counting sequence.
description_plain: Extend the counting sequence.

subject: Math
description: Determine the unknown whole number in an addition or subtraction equation relating three whole numbers.
description_plain: Your child can figure out the missing number in an addition or subtraction problem with three numbers.

subject: Math
description: Understand the meaning of the equal sign, and determine if equations involving addition and subtraction are true or false.
description_plain: Your child understands what the equal sign means and can tell if addition or subtraction equations are true or false.

subject: Math
description: Work with addition and subtraction equations.
description_plain: Your child can work with addition and subtraction equations.

subject: Math
description: Add and subtract within 20, demonstrating fluency for addition and subtraction within 10. Use mental strategies such as counting on; making ten (e.g., 8 + 6 = 8 + 2 + 4 = 10 + 4 = 14); decomposing a number leading to a ten (e.g., 13 - 4 = 13 - 3 - 1 = 10 - 1 = 9); using the relationship between addition and subtraction (e.g., knowing that 8 + 4 = 12, one knows 12 - 8 = 4); and creating equivalent but easier or known sums (e.g., adding 6 + 7 by creating the known equivalent 6 + 6 + 1 = 12 + 1 = 13).
description_plain: Your child can add and subtract numbers up to 20 quickly using mental tricks like making ten or breaking numbers apart.

subject: Math
description: Relate counting to addition and subtraction (e.g., by counting on 2 to add 2).
description_plain: Your child can use counting to help add and subtract numbers, like counting on to add.

subject: Math
description: Add and subtract within 20.
description_plain: Add and subtract within 20.

subject: Math
description: Understand subtraction as an unknown-addend problem.
description_plain: Your child can solve subtraction problems by figuring out what number needs to be added to reach a total.

subject: Math
description: Apply properties of operations to add and subtract.
description_plain: Your child can use properties of addition and subtraction to solve problems.

subject: Math
description: Understand and apply properties of operations and the relationship between addition and subtraction.
description_plain: Your child understands and uses the rules for adding and subtracting, and knows how they are connected.

subject: Math
description: Solve word problems that call for addition of three whole numbers whose sum is less than or equal to 20, e.g., by using objects, drawings, and equations with a symbol for the unknown number to represent the problem.
description_plain: Your child can solve word problems that add three numbers with a total of 20 or less using objects, drawings, or equations with a missing number.

subject: Math
description: Use addition and subtraction within 20 to solve word problems involving situations of adding to, taking from, putting together, taking apart, and comparing, with unknowns in all positions (e.g., by using objects, drawings, and equations with a symbol for the unknown number to represent the problem).
description_plain: Your child can solve word problems with addition and subtraction within 20 by using objects, drawings, or simple equations with a blank for the unknown number.

subject: Math
description: Represent and solve problems involving addition and subtraction.
description_plain: Your child can show and solve problems using addition and subtraction.

subject: Math
description: Look for and express regularity in repeated reasoning.
description_plain: Your child can notice and explain patterns or repeated steps when solving problems.

subject: Math
description: Look for and make use of structure.
description_plain: Your child can notice and use the way math problems are put together to help solve them.

subject: Math
description: Attend to precision.
description_plain: Attend to precision.

subject: Math
description: Use appropriate tools strategically.
description_plain: Your child can choose and use the right tools, like calculators, rulers, or graphs, to help solve math problems.

subject: Math
description: Model with mathematics.
description_plain: Model with mathematics.

subject: Math
description: Construct viable arguments and critique the reasoning of others.
description_plain: Your child can explain their math thinking and listen to and question the ideas of others.

subject: Math
description: Reason abstractly and quantitatively.
description_plain: Your child can think about math problems in different ways, using numbers and real-life examples.

subject: Math
description: Make sense of problems and persevere in solving them.
description_plain: Your child can understand what a math problem is asking and keep working until they find a solution.

subject: Math
description: Recognize a line of symmetry for a two-dimensional figure as a line across the figure such that the figure can be folded along the line into matching parts. Identify line-symmetric figures and draw lines of symmetry.
description_plain: Your child can find and draw lines of symmetry on flat shapes by folding them to see if both sides match.

subject: Math
description: Classify two-dimensional figures based on the presence or absence of parallel or perpendicular lines, or the presence or absence of angles of a specified size. Recognize right triangles as a category, and identify right triangles.
description_plain: Your child can sort flat shapes by looking for parallel or perpendicular lines and by naming triangles as right, acute, or obtuse based on their angles.

subject: Math
description: Draw points, lines, line segments, rays, angles (right, acute, obtuse), and perpendicular and parallel lines. Identify these in two-dimensional figures.
description_plain: Your child can draw points, lines, line segments, rays, and different types of angles, and find these parts in flat shapes.

subject: Math
description: Draw and identify lines and angles, and classify shapes by properties of their lines and angles.
description_plain: Your child can draw and identify lines and angles and group shapes based on their lines and angles.

subject: Math
description: Recognize area as additive. Find areas of rectilinear figures by decomposing them into non-overlapping rectangles and adding the areas of the non-overlapping parts, applying this technique to solve real-world problems.
description_plain: Your child can find the area of shapes made of rectangles by breaking them apart and adding the areas, and use this to solve real problems.

subject: Math
description: Relate area to operations of multiplication and addition.
description_plain: Your child understands that area relates to multiplication and addition.

subject: Math
description: Recognize angle measure as additive. When an angle is decomposed into non-overlapping parts, the angle measure of the whole is the sum of the angle measures of the parts. Solve addition and subtraction problems to find unknown angles on a diagram in real-world and mathematical problems, e.g., by using an equation with a letter for the unknown angle measure.
description_plain: Your child can find the size of a whole angle by adding the sizes of its parts and solve problems to find missing angles using addition or subtraction.

subject: Math
description: Measure angles in whole-number degrees using a protractor. Sketch angles of specified measure.
description_plain: Your child can measure angles using a protractor and draw angles with a given size.

subject: Math
description: An angle that turns through n one-degree angles is said to have an angle measure of n degrees.
description_plain: Your child knows that an angle made by turning through n one-degree steps measures n degrees.

subject: Math
description: An angle that turns through 1/360 of a circle is called a "one-degree angle," and can be used to measure angles.
description_plain: Your child knows that turning through 1/360 of a circle makes a one-degree angle, which can be used to measure angles.

subject: Math
description: An angle is measured with reference to a circle with its center at the common endpoint of the rays, by considering the fraction of the circular arc between the points where two rays intersect the circle.
description_plain: Your child understands that an angle is measured by how much it turns around a point, with one degree being 1/360 of a full turn.

subject: Math
description: Recognize angles as geometric shapes that are formed wherever two rays share a common endpoint, and understand concepts of angle measurement:
description_plain: Your child knows that angles are shapes made by two rays sharing a point and learns how to measure them.

subject: Math
description: Geometric measurement: understand concepts of angle and measure angles.
description_plain: Your child learns about angles and how to measure them.

subject: Math
description: Make a line plot to display a data set of measurements in fractions of a unit (½, ¼, 1/8). Solve problems involving addition and subtraction of fractions by using information presented in line plots.
description_plain: Your child can make a line plot to show measurements using fractions like 1/2 or 1/4 and solve addition or subtraction problems using the plot.

subject: Math
description: Represent and interpret data.
description_plain: Represent and interpret data.

subject: Math
description: Apply the area and perimeter formulas for rectangles in real-world and mathematical problems.
description_plain: Your child can use formulas to find the area and perimeter of rectangles in real-life and math problems.

subject: Math
description: Use the four operations to solve word problems involving distances, intervals of time, liquid volumes, masses of objects, and money, including problems involving whole numbers and/or simple fractions (addition and subtraction of fractions with like denominators and multiplying a fraction times a fraction3 or a whole number), and problems that require expressing measurements given in a larger unit in terms of a smaller unit. Represent measurement quantities using diagrams such as number line diagrams that feature a measurement scale.
description_plain: Your child can solve word problems involving distances, time, volume, weight, and money using addition, subtraction, multiplication, or division, including problems with fractions or decimals, and can show measurements on number lines.

subject: Math
description: Know relative sizes of measurement units within one system of units including ft, in; km, m, cm; kg, g; lb, oz.; l, ml; hr, min, sec. Within a single system of measurement, express measurements in a larger unit in terms of a smaller unit. Record measurement equivalents in a two-column table. (Conversions are limited to one-step conversions.)
description_plain: Your child knows how different units of measurement relate within one system and can change measurements from bigger to smaller units and record these conversions.

subject: Math
description: Solve problems involving measurement and conversion of measurements from a larger unit to a smaller unit.
description_plain: Your child can solve problems that involve measuring things and changing measurements from larger units to smaller units.

subject: Math
description: Compare two decimals to hundredths by reasoning about their size. Recognize that comparisons are valid only when the two decimals refer to the same whole. Record the results of comparisons with the symbols >, =, or <, and justify the conclusions, e.g., by using a visual model.
description_plain: Your child can compare two decimals to hundredths by thinking about their size, use >, =, or < to show which is bigger, and explain why, making sure the decimals refer to the same whole.

subject: Math
description: Use decimal notation for fractions with denominators 10 or 100.
description_plain: Your child can write fractions with denominators 10 or 100 as decimals.

subject: Math
description: Express a fraction with denominator 10 as an equivalent fraction with denominator 100, and use this technique to add two fractions with respective denominators 10 and 100.
description_plain: Your child can change a fraction with denominator 10 into an equal fraction with denominator 100 and use this to add fractions with denominators 10 and 100.

subject: Math
description: Understand decimal notation for fractions, and compare decimal fractions.
description_plain: Your child can understand how fractions and decimals are related and can compare decimal numbers.

subject: Math
description: Solve word problems involving multiplication of a fraction by a whole number, e.g., by using visual fraction models and equations to represent the problem.
description_plain: Your child can solve word problems that multiply a fraction by a whole number using pictures and equations.

subject: Math
description: Understand a multiple of a/b as a multiple of 1/b, and use this understanding to multiply a fraction by a whole number.
description_plain: Your child understands that multiplying a fraction by a whole number means multiplying the numerator and can use models to show this.

subject: Math
description: Understand a fraction a/b as a multiple of 1/b.
description_plain: Your child understands a fraction like a/b as a number made up of a parts of size 1/b.

subject: Math
description: Multiply a fraction by a whole number. (Denominators are limited to 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 8, 10, 12, and 100.)
description_plain: Your child can multiply a fraction by a whole number.

subject: Math
description: Solve word problems involving addition and subtraction of fractions referring to the same whole and having like denominators, e.g., by using visual fraction models and equations to represent the problem.
description_plain: Your child can solve word problems that add or subtract fractions with the same denominator using drawings or fraction models.

subject: Math
description: Add and subtract mixed numbers with like denominators, e.g., by replacing each mixed number with an equivalent fraction, and/or by using properties of operations and the relationship between addition and subtraction.
description_plain: Your child can add and subtract mixed numbers with the same bottom number by changing them into improper fractions or using addition and subtraction rules.

subject: Math
description: Decompose a fraction into a sum of fractions with the same denominator in more than one way, recording each decomposition by an equation. Justify decompositions, e.g., by using a visual fraction model.
description_plain: Your child can break a fraction into smaller fractions with the same bottom number in different ways and explain their thinking using drawings or models.

subject: Math
description: Understand addition and subtraction of fractions as joining and separating parts referring to the same whole.
description_plain: Your child understands that adding and subtracting fractions means putting parts together or taking parts apart from the same whole.

subject: Math
description: Understand a fraction a/b with a > 1 as a sum of fractions 1/b. (Denominators are limited to 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 8, 10, 12, and 100.)
description_plain: Your child understands that a fraction like a/b (where a is more than 1) is the same as adding 1/b fractions a times.

subject: Math
description: Build fractions from unit fractions by applying and extending previous understandings of operations on whole numbers.
description_plain: Your child can build fractions by adding unit fractions and use what they know about whole numbers to do this.

subject: Math
description: Compare two fractions with different numerators and different denominators, e.g., by creating common denominators or numerators, or by comparing to a benchmark fraction such as ½. Recognize that comparisons are valid only when the two fractions refer to the same whole. Record the results of comparisons with symbols >, =, or <, and justify the conclusions, e.g., by using a visual fraction model. (Denominators are limited to 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 8, 10, 12, and 100.)
description_plain: Your child can compare two fractions with different top and bottom numbers by finding common denominators or numerators, or by comparing to ½, and can use >, =, or < to show which is bigger, explaining their answer with pictures.

subject: Math
description: Explain why a fraction a/b is equivalent to a fraction (n × a)/(n × b) by using visual fraction models, with attention to how the number and size of the parts differ even though the two fractions themselves are the same size. Use this principle to recognize and generate equivalent fractions. (Denominators are limited to 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 8, 10, 12, and 100.)
description_plain: Your child can explain why two fractions are equal by using pictures to show how the parts are different sizes but the fractions are the same, and can find and create equivalent fractions, including those greater than 1.

subject: Math
description: Extend understanding of fraction equivalence and ordering.
description_plain: Your child understands how to tell if two fractions are equal and can put fractions in order from smallest to largest.

subject: Math
description: Find whole-number quotients and remainders with up to four-digit dividends and one-digit divisors, using strategies based on place value, the properties of operations, and/or the relationship between multiplication and division. Illustrate and explain the calculation by using equations, rectangular arrays, and/or area models.
description_plain: Your child can divide numbers up to four digits by one-digit numbers, find the quotient and remainder, and explain their work using equations and pictures like arrays or area models.

subject: Math
description: Multiply a whole number of up to four digits by a one-digit whole number, and multiply two two-digit numbers, using strategies based on place value and the properties of operations. Illustrate and explain the calculation by using equations, rectangular arrays, and/or area models.
description_plain: Your child can multiply a number with up to four digits by a one-digit number and multiply two two-digit numbers using place value and properties of multiplication, and explain their work with equations or models.

subject: Math
description: Fluently add and subtract multi-digit whole numbers with sums less than or equal to 1,000,000, using the standard algorithm.
description_plain: Your child can add and subtract large whole numbers up to one million using the standard method.

subject: Math
description: Use place value understanding and properties of operations to perform multi-digit arithmetic.
description_plain: Your child can use their understanding of place value to solve problems with large numbers.

subject: Math
description: Use place value understanding to round multi-digit whole numbers, less than or equal to 1,000,000, to any place.
description_plain: Your child can round large numbers up to one million to any place value.

subject: Math
description: Read and write multi-digit whole numbers less than or equal to 1,000,000 using base-ten numerals, number names, and expanded form. Compare two multi-digit numbers based on meanings of the digits in each place, using >, =, and < symbols to record the results of comparisons.
description_plain: Your child can read, write, and compare large numbers up to one million using digits, words, and expanded form.

subject: Math
description: Recognize that in a multi-digit whole number less than or equal to 1,000,000, a digit in one place represents ten times what it represents in the place to its right.
description_plain: Your child understands that in a large number, a digit in one place is ten times bigger than the same digit in the place to its right.

subject: Math
description: Generalize place value understanding for multi-digit whole numbers.
description_plain: Your child understands how place value works for numbers with more than one digit.

subject: Math
description: Generate a number or shape pattern that follows a given rule. Identify apparent features of the pattern that were not explicit in the rule itself.
description_plain: Your child can create number or shape patterns that follow a rule and notice new features in the pattern that weren’t obvious from the rule.

subject: Math
description: Generate and analyze patterns.
description_plain: Your child can create and study patterns in numbers or shapes.

subject: Math
description: Determine whether ta given whole number is prime or composite.
description_plain: Your child can tell if a whole number is prime or composite.

subject: Math
description: Determine whether a given whole number is a multiple of a given one-digit number.
description_plain: Your child can tell if a whole number is a multiple of a one-digit number.

subject: Math
description: Recognize that a given whole number is a multiple of each of its factors.
description_plain: Your child knows that a whole number is a multiple of each of its factors.

subject: Math
description: Find all factor pairs for a given whole number.
description_plain: Your child can find all pairs of numbers that multiply to make a given whole number.

subject: Math
description: Using whole numbers in the range 1–100
description_plain: Your child works with whole numbers from 1 to 100.

subject: Math
description: Gain familiarity with factors and multiples.
description_plain: Your child is getting to know how factors and multiples work.

subject: Math
description: Solve multi-step word problems posed with whole numbers and having whole-number answers using the four operations, including problems in which remainders must be interpreted. Represent these problems using equations with a letter standing for the unknown quantity. Assess the reasonableness of answers using mental computation and estimation strategies including rounding.
description_plain: Your child can solve multi-step word problems using addition, subtraction, multiplication, and division, including problems with remainders, and check if answers make sense.

subject: Math
description: Multiply or divide to solve word problems involving multiplicative comparison, e.g., by using drawings and/or equations with a symbol for the unknown number to represent the problem, distinguishing multiplicative comparison from additive comparison.
description_plain: Your child can solve word problems that compare amounts by multiplying or dividing, using drawings and equations with symbols for unknown numbers.

subject: Math
description: Interpret a multiplication equation as a comparison and represent verbal statements of multiplicative comparisons as multiplication equations, e.g., interpret 35 = 5 × 7 as a statement that 35 is 5 times as many as 7, and 7 times as many as 5.
description_plain: Your child understands multiplication as a way to compare numbers and can write multiplication equations to show these comparisons.

subject: Math
description: Use the four operations with whole numbers to solve problems.
description_plain: Your child can use addition, subtraction, multiplication, and division to solve different kinds of problems.

subject: Math
description: Look for and express regularity in repeated reasoning.
description_plain: Your child can notice and explain patterns or repeated steps when solving problems.

subject: Math
description: Look for and make use of structure.
description_plain: Your child can notice and use the way math problems are put together to help solve them.

subject: Math
description: Attend to precision.
description_plain: Attend to precision.

subject: Math
description: Use appropriate tools strategically.
description_plain: Your child can choose and use the right tools, like calculators, rulers, or graphs, to help solve math problems.

subject: Math
description: Model with mathematics.
description_plain: Model with mathematics.

subject: Math
description: Construct viable arguments and critique the reasoning of others.
description_plain: Your child can explain their math thinking and listen to and question the ideas of others.

subject: Math
description: Reason abstractly and quantitatively.
description_plain: Your child can think about math problems in different ways, using numbers and real-life examples.

subject: Math
description: Make sense of problems and persevere in solving them.
description_plain: Your child can understand what a math problem is asking and keep working until they find a solution.

subject: Math
description: Compose simple shapes to form larger shapes.
description_plain: Your child can put simple shapes together to make bigger shapes.

subject: Math
description: Model shapes in the world by building shapes from components (e.g., sticks and clay balls) and drawing shapes.
description_plain: Your child can build and draw shapes they see around them using parts like sticks and clay balls.

subject: Math
description: Analyze and compare two- and three-dimensional shapes, in different sizes and orientations, using informal language to describe their similarities, differences, parts (e.g., number of sides and vertices/"corners") and other attributes (e.g., having sides of equal length).
description_plain: Your child can look at flat and solid shapes of different sizes and talk about how they are alike and different, including counting sides and corners.

subject: Math
description: Analyze, compare, create, and compose shapes.
description_plain: Your child can explore, compare, create, and put shapes together.

subject: Math
description: Identify shapes as two-dimensional (lying in a plane, "flat") or three-dimensional ("solid").
description_plain: Your child can tell if a shape is flat (2D) or solid (3D).

subject: Math
description: Correctly name shapes regardless of their orientations or overall size.
description_plain: Your child can correctly name shapes no matter how they are turned or how big they are.

subject: Math
description: Describe objects in the environment using names of shapes, and describe the relative positions of these objects using terms such as above, below, beside, in front of, behind, and next to.
description_plain: Your child can name shapes they see around them and describe where things are using words like above, below, beside, in front of, behind, and next to.

subject: Math
description: Identify and describe shapes (squares, circles, triangles, rectangles, hexagons, cubes, cones, cylinders, and spheres).
description_plain: Your child can identify and talk about shapes like squares, circles, triangles, rectangles, hexagons, cubes, cones, cylinders, and spheres.

subject: Math
description: Recognize pennies, nickels, dimes, and quarters by name and value (e.g., This is a nickel and it is worth 5 cents.)
description_plain: Your child can name pennies, nickels, dimes, and quarters and know how much each coin is worth.

subject: Math
description: Work with money.
description_plain: Work with money.

subject: Math
description: Classify objects into given categories based on their attributes; count the numbers of objects in each category and sort the categories by count.3
description_plain: Your child can sort objects into groups based on their features, count how many are in each group, and arrange the groups by size.

subject: Math
description: Classify objects and count the number of objects in each category.
description_plain: Your child can sort objects into groups and count how many are in each group.

subject: Math
description: Directly compare two objects with a measurable attribute in common, to see which object has "more of"/"less of" the attribute, and describe the difference. For example, directly compare the heights of two children and describe one child as taller/shorter.
description_plain: Your child can compare two objects to see which one is longer, heavier, or has more of something and describe the difference.

subject: Math
description: Describe measurable attributes of objects, such as length or weight. Describe several measurable attributes of a single object.
description_plain: Your child can describe things you can measure about objects, like how long or heavy they are, and describe several of these for one object.

subject: Math
description: Describe and compare measurable attributes.
description_plain: Your child can describe and compare things that can be measured.

subject: Math
description: Record each composition or decomposition using a drawing or equation (e.g., 18 is one ten and eight ones, 18 = 1 ten + 8 ones, 18 = 10 + 8).
description_plain: Your child can show how numbers are made or broken apart using drawings or simple equations.

subject: Math
description: Compose and decompose numbers 11 to 19 using place value (e.g., by using objects or drawings).
description_plain: Your child can put together and take apart numbers from 11 to 19 using objects or pictures.

subject: Math
description: Understand that the numbers 11–19 are composed of ten ones and one, two, three, four, five, six, seven, eight, or nine ones.
description_plain: Your child understands that numbers 11 to 19 are made of ten ones plus some more ones.

subject: Math
description: Gain understanding of place value.
description_plain: Your child understands how place value works in numbers.

subject: Math
description: Work with numbers 11–19 to gain foundations for place value.
description_plain: Your child works with numbers 11 to 19 to learn about tens and ones.

subject: Math
description: Fluently add and subtract within 5.
description_plain: Your child can quickly add and subtract numbers up to 5.

subject: Math
description: For any number from 1 to 9, find the number that makes 10 when added to the given number, e.g., by using objects or drawings, and record the answer with a drawing or equation.
description_plain: Your child can find the number that adds to another number to make 10, using objects or drawings, and write the answer.

subject: Math
description: Decompose numbers less than or equal to 10 into pairs in more than one way, e.g., by using objects or drawings, and record each decomposition by a drawing or equation (e.g., 5 = 2 + 3 and 5 = 4 + 1).
description_plain: Your child can break numbers up to 10 into pairs in different ways using objects or drawings and show each way with a drawing or equation.

subject: Math
description: Solve addition and subtraction word problems, and add and subtract within 10, e.g., by using objects or drawings to represent the problem.
description_plain: Your child can solve simple addition and subtraction story problems and add or subtract numbers up to 10 using objects or drawings.

subject: Math
description: Represent addition and subtraction with objects, fingers, mental images, drawings, sounds (e.g., claps), acting out situations, verbal explanations, expressions, or equations.
description_plain: Your child can show addition and subtraction using objects, fingers, drawings, sounds, acting, or words.

subject: Math
description: Understand addition as putting together and adding to, and understand subtraction as taking apart and taking from.
description_plain: Your child understands that addition means putting things together and subtraction means taking things apart or away.

subject: Math
description: Compare two numbers between 1 and 10 presented as written numerals.
description_plain: Your child can compare two numbers from 1 to 10 written as numerals to see which is bigger or smaller.

subject: Math
description: Identify whether the number of objects in one group is greater than, less than, or equal to the number of objects in another group, e.g., by using matching and counting strategies.1
description_plain: Your child can tell if one group of up to 10 objects has more, less, or the same number as another group by matching or counting.

subject: Math
description: Compare numbers.
description_plain: Compare numbers.

subject: Math
description: When given a number from 1-20, count out that many objects.
description_plain: Your child can count out the right number of objects when given a number from 1 to 20.

subject: Math
description: Count objects up to 10 in a scattered configuration.
description_plain: Your child can count up to 10 objects even if they are spread out.

subject: Math
description: Count objects up to 20, arranged in a line, a rectangular array, or a circle.
description_plain: Your child can count up to 20 objects arranged in a line, rectangle, or circle.

subject: Math
description: Count to answer "How many?" questions.
description_plain: Your child can count objects to answer 'How many?' questions.

subject: Math
description: Understand that each successive number name refers to a quantity that is one larger.
description_plain: Your child understands that when you say the next number in counting, it means one more than the number before.

subject: Math
description: Understand that the last number name said tells the number of objects counted. The number of objects is the same regardless of their arrangement or the order in which they were counted.
description_plain: Your child knows that the last number said when counting tells how many objects there are, no matter how they are arranged or counted.

subject: Math
description: When counting objects in standard order, say the number names as they relate to each object in the group, demonstrating one-to-one correspondence.
description_plain: Your child can count objects one by one, saying a number for each object.

subject: Math
description: Understand the relationship between numbers and quantities; connect counting to cardinality.
description_plain: Your child understands how numbers and counting relate to the amount of things.

subject: Math
description: Count to tell the number of objects.
description_plain: Your child can count objects to find out how many there are.

subject: Math
description: Write numbers from 0 to 20. Represent a number of objects with a written numeral 0–20 (with 0 representing a count of no objects).
description_plain: Your child can write numbers from 0 to 20 and match written numbers to groups of objects, including zero for no objects.

subject: Math
description: Count forward beginning from a given number within the known sequence (instead of having to begin at 1).
description_plain: Your child can count forward starting from any number they know, not just from 1.

subject: Math
description: Count to 100 by ones and by tens.
description_plain: Your child can count to 100 by ones and by tens.

subject: Math
description: Know number names and the count sequence.
description_plain: Your child knows number names and the order of counting.

subject: Math
description: Look for and express regularity in repeated reasoning.
description_plain: Your child can notice and explain patterns or repeated steps when solving problems.

subject: Math
description: Look for and make use of structure.
description_plain: Your child can notice and use the way math problems are put together to help solve them.

subject: Math
description: Attend to precision.
description_plain: Attend to precision.

subject: Math
description: Use appropriate tools strategically.
description_plain: Your child can choose and use the right tools, like calculators, rulers, or graphs, to help solve math problems.

subject: Math
description: Model with mathematics.
description_plain: Model with mathematics.

subject: Math
description: Construct viable arguments and critique the reasoning of others.
description_plain: Your child can explain their math thinking and listen to and question the ideas of others.

subject: Math
description: Reason abstractly and quantitatively.
description_plain: Your child can think about math problems in different ways, using numbers and real-life examples.

subject: Math
description: Make sense of problems and persevere in solving them.
description_plain: Your child can understand what a math problem is asking and keep working until they find a solution.

subject: Math
description: Describe qualitatively the functional relationship between two quantities by analyzing a graph (e.g., where the function is increasing or decreasing, linear or nonlinear). Sketch a graph that exhibits the qualitative features of a function that has been described verbally.
description_plain: Your child can look at a graph and describe how two things change together, like when a line goes up or down, and draw a graph from a description.

subject: Math
description: Construct a function to model a linear relationship between two quantities. Determine the rate of change and initial value of the function from a description of a relationship or from two (x, y) values, including reading these from a table or from a graph. Interpret the rate of change and initial value of a linear function in terms of the situation it models, and in terms of its graph or a table of values.
description_plain: Your child can create a function to show a straight-line relationship between two things, find the rate of change and starting value, and explain what they mean.

subject: Math
description: Use functions to model relationships between quantities.
description_plain: Your child uses functions to show how different amounts are connected.

subject: Math
description: Interpret the equation y = mx + b as defining a linear function, whose graph is a straight line; categorize functions as linear or nonlinear when given equations, graphs, or tables.
description_plain: Your child understands that y = mx + b is a straight-line graph and can tell if a function is linear or not from equations, graphs, or tables.

subject: Math
description: Compare properties of two functions each represented in a different way (algebraically, graphically, numerically in tables, or by verbal descriptions).
description_plain: Your child can compare two functions shown in different ways, like with an equation, graph, table, or words.

subject: Math
description: Understand that a function is a rule that assigns to each input exactly one output. The graph of a function is the set of ordered pairs consisting of an input and the corresponding output.
description_plain: Your child understands that a function is a rule that gives exactly one answer for each input, and can find points on its graph that match inputs and outputs.

subject: Math
description: Define, evaluate, and compare functions.
description_plain: Your child can define what functions are, find their values, and compare different functions.

subject: Math
description: Understand that patterns of association can also be seen in bivariate categorical data by displaying frequencies and relative frequencies in a two-way table. Construct and interpret a two-way table summarizing data on two categorical variables collected from the same subjects. Use relative frequencies calculated for rows or columns to describe possible association between the two variables.
description_plain: Your child can make and read two-way tables to see how two categories of data might be related.

subject: Math
description: Use the equation of a linear model to solve problems in the context of bivariate measurement data, interpreting the slope and intercept.
description_plain: Your child can use a line equation to solve problems with two related measurements and explain what the slope and intercept mean.

subject: Math
description: Know that straight lines are widely used to model relationships between two quantitative variables. For scatter plots that suggest a linear association, informally fit a straight line, and informally assess the model fit by judging the closeness of the data points to the line.
description_plain: Your child knows that straight lines often show relationships between two numbers and can draw a line that fits data points on a graph.

subject: Math
description: Construct and interpret scatter plots for bivariate measurement data to investigate patterns of association between two quantities. Describe patterns such as clustering, outliers, positive or negative association, linear association, and nonlinear association.
description_plain: Your child can make and read scatter plots to find patterns between two sets of data, like clusters or trends.

subject: Math
description: Investigate patterns of association in bivariate data.
description_plain: Your child can study how two sets of data relate to each other.

subject: Math
description: Solve real-world and mathematical problems leading to two linear equations in two variables.
description_plain: Your child can solve real and math problems that involve two linear equations with two variables.

subject: Math
description: Solve systems of two linear equations in two variables algebraically, and estimate solutions by graphing the equations. Solve simple cases by inspection.
description_plain: Your child can solve two straight-line equations with two unknowns using algebra, estimate answers by graphing, and solve simple cases by looking.

subject: Math
description: Understand that solutions to a system of two linear equations in two variables correspond to points of intersection of their graphs, because points of intersection satisfy both equations simultaneously.
description_plain: Your child understands that the solution to two straight-line equations is where their graphs cross, because that point fits both equations.

subject: Math
description: Analyze and solve pairs of simultaneous linear equations.
description_plain: Your child can analyze and solve pairs of straight-line equations at the same time.

subject: Math
description: Solve linear equations with rational number coefficients, including equations whose solutions require expanding expressions using the distributive property and collecting like terms.
description_plain: Your child can solve linear equations with fractions and decimals, including those that need expanding and combining like terms.

subject: Math
description: Give examples of linear equations in one variable with one solution, infinitely many solutions, or no solutions. Show which of these possibilities is the case by successively transforming the given equation into simpler forms, until an equivalent equation of the form x = a, a = a, or a = b results (where a and b are different numbers).
description_plain: Your child can give examples of straight-line equations with one solution, many solutions, or no solutions, and show which by simplifying the equation step-by-step.

subject: Math
description: Solve linear equations in one variable.
description_plain: Your child can solve one-step and multi-step linear equations with one variable.

subject: Math
description: Analyze and solve linear equations and pairs of simultaneous linear equations.
description_plain: Your child understands how to solve single and pairs of linear equations.

subject: Math
description: Use similar triangles to explain why the slope m is the same between any two distinct points on a non-vertical line in the coordinate plane; derive the equation y = mx for a line through the origin and the equation y = mx + b for a line intercepting the vertical axis at b.
description_plain: Your child can explain why the slope (steepness) is the same between any two points on a line and use the equations y = mx and y = mx + b to describe lines.

subject: Math
description: Graph proportional relationships, interpreting the unit rate as the slope of the graph. Compare two different proportional relationships represented in different ways.
description_plain: Your child can graph proportional relationships and understand the unit rate as the slope, and compare different proportional relationships shown in various ways.

subject: Math
description: Understand the connections between proportional relationships, lines, and linear equations.
description_plain: Your child understands how proportional relationships, lines, and straight-line equations are connected.

subject: Math
description: Perform operations with numbers expressed in scientific notation, including problems where both decimal and scientific notation are used. Use scientific notation and choose units of appropriate size for measurements of very large or very small quantities (e.g., use millimeters per year for seafloor spreading). Interpret scientific notation that has been generated by technology.
description_plain: Your child can do math with numbers in scientific notation, including mixing decimals and scientific notation, choose the right units for very big or small measurements, and understand scientific notation from technology.

subject: Math
description: Use numbers expressed in the form of a single digit times an integer power of 10 to estimate very large or very small quantities, and to express how many times as much one is than the other.
description_plain: Your child can use numbers written as a digit times a power of 10 to estimate very big or small amounts and compare how many times bigger one is than another.

subject: Math
description: Use square root and cube root symbols to represent solutions to equations of the form x² = p and x³ = p, where p is a positive rational number. Evaluate square roots of small perfect squares and cube roots of small perfect cubes. Know that √2 is irrational.
description_plain: Your child can use square root and cube root symbols to solve equations like x² = p and x³ = p, and can find the roots of small perfect squares and cubes.

subject: Math
description: Know and apply the properties of integer exponents to generate equivalent numerical expressions.
description_plain: Your child understands how to use rules for exponents to rewrite and simplify expressions with whole number powers.

subject: Math
description: Work with radicals and integer exponents.
description_plain: Your child works with roots (like square roots) and whole number exponents.

subject: Math
description: Use rational approximations of irrational numbers to compare the size of irrational numbers, locate them approximately on a number line diagram, and estimate the value of expressions (e.g., π²).
description_plain: Your child can estimate and compare irrational numbers by using close rational numbers and place them approximately on a number line.

subject: Math
description: Know that numbers that are not rational are called irrational. Understand informally that every number has a decimal expansion; for rational numbers, show that the decimal expansion repeats eventually. Convert a decimal expansion that repeats eventually into a rational number by analyzing repeating patterns.
description_plain: Your child knows that numbers that can’t be written as fractions are called irrational, understands that all numbers have decimal forms, and can change repeating decimals back into fractions.

subject: Math
description: Know that there are numbers that are not rational, and approximate them by rational numbers.
description_plain: Your child knows there are numbers that can't be written as fractions and can estimate them using fractions or decimals.

subject: Math
description: Know the formulas for the volumes of cones, cylinders, and spheres and use them to solve real-world and mathematical problems.
description_plain: Your child knows formulas for the volume of cones, cylinders, and spheres and can use them to solve problems.

subject: Math
description: Solve real-world and mathematical problems involving volume of cylinders, cones, and spheres.
description_plain: Your child can solve real-life and math problems involving the volume of cylinders, cones, and spheres.

subject: Math
description: Apply the Pythagorean Theorem to find the distance between two points in a coordinate system.
description_plain: Your child can use the Pythagorean Theorem to find the distance between two points on a graph.

subject: Math
description: Apply the Pythagorean Theorem to determine unknown side lengths in right triangles in real-world and mathematical problems in two and three dimensions.
description_plain: Your child can use the Pythagorean Theorem to find missing side lengths in right triangles in both 2D and 3D problems.

subject: Math
description: Explain a proof of the Pythagorean Theorem and its converse using the area of squares.
description_plain: Your child can explain the Pythagorean Theorem and its reverse using the areas of squares.

subject: Math
description: Understand and apply the Pythagorean Theorem.
description_plain: Your child can understand and use the Pythagorean Theorem to solve problems.

subject: Math
description: Use informal arguments to establish facts about the angle sum and exterior angle of triangles, about the angles created when parallel lines are cut by a transversal, and the angle-angle criterion for similarity of triangles.
description_plain: Your child can explain facts about angles in triangles and parallel lines and how to tell if triangles are similar.

subject: Math
description: Explain that a two-dimensional figure is similar to another if the second can be obtained from the first by a sequence of rotations, reflections, translations, and dilations; given two similar two-dimensional figures, describe a sequence that exhibits the similarity between them. (Rotations are only about the origin, dilations only use the origin as the center of dilation, and reflections are only over the y-axis and x-axis in Grade 8.)
description_plain: Your child understands that two shapes are similar if one can be changed into the other by moving, flipping, or resizing it.

subject: Math
description: Describe the effect of dilations, translations, rotations, and reflections on two-dimensional figures using coordinates. (Rotations are only about the origin, dilations only use the origin as the center of dilation, and reflections are only over the y-axis and x-axis in Grade 8.)
description_plain: Your child can describe how moving, flipping, turning, or resizing shapes changes their position using coordinates.

subject: Math
description: Explain that a two-dimensional figure is congruent to another if the second can be obtained from the first by a sequence of rotations, reflections, and translations; given two congruent figures, describe a sequence that exhibits the congruence between them. (Rotations are only about the origin and reflections are only over the y-axis and x-axis in Grade 8.)
description_plain: Your child understands that two shapes are congruent if one can be moved or flipped to match the other exactly.

subject: Math
description: Parallel lines are taken to parallel lines.
description_plain: Your child understands that parallel lines stay parallel after transformations.

subject: Math
description: Angles are taken to angles of the same measure.
description_plain: Your child understands that angles keep their size after transformations.

subject: Math
description: Lines are taken to lines, and line segments to line segments of the same length.
description_plain: Your child understands that lines and line segments keep their length after transformations.

subject: Math
description: Verify experimentally the properties of rotations, reflections, and translations:
description_plain: Your child can test and confirm how rotations, reflections, and slides change shapes.

subject: Math
description: Understand congruence and similarity using physical models, transparencies, or geometry software.
description_plain: Your child uses models or tools to understand when shapes are the same size and shape or just similar.

subject: Math
description: Look for and express regularity in repeated reasoning.
description_plain: Your child can notice and explain patterns or repeated steps when solving problems.

subject: Math
description: Look for and make use of structure.
description_plain: Your child can notice and use the way math problems are put together to help solve them.

subject: Math
description: Attend to precision.
description_plain: Attend to precision.

subject: Math
description: Use appropriate tools strategically.
description_plain: Your child can choose and use the right tools, like calculators, rulers, or graphs, to help solve math problems.

subject: Math
description: Model with mathematics.
description_plain: Model with mathematics.

subject: Math
description: Construct viable arguments and critique the reasoning of others.
description_plain: Your child can explain their math thinking and listen to and question the ideas of others.

subject: Math
description: Reason abstractly and quantitatively.
description_plain: Your child can think about math problems in different ways, using numbers and real-life examples.

subject: Math
description: Make sense of problems and persevere in solving them.
description_plain: Your child can understand what a math problem is asking and keep working until they find a solution.

subject: Math
description: Relating the choice of measures of center and variability to the shape of the data distribution and the context in which the data were gathered.
description_plain: Your child can choose the right way to describe data based on how the data looks and where it came from.

subject: Math
description: Giving quantitative measures of center (median and/or mean) and variability (interquartile range) as well as describing any overall pattern and any striking deviations from the overall pattern with reference to the context in which the data were gathered.
description_plain: Your child can describe the middle and spread of data, notice patterns, and point out anything unusual using numbers like median, mean, and interquartile range.

subject: Math
description: Describing the nature of the attribute under investigation, including how it was measured and its units of measurement.
description_plain: Your child can describe what is being measured in data, how it was measured, and the units used.

subject: Math
description: Reporting the number of observations.
description_plain: Your child can tell how many observations or data points were collected.

subject: Math
description: Summarize numerical data sets in relation to their context, such as by:
description_plain: Your child can summarize sets of numbers by explaining what they mean in real life.

subject: Math
description: Display numerical data in plots on a number line, including dot plots, histograms, and box plots.
description_plain: Your child can show number data on number lines using dot plots, histograms, and box plots.

subject: Math
description: Summarize and describe distributions.
description_plain: Your child can summarize and describe how data is spread out and what patterns it shows.

subject: Math
description: Recognize that a measure of center for a numerical data set summarizes all of its values with a single number, while a measure of variation describes how its values vary with a single number.
description_plain: Your child understands that one number can show the middle of data, and another number can show how much the data varies.

subject: Math
description: Understand that a set of data collected to answer a statistical question has a distribution that can be described by its center, spread, and overall shape.
description_plain: Your child understands that data collected to answer questions has a center, spread, and shape that can be described.

subject: Math
description: Recognize a statistical question as one that anticipates variability in the data related to the question and accounts for it in the answers.
description_plain: Your child can recognize questions that expect different answers because the data changes each time.

subject: Math
description: Develop understanding of statistical variability.
description_plain: Your child understands that data can vary and learns how to describe that variability.

subject: Math
description: Use variables to represent two quantities in a real-world problem that change in relationship to one another; write an equation to express one quantity, thought of as the dependent variable, in terms of the other quantity, thought of as the independent variable. Analyze the relationship between the dependent and independent variables using graphs and tables, and relate these to the equation.
description_plain: Your child can use letters to stand for two amounts in a real-life problem that change depending on each other.

subject: Math
description: Represent and analyze quantitative relationships between dependent and independent variables.
description_plain: Your child can show and study how one quantity depends on another using math.

subject: Math
description: Write an inequality of the form x > c or x < c to represent a constraint or condition in a real-world or mathematical problem. Recognize that inequalities of the form x > c or x < c have infinitely many solutions; represent solutions of such inequalities on number line diagrams.
description_plain: Your child can write inequalities to describe limits in real life or math problems, know these have many answers, and show those answers on a number line.

subject: Math
description: Solve real-world and mathematical problems by writing and solving equations and inequalities of the form x + p = q and px = q for cases in which p, q and x are all nonnegative rational numbers. Inequalities will include <, >, ≤, and ≥.
description_plain: Your child can solve real-life and math problems by writing and solving simple equations and inequalities with positive numbers.

subject: Math
description: Use variables to represent numbers and write expressions when solving a real-world or mathematical problem; understand that a variable can represent an unknown number, or, depending on the purpose at hand, any number in a specified set.
description_plain: Your child can use letters to stand for numbers and write expressions to solve real-life or math problems, knowing that letters can mean unknown or any number in a group.

subject: Math
description: Understand solving an equation or inequality as a process of answering a question: which values from a specified set, if any, make the equation or inequality true? Use substitution to determine whether a given number in a specified set makes an equation or inequality true.
description_plain: Your child understands that solving an equation or inequality means finding which numbers make it true and can check answers by substituting numbers.

subject: Math
description: Reason about and solve one-variable equations and inequalities.
description_plain: Your child can think through and solve equations and inequalities with one variable.

subject: Math
description: Identify when two expressions are equivalent (i.e., when the two expressions name the same number regardless of which value is substituted into them).
description_plain: Your child can tell when two expressions are equal because they give the same result no matter what number is used.

subject: Math
description: Apply the properties of operations to generate equivalent expressions.
description_plain: Your child can use math rules to create different expressions that are equal.

subject: Math
description: Evaluate expressions at specific values of their variables. Include expressions that arise from formulas used in real-world problems. Perform arithmetic operations, including those involving whole-number exponents, in the conventional order when there are no parentheses to specify a particular order (Order of Operations).
description_plain: Your child can find the value of expressions by plugging in numbers for letters, including using formulas from real-life problems, and follows the correct order of operations.

subject: Math
description: Identify parts of an expression using mathematical terms (sum, term, product, factor, quotient, coefficient); view one or more parts of an expression as a single entity.
description_plain: Your child can name parts of an expression using words like sum, term, product, factor, quotient, and coefficient, and can see parts of an expression as one piece.

subject: Math
description: Write expressions that record operations with numbers and with letters standing for numbers.
description_plain: Your child can write expressions that show math operations using numbers and letters that stand for numbers.

subject: Math
description: Write, read, and evaluate expressions in which letters stand for numbers.
description_plain: Your child can write, read, and find the value of expressions where letters stand for numbers.

subject: Math
description: Write and evaluate numerical expressions involving whole-number exponents.
description_plain: Your child can write and solve number expressions that include whole-number exponents.

subject: Math
description: Apply and extend previous understandings of arithmetic to algebraic expressions.
description_plain: Your child can use what they know about arithmetic to work with algebra expressions.

subject: Math
description: Solve real-world and mathematical problems by graphing points in all four quadrants of the coordinate plane. Include use of coordinates and absolute value to find distances between points with the same first coordinate or the same second coordinate.
description_plain: Your child can solve problems by plotting points in all four parts of the coordinate plane and use coordinates and absolute value to find distances between points.

subject: Math
description: Distinguish comparisons of absolute value from statements about order.
description_plain: Your child can tell the difference between comparing absolute values and comparing the order of numbers.

subject: Math
description: Understand the absolute value of a rational number as its distance from 0 on the number line; interpret absolute value as magnitude for a positive or negative quantity in a real-world situation.
description_plain: Your child understands absolute value as the distance of a number from zero and can explain it in real situations.

subject: Math
description: Write, interpret, and explain statements of order for rational numbers in real-world contexts.
description_plain: Your child can write and explain statements about the order of rational numbers in real-life situations.

subject: Math
description: Interpret statements of inequality as statements about the relative position of two numbers on a number line diagram.
description_plain: Your child can understand inequalities as showing which number is bigger or smaller on a number line.

subject: Math
description: Understand ordering and absolute value of rational numbers.
description_plain: Your child understands how to order rational numbers and what absolute value means.

subject: Math
description: Find and position integers and other rational numbers on a horizontal or vertical number line diagram; find and position pairs of integers and other rational numbers on a coordinate plane.
description_plain: Your child can find and place integers and other rational numbers on number lines and coordinate planes.

subject: Math
description: Understand signs of numbers in ordered pairs as indicating locations in quadrants of the coordinate plane; recognize that when two ordered pairs differ only by signs, the locations of the points are related by reflections across one or both axes.
description_plain: Your child understands that the signs in ordered pairs tell where points are on the coordinate plane and that changing signs reflects points across axes.

subject: Math
description: Recognize opposite signs of numbers as indicating locations on opposite sides of 0 on the number line; recognize that the opposite of the opposite of a number is the number itself, e.g., –(–3) = 3, and that 0 is its own opposite.
description_plain: Your child knows that opposite signs mean numbers are on opposite sides of zero and that the opposite of the opposite number is the original number.

subject: Math
description: Understand a rational number as a point on the number line. Extend number line diagrams and coordinate axes familiar from previous grades to represent points on the line and in the plane with negative number coordinates.
description_plain: Your child understands that rational numbers can be shown as points on a number line, including negative numbers.

subject: Math
description: Understand that positive and negative numbers are used together to describe quantities having opposite directions or values (e.g., temperature above/below zero, elevation above/below sea level, credits/debits, positive/negative electric charge); use positive and negative numbers to represent quantities in real-world contexts, explaining the meaning of 0 in each situation.
description_plain: Your child understands that positive and negative numbers can show things like direction or value, and can explain what zero means in different situations.

subject: Math
description: Apply and extend previous understandings of numbers to the system of rational numbers.
description_plain: Your child can use what they know about numbers to understand and work with positive and negative numbers, including fractions and decimals.

subject: Math
description: Find the greatest common factor of two whole numbers less than or equal to 100 and the least common multiple of two whole numbers less than or equal to 12. Use the distributive property to express a sum of two whole numbers 1–100 with a common factor as a multiple of a sum of two whole numbers with no common factor.
description_plain: Your child can find the greatest common factor of two whole numbers up to 100 and the least common multiple of two whole numbers up to 12, and can use the distributive property to rewrite sums with common factors.

subject: Math
description: Fluently add, subtract, multiply, and divide multi-digit decimals using the standard algorithm for each operation.
description_plain: Your child can quickly and accurately add, subtract, multiply, and divide decimals with many digits using standard methods.

subject: Math
description: Fluently divide multi-digit numbers using the standard algorithm.
description_plain: Your child can quickly and accurately divide large numbers using the standard method.

subject: Math
description: Compute fluently with multi-digit numbers and find common factors and multiples.
description_plain: Your child can work easily with large numbers and find common factors and multiples.

subject: Math
description: Interpret and compute quotients of fractions, and solve word problems involving division of fractions by fractions, e.g., by using visual fraction models and equations to represent the problem.
description_plain: Your child can understand and calculate division of fractions and solve word problems about dividing fractions by fractions using pictures or equations.

subject: Math
description: Apply and extend previous understandings of multiplication and division to divide fractions by fractions.
description_plain: Your child can use what they know about multiplication and division to solve problems with fractions dividing by fractions.

subject: Math
description: Use ratio reasoning to convert measurement units; manipulate and transform units appropriately when multiplying or dividing quantities.
description_plain: Your child can use ratio thinking to change measurement units and correctly adjust units when multiplying or dividing.

subject: Math
description: Find a percent of a quantity as a rate per 100 (e.g., 30% of a quantity means 30/100 times the quantity); solve problems involving finding the whole, given a part and the percent.
description_plain: Your child can find a percent of a number and solve problems where they know a part and the percent to find the whole amount.

subject: Math
description: Solve unit rate problems including those involving unit pricing and constant speed.
description_plain: Your child can solve problems about unit rates, like finding the best price per item or speed.

subject: Math
description: Make tables of equivalent ratios relating quantities with whole-number measurements, find missing values in the tables, and plot the pairs of values on the coordinate plane. Use tables to compare ratios.
description_plain: Your child can make tables showing equivalent ratios with whole numbers, find missing numbers, and graph these pairs to compare ratios.

subject: Math
description: Use ratio and rate reasoning to solve real-world and mathematical problems, e.g., by reasoning about tables of equivalent ratios, tape diagrams, double number line diagrams, or equations.
description_plain: Your child can use ratios and rates to solve real-life and math problems by working with tables, diagrams, or equations that show equivalent ratios.

subject: Math
description: Understand the concept of a unit rate a/b associated with a ratio a:b with b ≠ 0, and use rate language in the context of a ratio relationship.
description_plain: Your child understands what a unit rate is and can talk about rates in ratio problems.

subject: Math
description: Understand the concept of a ratio and use ratio language to describe a ratio relationship between two quantities.
description_plain: Your child understands what a ratio is and can use words to describe the relationship between two amounts, knowing that ratios are different from fractions.

subject: Math
description: Understand ratio concepts and use ratio reasoning to solve problems.
description_plain: Your child understands ratios and can use them to solve problems.

subject: Math
description: Represent three-dimensional figures using nets made up of rectangles and triangles, and use the nets to find the surface area of these figures. Apply these techniques in the context of solving real-world and mathematical problems.
description_plain: Your child can draw flat patterns (nets) made of rectangles and triangles to build 3D shapes and use these to find surface area in real problems.

subject: Math
description: Draw polygons in the coordinate plane given coordinates for the vertices; use coordinates to find the length of a side joining points with the same first coordinate or the same second coordinate. Apply these techniques in the context of solving real-world and mathematical problems.
description_plain: Your child can draw shapes on a grid using points and find side lengths when sides are straight up/down or left/right, solving real problems.

subject: Math
description: Find the volume of a right rectangular prism with fractional edge lengths by packing it with unit cubes of the appropriate unit fraction edge lengths, and show that the volume is the same as would be found by multiplying the edge lengths of the prism. Apply the formulas V = l w h and V = b h to find volumes of right rectangular prisms with fractional edge lengths in the context of solving real-world and mathematical problems.
description_plain: Your child can find the volume of boxes with fractional side lengths by filling them with small cubes and by multiplying the edges, using formulas in real-life problems.

subject: Math
description: Find the area of right triangles, other triangles, special quadrilaterals, and polygons by composing into rectangles or decomposing into triangles and other shapes; apply these techniques in the context of solving real-world and mathematical problems.
description_plain: Your child can find the area of triangles, special four-sided shapes, and other polygons by breaking them into rectangles or triangles, and use this in real problems.

subject: Math
description: Solve real-world and mathematical problems involving area, surface area, and volume.
description_plain: Your child can solve real-life and math problems involving area, surface area, and volume.

subject: Math
description: Look for and express regularity in repeated reasoning.
description_plain: Your child can notice and explain patterns or repeated steps when solving problems.

subject: Math
description: Look for and make use of structure.
description_plain: Your child can notice and use the way math problems are put together to help solve them.

subject: Math
description: Attend to precision.
description_plain: Attend to precision.

subject: Math
description: Use appropriate tools strategically.
description_plain: Your child can choose and use the right tools, like calculators, rulers, or graphs, to help solve math problems.

subject: Math
description: Model with mathematics.
description_plain: Model with mathematics.

subject: Math
description: Construct viable arguments and critique the reasoning of others.
description_plain: Your child can explain their math thinking and listen to and question the ideas of others.

subject: Math
description: Reason abstractly and quantitatively.
description_plain: Your child can think about math problems in different ways, using numbers and real-life examples.

subject: Math
description: Make sense of problems and persevere in solving them.
description_plain: Your child can understand what a math problem is asking and keep working until they find a solution.

subject: Science
description: Make observations at different times of year to relate the amount of daylight to the time of year.
description_plain: Your child can watch how the amount of daylight changes during different times of the year.

subject: Science
description: Use observations of the sun, moon, and stars to describe patterns that can be predicted.
description_plain: Your child can observe the sun, moon, and stars to find patterns that happen again and again.

subject: Science
description: Make observations to construct an evidence-based account that young plants and animals are similar, but not exactly like, their parents.
description_plain: Your child can observe and explain that young plants and animals look like their parents but are not exactly the same.

subject: Science
description: Read grade-appropriate texts and use media to determine patterns in behavior of parents and offspring that help offspring survive.
description_plain: Your child can read and watch videos to find patterns in how parents and their young behave to help the young survive.

subject: Science
description: Use tools and materials to design a solution to a human problem by mimicking how plants and/or animals use their external parts to help them survive, grow, and meet their needs.
description_plain: Your child can use tools and materials to design a way to solve a problem by copying how plants or animals use their body parts to survive and grow.

subject: Science
description: Use tools and materials to design and build a device that uses light or sound to solve the problem of communicating over a distance.
description_plain: Your child can design and build a tool that uses light or sound to help people communicate from far away.

subject: Science
description: Plan and conduct an investigation to determine the effect of placing objects made with different materials in the path of a beam of light.
description_plain: Your child can plan and do an experiment to see what happens when light shines on objects made of different materials.

subject: Science
description: Make observations to construct an evidence-based account that objects can be seen only when illuminated.
description_plain: Your child can observe and explain that we can only see objects when light shines on them.

subject: Science
description: Plan and conduct investigations to provide evidence that vibrating materials can make sound and that sound can make materials vibrate.
