description_plain: Your child can plan and do experiments to show that vibrating things make sound and sound can make things vibrate.

subject: Science
description: Communicate solutions that will reduce the impact of humans on the land, water, air, and/or other living things in the local environment.
description_plain: Your child can share ideas to help reduce how people harm the land, water, air, or living things nearby.

subject: Science
description: Ask questions to obtain information about the purpose of weather forecasting to prepare for and respond to severe weather.
description_plain: Your child can ask questions to learn why weather forecasts help us get ready for weather.

subject: Science
description: Use a model to represent the relationship between the needs of different plants or animals (including humans) and the places they live.
description_plain: Your child will use a model to show how different plants and animals (including people) need certain things and places to live.

subject: Science
description: Construct an argument supported by evidence for how plants and animals (including humans) can change the environment to meet their needs.
description_plain: Your child can explain with examples how plants and animals (including people) change their surroundings to meet their needs.

subject: Science
description: Use and share observations of local weather conditions to describe patterns over time.
description_plain: Your child will watch and share what the weather is like in your area to notice patterns over time.

subject: Science
description: Use observations to describe patterns of what plants and animals (including humans) need to survive.
description_plain: Your child can watch and describe what plants and animals, including people, need to live.

subject: Science
description: Use tools and materials to design and build a structure that will reduce the warming effect of sunlight on an area.
description_plain: Your child can use tools and materials to build something that helps keep an area cooler by blocking sunlight.

subject: Science
description: Make observations to determine the effect of sunlight on Earth's surface.
description_plain: Your child will observe and describe how sunlight affects the Earth's surface.

subject: Science
description: Analyze data to determine if a design solution works as intended to change the speed or direction of an object with a push or a pull.
description_plain: Your child can look at data to decide if a design works to change how fast or which way an object moves when pushed or pulled.

subject: Science
description: Plan and conduct an investigation to compare the effects of different strengths or different directions of pushes and pulls on the motion of an object.
description_plain: Your child will plan and do an experiment to see how different pushes and pulls affect how an object moves.

subject: Science
description: Obtain and communicate information to identify where water is found on Earth and that it can be solid or liquid.
description_plain: Your child can find and share information about where water is on Earth and that it can be ice or liquid.

subject: Science
description: Develop a model to represent the shapes and kinds of land and bodies of water in an area.
description_plain: Your child can create a model that shows the shapes and types of land and water in a certain place.

subject: Science
description: Compare multiple solutions designed to slow or prevent wind or water from changing the shape of the land.
description_plain: Your child can compare different ways people try to stop wind or water from changing the land’s shape.

subject: Science
description: Use information from several sources to provide evidence that Earth events can occur quickly or slowly.
description_plain: Your child can use information from different places to show that Earth changes can happen fast or slowly.

subject: Science
description: Make observations of plants and animals to compare the diversity of life in different habitats.
description_plain: Your child can watch plants and animals and compare how many different kinds live in different places.

subject: Science
description: Develop a simple model that mimics the function of an animal in dispersing seeds or pollinating plants.
description_plain: Your child can make a simple model that shows how an animal helps spread seeds or pollinate plants.

subject: Science
description: Plan and conduct an investigation to determine if plants need sunlight and water to grow.
description_plain: Your child can plan and do an experiment to see if plants need sunlight and water to grow.

subject: Science
description: Construct an argument with evidence that some changes caused by heating or cooling can be reversed and some cannot.
description_plain: Your child can explain with evidence which changes caused by heating or cooling can be undone and which cannot.

subject: Science
description: Make observations to construct an evidence-based account of how an object made of a small set of pieces can be disassembled and made into a new object.
description_plain: Your child can watch and describe how an object made of a few parts can be taken apart and made into something new.

subject: Science
description: Analyze data obtained from testing different materials to determine which materials have the properties that are best suited for an intended purpose.
description_plain: Your child can test different materials and decide which ones work best for a certain use.

subject: Science
description: Plan and conduct an investigation to describe and classify different kinds of materials by their observable properties.
description_plain: Your child can plan and do an experiment to describe and sort materials by what they look and feel like.

subject: Science
description: Identify evidence from patterns in rock formations and fossils in rock layers to support an explanation for changes in landforms over time.
description_plain: Your child uses patterns in rocks and fossils to explain how a landscape has changed over time.

subject: Science
description: Generate and compare multiple solutions to reduce the impacts of natural Earth processes on humans.
description_plain: Your child comes up with and compares different ideas to lessen how natural Earth events impact people.

subject: Science
description: Obtain and combine information to describe that energy and fuels are derived from renewable and non-renewable resources and how their uses affect the environment.
description_plain: Your child can gather information to explain that energy and fuels come from resources that can be used again or not, and how using them affects the environment.

subject: Science
description: Ask questions that can be investigated and predict reasonable outcomes about how living things affect the physical characteristics of their environment.
description_plain: Your child can ask questions and guess what will happen about how living things change their surroundings.

subject: Science
description: Analyze and interpret data from maps to describe patterns of Earth's features.
description_plain: Your child looks at maps and data to describe patterns in Earth’s features.

subject: Science
description: Plan and conduct investigations on the effects of water, ice, wind, and vegetation on the relative rate of weathering and erosion.
description_plain: Your child can plan and do experiments to see how water, ice, wind, and plants change the speed of weathering and erosion.

subject: Science
description: Construct an explanation to describe how animals receive different types of information through their senses, process the information in their brains, and respond to the information in different ways.
description_plain: Your child can explain how animals get information through their senses, think about it in their brains, and react in different ways.

subject: Science
description: Construct an argument that plants and animals have internal and external structures that function to support survival, growth, behavior, and reproduction.
description_plain: Your child explains how plants and animals have body parts inside and outside that help them survive, grow, behave, and reproduce.

subject: Science
description: Develop a model to describe that light reflecting from objects and entering the eye allows objects to be seen.
description_plain: Your child makes a model to show that we see objects because light reflects off them and enters our eyes.

subject: Science
description: Develop a model of waves to describe patterns in terms of amplitude and wavelength and to show that waves can cause objects to move.
description_plain: Your child can build a model to show how waves have height and length and how waves can make things move.

subject: Science
description: Apply scientific ideas to design, test, and refine a device that converts energy from one form to another.
description_plain: Your child can design, test, and improve a device that changes energy from one type to another.

subject: Science
description: Ask questions and predict outcomes about the changes in energy that occur when objects collide.
description_plain: Your child will ask questions and guess what will happen to energy when objects bump into each other.

subject: Science
description: Make observations to provide evidence that energy can be transferred from place to place by sound, light, heat, and electric currents.
description_plain: Your child can watch and describe how energy moves from one place to another through sound, light, heat, and electricity.

subject: Science
description: Use evidence to construct an explanation relating the speed of an object to the energy of that object.
description_plain: Your child will use evidence to explain how the speed of something relates to its energy.

subject: Science
description: Generate and compare multiple solutions about ways individual communities can use science to protect the Earth's resources and environment.
description_plain: Your child can come up with and compare different ideas about how communities can use science to protect Earth's resources and environment.

subject: Science
description: Describe and graph the amounts and percentages of water and fresh water in various reservoirs to provide evidence about the distribution of water on Earth.
description_plain: Your child can describe and graph amounts and percentages of water in different places to show how water is spread on Earth.

subject: Science
description: Develop a model using an example to describe ways the geosphere, biosphere, hydrosphere, and/or atmosphere interact.
description_plain: Your child can build a model to show how land, living things, water, and air work together.

subject: Science
description: Represent data in graphical displays to reveal patterns of daily changes in length and direction of shadows, day and night, and the seasonal appearance of some stars in the night sky.
description_plain: Your child can create graphs to show daily changes in shadows, day and night, and how some stars appear differently during seasons.

subject: Science
description: Support an argument that differences in the apparent brightness of the sun compared to other stars is due to their relative distances from the Earth.
description_plain: Your child can explain why the sun looks brighter than other stars because it is closer to Earth.

subject: Science
description: Develop a model to describe the movement of matter among plants, animals, decomposers, and the environment.
description_plain: Your child can create a model to show how matter moves between plants, animals, decomposers, and the environment.

subject: Science
description: Ask questions about how air and water affect the growth of plants.
description_plain: Your child can ask questions about how air and water help plants grow.

subject: Science
description: Use models to describe that energy in animals' food (used for body repair, growth, motion, and to maintain body warmth) was once energy from the sun.
description_plain: Your child can use models to show that the energy animals get from food originally comes from the sun.

subject: Science
description: Support an argument that the gravitational force exerted by the Earth is directed down.
description_plain: Your child can explain why Earth's gravity pulls things down.

subject: Science
description: Conduct an investigation to determine whether the mixing of two or more substances results in new substances.
description_plain: Your child can do an experiment to find out if mixing substances creates new substances.

subject: Science
description: Make observations and measurements to identify materials based on their properties.
description_plain: Your child can observe and measure materials to identify them by their properties.

subject: Science
description: Measure and graph quantities to provide evidence that regardless of the type of change that occurs when heating, cooling, or mixing substances, the total amount of matter is conserved.
description_plain: Your child can measure and graph amounts to show that matter's weight stays the same during heating, cooling, or mixing.

subject: Science
description: Develop a model to describe that matter is made of particles too small to be seen.
description_plain: Your child can create a model to show that matter is made of tiny particles too small to see.

subject: Science
description: Make a claim about the merit of a design solution that reduces the impact of a weather-related hazard.
description_plain: Your child can make a claim about how well a design helps reduce damage from weather hazards.

subject: Science
description: Obtain and combine information to describe climates in different regions around the world.
description_plain: Your child can find and combine information to describe climates in different parts of the world.

subject: Science
description: Represent data in tables and graphical displays to describe typical weather conditions expected during a particular season.
description_plain: Your child can show weather data in tables and graphs to explain what weather is usually like in a season.

subject: Science
description: Make a claim about the merit of a solution to a problem caused when the environment changes and the types of plants and animals that live there may change.
description_plain: Your child can explain if a solution is good for helping plants and animals when their environment changes.

subject: Science
description: Construct and support an argument with evidence that in a particular habitat some organisms can survive well, some survive less well, and some cannot survive at all.
description_plain: Your child can explain with evidence that in a habitat some animals survive well, some less well, and some not at all.

subject: Science
description: Use evidence to construct an explanation for how the variations in characteristics among individuals of the same species may provide advantages in surviving, finding mates, and reproducing.
description_plain: Your child understands how differences in animals or plants help them survive, find mates, and have babies.

subject: Science
description: Analyze and interpret data from fossils to provide evidence of the organisms and the environments in which they lived long ago.
description_plain: Your child can study fossils to learn about animals and environments from long ago.

subject: Science
description: Use evidence to support the explanation that traits can be influenced by the environment.
description_plain: Your child can use evidence to show how the environment can change traits in living things.

subject: Science
description: Analyze and interpret data to provide evidence that plants and animals have traits inherited from their parents and that variation of these traits exists in a group of similar organisms.
description_plain: Your child can study data to show that plants and animals inherit traits from their parents and that these traits can vary.

subject: Science
description: Construct and support an argument that some animals form groups that help members survive.
description_plain: Your child can explain with reasons that some animals live in groups that help them survive.

subject: Science
description: Develop models to describe that organisms have unique and diverse life cycles but all have in common birth, growth, reproduction, and death.
description_plain: Your child can create models to show that all living things have life stages like birth, growth, reproduction, and death.

subject: Science
description: Define a simple design problem that can be solved by applying scientific ideas about magnets.
description_plain: Your child can describe a simple problem that can be solved using ideas about magnets.

subject: Science
description: Ask questions to determine cause and effect relationships of electric or magnetic interactions between two objects not in contact with each other.
description_plain: Your child can ask questions to find out how electric or magnetic forces affect objects without touching.

subject: Science
description: Make observations and/or measurements of an object's motion to provide evidence that a pattern can be used to predict future motion.
description_plain: Your child can watch and measure how things move to find patterns that help predict future motion.

subject: Science
description: Plan and conduct an investigation to provide evidence of the effects of balanced and unbalanced forces on the motion of an object.
description_plain: Your child can plan and do an experiment to show how balanced and unbalanced forces change how things move.

subject: Science
description: Gather, read, and synthesize information about technologies that have changed the way humans influence the inheritance of desired traits in organisms.
description_plain: Your child can gather and understand information about technologies that let people change inherited traits in organisms.

subject: Science
description: Construct an explanation based on evidence that describes how genetic variations of traits in a population increase some individuals' probability of surviving and reproducing in a specific environment.
description_plain: Your child can explain how differences in traits help some individuals survive and have babies better in certain environments.

subject: Science
description: Develop and use a model to describe why asexual reproduction results in offspring with identical genetic information and sexual reproduction results in offspring with genetic variation.
description_plain: Your child can use a model to show why asexual reproduction makes identical offspring and sexual reproduction makes offspring with different traits.

subject: Science
description: Construct an argument supported by empirical evidence that changes to physical or biological components of an ecosystem affect populations.
description_plain: Your child can explain, using real evidence, how changes in parts of an ecosystem affect the number of living things there.

subject: Science
description: Undertake a design project that assists in maintaining diversity and ecosystem services.
description_plain: Your child will create a project that helps protect different kinds of plants and animals and keeps ecosystems healthy.

subject: Science
description: Develop a model to describe how food is rearranged through chemical reactions forming new molecules that support growth and/or release energy as this matter moves through an organism.
description_plain: Your child can build a model showing how food changes inside living things to help them grow or give them energy.

subject: Science
description: Construct a scientific explanation based on evidence for the role of photosynthesis and cellular respiration in the cycling of matter and flow of energy into and out of organisms.
description_plain: Your child can explain, using evidence, how photosynthesis and cellular respiration help move matter and energy in and out of living things.

subject: Science
description: Use an argument supported by evidence for how the body is a system of interacting subsystems composed of groups of cells.
description_plain: Your child can explain, with evidence, how the body is made of different parts working together, all made of groups of cells.

subject: Science
description: Ask questions to clarify evidence of the factors that have caused the rise in global temperatures over the past century.
description_plain: Your child will ask questions to better understand what has caused Earth's temperatures to rise over the last 100 years.

subject: Science
description: Develop and use a model to describe how unequal heating and rotation of the Earth causes patterns of atmospheric and oceanic circulation that determine regional climates.
description_plain: Your child can make and use a model to show how the sun heating Earth unevenly and Earth's spinning cause weather and climate patterns.

subject: Science
description: Collect data to provide evidence for how the motions and complex interactions of air masses results in changes in weather conditions.
description_plain: Your child will collect information to show how moving air masses and their interactions change the weather.

subject: Science
description: Develop a model to describe the cycling of water through Earth's systems driven by energy from the sun and the force of gravity.
description_plain: Your child can create a model to explain how water moves through Earth's systems because of the sun's energy and gravity.

subject: Science
description: Plan an investigation to determine the relationships among the energy transferred, the type of matter, the mass, and the change in the average kinetic energy of the particles as measured by the temperature of the sample.
description_plain: Your child will plan an experiment to find out how energy changes with different materials, amounts, and temperatures.

subject: Science
description: Develop and use a model to describe how the total number of atoms does not change in a chemical reaction and thus mass is conserved.
description_plain: Your child can make and use a model to show that atoms stay the same in a chemical reaction, so mass doesn’t change.

subject: Science
description: Develop a model that predicts and describes changes in particle motion, temperature, and the state of a pure substance when thermal energy is added or removed.
description_plain: Your child can build a model to predict and explain how particles move, temperature changes, and states of matter change when heat is added or taken away.

subject: Science
description: Analyze and interpret data on the properties of substances before and after the substances interact to determine if a chemical reaction has occurred.
description_plain: Your child will study data about substances before and after they react to decide if a chemical change happened.

subject: Science
description: Develop a model to describe the cycling of matter and flow of energy among living and nonliving parts of an ecosystem.
description_plain: Your child can create a model to show how matter and energy move between living things and their environment.

subject: Science
description: Construct an explanation that predicts patterns of interactions among organisms across multiple ecosystems.
description_plain: Your child can explain and predict how different living things interact in various ecosystems.

subject: Science
description: Analyze and interpret data to provide evidence for the effects of resource availability on organisms and populations of organisms in an ecosystem.
description_plain: Your child will look at data to show how the availability of resources affects living things and their populations.

subject: Science
description: Develop and use a model to describe the function of a cell as a whole and ways parts of cells contribute to the function.
description_plain: Your child can make and use a model to explain how a cell works and how its parts help it function.

subject: Science
description: Conduct an investigation to provide evidence that living things are made of cells, either one or many different numbers and types.
description_plain: Your child will do an investigation to show that all living things are made of one or more cells.

subject: Science
description: Construct an argument supported by evidence for how increases in human population and per-capita consumption of natural resources impact Earth's systems.
description_plain: Your child can explain, using evidence, how growing human populations and using more resources affect Earth's systems.

subject: Science
description: Analyze and interpret data to determine scale properties of objects in the solar system.
description_plain: Your child will study data to understand the sizes and distances of objects in our solar system.

subject: Science
description: Use a model to describe the role of gravity in the motions within galaxies and the solar system.
description_plain: Your child can use a model to explain how gravity affects the movement of objects in galaxies and the solar system.

subject: Science
description: Develop and use a model of the Earth-sun-moon system to describe the reoccurring patterns of lunar phases, eclipses of the sun and moon, and seasons.
description_plain: Your child can build and use a model to describe how the Earth, sun, and moon cause phases of the moon, eclipses, and seasons.

subject: Science
description: Develop and use a model to describe that waves are refracted, reflected, absorbed, transmitted, or scattered through various materials.
description_plain: Your child can make and use a model to show how waves bend, bounce, get absorbed, pass through, or scatter when they meet different materials.

subject: Science
description: Use mathematical representations to describe a simple model for waves that includes how the amplitude of a wave is related to the energy in a wave and how the frequency and wavelength change the expression of the wave.
description_plain: Your child can use math to describe a simple wave model, showing how wave height relates to energy and how frequency and wavelength change the wave.

subject: Science
description: Develop a model to describe that when the arrangement of objects interacting at a distance changes, different amounts of potential energy are stored in the system.
description_plain: Your child can create a model to show how changing the positions of objects that affect each other from a distance changes stored energy.

subject: Science
description: Construct and interpret graphical displays of data to describe the relationships of kinetic energy to the mass of an object and to the speed of an object.
description_plain: Your child can make and read graphs to explain how an object's kinetic energy relates to its mass and speed.

subject: Science
description: Conduct an investigation and evaluate the experimental design to provide evidence that fields exist between objects exerting forces on each other even though the objects are not in contact.
description_plain: Your child will do an experiment and review its design to show that forces act between objects even when they don’t touch.

subject: Science
description: Construct and present arguments using evidence to support the claim that gravitational interactions are attractive and depend on the masses of interacting objects.
description_plain: Your child can explain, using evidence, that gravity pulls objects together and depends on how heavy the objects are.

subject: Science
description: Ask questions about data to determine the factors that affect the strength of electric and magnetic forces.
description_plain: Your child will ask questions about data to find out what affects how strong electric and magnetic forces are.

subject: Science
description: Plan an investigation to provide evidence that the change in an object's motion depends on the sum of the forces on the object and the mass of the object.
description_plain: Your child will plan an experiment to show that how an object moves changes based on the total forces on it and its mass.

subject: Science
description: Apply Newton's Third Law to design a solution to a problem involving the motion of two colliding objects.
description_plain: Your child can use Newton’s Third Law to design a way to solve a problem involving two objects hitting each other.

subject: Science
description: Develop models to describe the atomic composition of simple molecules and extended structures.
description_plain: Your child can create models to show what atoms make up simple molecules and larger structures.

subject: Science
description: Use mathematical representations to support explanations of how natural selection may lead to increases and decreases of specific traits in populations of species over time.
description_plain: Your child can use math to explain how natural selection can cause certain traits in species to increase or decrease over time.

subject: Science
description: Analyze displays of pictorial data to compare patterns of similarities in the embryological development across multiple species to identify relationships not evident in the fully formed anatomy.
description_plain: Your child can study pictures of embryos from different species to find similarities that show how they are related.

subject: Science
description: Apply scientific ideas to construct an explanation for the anatomical similarities and differences among modern organisms and between modern and fossil organisms to infer evolutionary relationships.
description_plain: Your child can explain how similarities and differences in body parts of living and fossil organisms show their evolutionary relationships.

subject: Science
description: Analyze and interpret data for patterns in the fossil record that document the existence, diversity, extinction, and change of life forms throughout the history of life on Earth under the assumption that natural laws operate today as in the past.
description_plain: Your child can study fossil data to find patterns that show how life on Earth has changed, including species that lived, went extinct, or evolved.

subject: Science
description: Develop and use a model to describe why structural changes to genes (mutations) located on chromosomes may affect proteins and may result in harmful, beneficial, or neutral effects to the structure and function of the organism.
description_plain: Your child can build and use a model to show how changes in genes can affect proteins and cause helpful, harmful, or no effects on an organism.

subject: Science
description: Construct a scientific explanation based on evidence for how environmental and genetic factors influence the growth of organisms.
description_plain: Your child can explain, using evidence, how both the environment and genes affect how organisms grow.

subject: Science
description: Construct and use argument(s) based on empirical evidence and scientific reasoning to support an explanation for how characteristic animal behaviors and specialized plant structures affect the probability of survival and successful reproduction of animals and plants respectively.
description_plain: Your child can use evidence and reasoning to explain how animal behaviors and special plant parts help them survive and reproduce.

subject: Science
description: Apply scientific principles to design a method for monitoring and minimizing human impact on the environment.
description_plain: Your child can use science ideas to design a way to watch and reduce how humans harm the environment.

subject: Science
description: Analyze and interpret data on natural hazards to forecast future catastrophic events and inform the development of technologies to mitigate their effects.
description_plain: Your child can look at information about natural disasters to predict future events and help create ways to reduce their damage.

subject: Science
description: Construct a scientific explanation based on evidence for how the uneven distributions of Earth's mineral, energy, and groundwater resources are the result of past and current geoscience processes.
description_plain: Your child will explain how Earth's minerals, energy, and water are spread unevenly because of past and present Earth processes.

subject: Science
description: Analyze and interpret data on the distribution of fossils and rocks, continental shapes, and sea floor structures to provide evidence of the past plate motions.
description_plain: Your child can study fossils, rocks, continents, and ocean floors to find proof of how Earth's plates have moved in the past.

subject: Science
description: Construct an explanation based on evidence for how geoscience processes have changed Earth's surface at varying time and spatial scales.
description_plain: Your child will explain how Earth's surface has changed over time and in different places because of natural Earth processes.

subject: Science
description: Develop a model to describe the cycling of Earth's materials and the flow of energy that drives this process.
description_plain: Your child can create a model to show how Earth's materials cycle and how energy moves through this process.

subject: Science
description: Construct a scientific explanation based on evidence from rock strata for how the geologic time scale is used to organize Earth's geologic history.
description_plain: Your child will explain how scientists use layers of rock to organize Earth's history over time.

subject: Science
description: Construct, use, and present arguments to support the claim that when the kinetic energy of an object changes, energy is transferred to or from the object.
description_plain: Your child can explain and share reasons why when an object's movement energy changes, energy moves to or from that object.

subject: Science
description: Apply scientific principles to design, construct, and test a device that either minimizes or maximizes thermal energy transfer
description_plain: Your child will design, build, and test something that either keeps heat in or lets heat out.

subject: Science
description: Undertake a design project to construct, test, and modify a device that either releases or absorbs thermal energy by chemical processes.
description_plain: Your child can create and improve a device that uses chemical reactions to give off or take in heat.

description: Gather and make sense of information to describe that synthetic materials come from natural resources and impact society.
description_plain: Your child will gather information to explain that man-made materials come from natural resources and affect our lives.

subject: Science
description: Develop models to describe the atomic composition of simple molecules and extended structures.
description_plain: Your child can make models to show what atoms make up simple molecules and larger structures.
