subject: Social Studies
description: Describe the influence of geographic features on the origins of the Mayan civilization and explain theories related to the abandonment of their cities.
description_plain: Your child can explain how land and geography affected where the Mayan civilization started and why their cities were abandoned.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain the major accomplishments of the Mayans, including advancements in astronomy, mathematics and the calendar, construction of pyramids, temples, and hieroglyphic writing.
description_plain: Your child knows about the Mayans’ important achievements like their work in astronomy, math, calendars, building pyramids and temples, and their writing system.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe key characteristics of Poverty Point culture, including art, hunting methods, dress, food, use of mounds, and resources traded there.
description_plain: Your child can describe the Poverty Point culture, including their art, hunting, clothing, food, use of mounds, and trading.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain how people living in what would become Louisiana gradually moved towards seasonal hunting and gathering, using new tools and practices for hunting, and building large mounds for ceremonial and practical purposes.
description_plain: Your child understands how people in early Louisiana started hunting seasonally, used new tools, and built large mounds for ceremonies and daily life.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain how nomadic groups of people first hunted and traveled throughout what would become Louisiana.
description_plain: Your child can explain how early groups of people hunted and moved around in what is now Louisiana.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe the cultural elements among Indigenous communities in the Americas, including housing, clothing, games/entertainment, dance, and how food was gathered/caught and cooked.
description_plain: Your child knows about the homes, clothing, games, dances, and food gathering of Indigenous peoples in the Americas.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify and locate geographic features in the Americas, including Mississippi River and Delta, Amazon River, the Pacific Ocean, Appalachian Mountains, Gulf of Mexico, Atlantic Ocean, South America, and the Yucatan Peninsula.
description_plain: Your child can find and name important places in the Americas like the Mississippi River, Amazon River, Pacific Ocean, Appalachian Mountains, Gulf of Mexico, Atlantic Ocean, South America, and Yucatan Peninsula.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe the geographic, political, economic, and cultural structures of Indigenous civilizations of the Americas.
description_plain: Your child understands the land, government, economy, and culture of Indigenous civilizations in the Americas.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe how the desire for Chinese goods influenced the creation of The Silk Road and began a process of cultural diffusion throughout Eurasia.
description_plain: Your child can explain how wanting Chinese goods led to the creation of the Silk Road and helped spread cultures across Eurasia.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain the major accomplishments of the Han Dynasty, including the magnetic compass, paper making, porcelain, silk, and woodblock printing.
description_plain: Your child knows about important inventions from the Han Dynasty like the compass, paper, porcelain, silk, and printing.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe how the size of ancient China made governing difficult and how early dynasties attempted to solve this problem, including the construction of the Grand Canal and the Great Wall.
description_plain: Your child understands how ancient China’s large size made it hard to govern and how building the Grand Canal and Great Wall helped solve problems.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain the significance of the unification of ancient China into the first Chinese empire by Qin Shi Huangdi.
description_plain: Your child can explain why it was important when Qin Shi Huangdi united ancient China into its first empire.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe problems prevalent in the time of Confucius and explain the concepts of filial piety (dutiful respect) and the Mandate of Heaven.
description_plain: Your child knows about problems during Confucius’s time and understands ideas like respect for parents and the Mandate of Heaven.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe the influence of geographic features on the origins of ancient Chinese civilization in the Yellow River Valley, and explain how China's geography helped create a unique cultural identity.
description_plain: Your child can explain how China’s geography, especially the Yellow River Valley, helped shape its early civilization and culture.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify and locate geographic features of ancient China, including the Gobi Desert, Plateau of Tibet, Himalayan Mountains, Yangtze River, Pacific Ocean, and the Yellow River.
description_plain: Your child can find and name important places in ancient China like the Gobi Desert, Plateau of Tibet, Himalayan Mountains, Yangtze River, Pacific Ocean, and Yellow River.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe the geographic, political, economic, and cultural structures of ancient China.
description_plain: Your child understands the land, government, economy, and culture of ancient China.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe the fall of the Western Roman Empire, including difficulty governing its large territory and political, military, and economic problems.
description_plain: Your child can describe why the Western Roman Empire fell, including problems with ruling a large area and political, military, and money issues.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe how innovations in engineering and architecture contributed to Roman expansion, including the role of: aqueducts, domes, arches, roads, bridges, and sanitation.
description_plain: Your child knows how Roman inventions like aqueducts, domes, arches, roads, bridges, and sanitation helped the empire grow.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain the influence of Augustus Caesar, including the establishment of the Roman Empire and its expansion during the Pax Romana.
description_plain: Your child can explain how Augustus Caesar helped start the Roman Empire and expand it during a peaceful time called the Pax Romana.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe the characteristics of Julius Caesar's rule, including his role as dictator for life.
description_plain: Your child understands Julius Caesar’s rule and his role as a lifelong dictator.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe the polytheistic religion of ancient Rome and its connection to ancient Greek beliefs.
description_plain: Your child knows about the many gods of ancient Rome and how their religion was connected to ancient Greek beliefs.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe the class system of ancient Rome, including the roles and rights of patricians, plebeians, and enslaved people in Roman society.
description_plain: Your child can describe the social classes in ancient Rome and the roles of rich people, common people, and enslaved people.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain how the geographic location of ancient Rome contributed to its political and economic growth in the Mediterranean region and beyond.
description_plain: Your child understands how Rome’s location helped it grow politically and economically in the Mediterranean and beyond.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify and locate the geographic features of ancient Rome, including the Mediterranean Sea, Italian Alps, Rome, Italian Peninsula, and the Tiber River.
description_plain: Your child can find and name important places in ancient Rome like the Mediterranean Sea, Italian Alps, Rome city, Italian Peninsula, and Tiber River.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe the geographic, political, economic, and cultural structures of ancient Rome.
description_plain: Your child understands the land, government, economy, and culture of ancient Rome.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify Alexander the Great and explain how his conquests spread Hellenistic (Greek) culture.
description_plain: Your child knows who Alexander the Great was and how his conquests spread Greek culture.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify examples of ancient Greek architecture, including the Parthenon and the Acropolis.
description_plain: Your child can identify famous Greek buildings like the Parthenon and the Acropolis.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify Socrates, Plato, and Aristotle as great philosophers of ancient Greece and explain how ideas can spread through writing and teaching.
description_plain: Your child knows about Greek philosophers Socrates, Plato, and Aristotle and how ideas spread through writing and teaching.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe the polytheistic religion of ancient Greece.
description_plain: Your child understands the many gods worshiped in ancient Greece.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe the causes and consequences of the Persian Wars, including the role of Athens and its cooperation with Sparta.
description_plain: Your child can explain why the Persian Wars happened and how Athens and Sparta worked together.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain the characteristics of the major Greek city-states of Athens and Sparta, including status of women, approaches to education, type of government, and the practice of slavery.
description_plain: Your child knows about Athens and Sparta, including women’s roles, education, government, and slavery.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain the basic concepts of direct democracy and oligarchy.
description_plain: Your child understands the basics of direct democracy and rule by a few leaders (oligarchy).

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe the concept of the polis in Greek city-states, including the ideas of citizenship, civic participation, and the rule of law.
description_plain: Your child can explain what a polis is and what it meant to be a citizen, take part in government, and follow laws in Greek city-states.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe how the geographic features of ancient Greece, including its mountainous terrain and access to the Mediterranean Sea contributed to its organization into city-states and the development of maritime trade.
description_plain: Your child can explain how Greece's mountains and location by the Mediterranean Sea led to separate city-states and helped them trade by sea.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify and locate geographic features of ancient Greece, including the Mediterranean Sea, Athens, the Peloponnesian peninsula, and Sparta.
description_plain: Your child can find and name important places in ancient Greece like the Mediterranean Sea, Athens, the Peloponnesian peninsula, and Sparta.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe the geographic, political, economic, and cultural structures of ancient Greece.
description_plain: Your child understands the land, government, economy, and culture of ancient Greece.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify the long-lasting intellectual traditions that emerged during the late empire of ancient India, including advances in medicine and Hindu-Arabic numerals.
description_plain: Your child can name important ideas from ancient India, like advances in medicine and the number system we use today.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain the emergence of civilization in the Indus River Valley as an early agricultural civilization and describe its achievements, including architecture built with bricks, roads arranged into a series of grid systems, and sewer systems.
description_plain: Your child can explain how people in the Indus River Valley started farming and built things like brick buildings, straight roads, and sewer systems.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify and locate geographic features of ancient India, including the Ganges River, Indus River, Himalayan Mountains, Indian Ocean, and the subcontinent of India.
description_plain: Your child can find and name important places in ancient India like the Ganges River, Indus River, Himalayan Mountains, Indian Ocean, and the Indian subcontinent.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe the geographic, political, economic, and cultural structures of ancient India.
description_plain: Your child understands the land, government, economy, and culture of ancient India.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe the cultural diffusion of ancient Egypt with surrounding civilizations through trade and conflict.
description_plain: Your child can explain how ancient Egypt shared ideas and goods with nearby civilizations through trade and conflicts.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe the achievements of ancient Egyptian civilization, including hieroglyphics, papyrus, and the pyramids and Sphinx at Giza.
description_plain: Your child can describe important Egyptian achievements like their writing system, papyrus paper, and famous buildings like the pyramids and Sphinx.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe the significance of key figures from ancient Egypt, including Queen Hatshepsut, Ramses the Great, and the significance of the discovery of Tutankhamun's tomb on the modern understanding of ancient Egypt.
description_plain: Your child knows about important Egyptian leaders like Queen Hatshepsut and Ramses the Great, and why finding King Tut’s tomb helped us learn about Egypt.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain Egyptian beliefs about the afterlife, the reasons for mummification, and the use of pyramids.
description_plain: Your child understands Egyptian beliefs about life after death, why they mummified bodies, and why they built pyramids.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain the structure of ancient Egyptian society, including the relationships between groups of people and the role played by the pharaoh and enslaved people.
description_plain: Your child can explain how ancient Egyptian society was organized, including the roles of the pharaoh and enslaved people.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify and locate geographic features of ancient Egypt, including the Mediterranean Sea, Red Sea, Nile River and Delta, and the Sahara Desert.
description_plain: Your child can find and name important places in ancient Egypt like the Mediterranean Sea, Red Sea, Nile River and Delta, and the Sahara Desert.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe the geographic, political, economic, and cultural structures of ancient Egypt.
description_plain: Your child understands the land, government, economy, and culture of ancient Egypt.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe the achievements of the ancient Israelites.
description_plain: Your child can describe important achievements of the ancient Israelites.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe the significance of the written law in the Code of Hammurabi, and explain the meaning of the phrase "an eye for an eye and a tooth for a tooth."
description_plain: Your child understands the importance of the Code of Hammurabi’s laws and what 'an eye for an eye' means.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify important achievements of the Mesopotamian civilization, including cuneiform, clay tablets, ziggurats, and the Epic of Gilgamesh as the oldest written epic.
description_plain: Your child can name important Mesopotamian achievements like their writing, clay tablets, temples called ziggurats, and the oldest epic story, the Epic of Gilgamesh.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe how changes in agriculture in Sumer led to economic growth, expansion of trade and transportation, and the growth of independent city-states.
description_plain: Your child can explain how better farming in Sumer helped their economy grow, increased trade, and led to the creation of city-states.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain how irrigation, silt, metallurgy, production of tools, use of animals and inventions, such as the wheel and plow, led to advancements in agriculture.
description_plain: Your child understands how inventions like irrigation, tools, the wheel, and plow improved farming.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain how geographic and climatic features led to the region being known as the Fertile Crescent.
description_plain: Your child can explain why the area called the Fertile Crescent had good land and climate for farming.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify and locate geographic features of the ancient Near East, including the Black Sea, Persian Gulf, Euphrates River, Tigris River, Mediterranean Sea, and Zagros Mountains.
description_plain: Your child can find and name important places in the ancient Near East like the Black Sea, Persian Gulf, Euphrates River, Tigris River, Mediterranean Sea, and Zagros Mountains.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe the geographic, political, economic, and cultural structures of the ancient Near East.
description_plain: Your child understands the land, government, economy, and culture of the ancient Near East.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify and explain the importance of the following key characteristics of civilizations: culture, specialization, infrastructure, stable food supply, government, technology, belief systems, writing, and social structure.
description_plain: Your child can identify and explain important parts of civilizations like culture, jobs, buildings, food, government, technology, beliefs, writing, and social groups.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain the effects of the Agricultural Revolution, including the barter economy, food surpluses, domestication of plants and animals, specialization, and the growth of permanent settlements.
description_plain: Your child can explain how farming changed life by creating food surpluses, trade, specialized jobs, and permanent homes.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe early human migration out of Africa, first to Europe and Asia, then to the Americas and Australia.
description_plain: Your child can describe how early humans moved from Africa to Europe, Asia, the Americas, and Australia.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe the characteristics of nomadic hunter-gatherer societies, including their use of hunting weapons, fire, shelter and tools.
description_plain: Your child understands how nomadic hunter-gatherers lived using hunting tools, fire, shelters, and other tools.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe the origin and spread of major world religions as they developed throughout history.
description_plain: Your child can describe how major world religions started and spread over time.

subject: Social Studies
description: Use geographic representations and historical information to explain how physical geography influenced the development of ancient civilizations and empires.
description_plain: Your child can use maps and history to explain how land and geography affected ancient civilizations and empires.

subject: Social Studies
description: Create and use geographic representations to locate and describe places and geographic characteristics, including hemispheres; landforms such as continents, oceans, rivers, mountains, and deserts; cardinal and intermediate directions; climate and environment.
description_plain: Your child can make and use maps to find and describe places, landforms, directions, climate, and environments.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe the development of the transatlantic slave trade and the experiences of enslaved people in the Americas.
description_plain: Your child can describe how the transatlantic slave trade developed and what life was like for enslaved people in the Americas.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain how Spanish colonization introduced Christianity, the mission system, and the encomienda system to the Americas as well as the transition to African slavery.
description_plain: Your child can explain how Spanish colonization brought Christianity, missions, and the encomienda system to the Americas, and how African slavery began there.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain the impact of the Columbian Exchange on people, plants, animals, technology, culture, ideas, and diseases among Europe, Africa, Asia, and the Americas in the fifteenth and sixteenth centuries, and examine the major effects on each continent.
description_plain: Your child can explain how the Columbian Exchange changed people, plants, animals, technology, culture, ideas, and diseases between Europe, Africa, Asia, and the Americas in the 1400s and 1500s, and what effects these changes had on each continent.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe how the Aztec and Inca empires were eventually defeated by Spanish conquistadors.
description_plain: Your child will describe how Spanish explorers defeated the Aztec and Inca empires.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe Prince Henry the Navigator's influence on exploration, voyages, cartographic improvements, and tools related to exploration, including the compass, caravel, and astrolabe.
description_plain: Your child understands how Prince Henry the Navigator helped exploration by improving maps and tools like the compass, caravel ships, and astrolabe.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify the significance of the voyages and routes of discovery of the following explorers by their sponsoring country: England: Henry Hudson; France: Jacques Cartier; Portugal: Vasco da Gama, Bartolomeu Dias; Spain: Christopher Columbus, Hernando de Soto, Ferdinand Magellan, and Amerigo Vespucci.
description_plain: Your child can name important explorers from England, France, Portugal, and Spain and explain the routes they took and which countries sent them.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze why European countries were motivated to explore the world, including religion, political rivalry, and economic gain.
description_plain: Your child understands why European countries wanted to explore the world, including reasons like religion, competition, and making money.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the motivations for the movement of people from Europe to the Americas and describe the effects of exploration by Europeans.
description_plain: Your child can explain why people moved from Europe to the Americas and what happened because of European exploration.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain how the Inca kept their empire together without a written language.
description_plain: Your child understands how the Inca kept their empire united even though they did not have a written language.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain how the Inca built and organized their empire and how Inca engineers overcame challenges presented by the geography of the land.
description_plain: Your child can explain how the Inca built and organized their empire and how their engineers solved problems caused by the land's geography.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify Moctezuma II and describe features of his reign.
description_plain: Your child can identify Moctezuma II and describe important parts of his rule.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe Tenochtitlán and the surrounding landscape, including aqueducts, massive temples, and chinampa agriculture.
description_plain: Your child will describe the city of Tenochtitlán and its surroundings, including its aqueducts, large temples, and farming on chinampas.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe Aztec religious beliefs and how they were linked to the traditions of the society.
description_plain: Your child understands the Aztec religious beliefs and how these beliefs were connected to their society's traditions.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain how the Aztec built and controlled a powerful empire that covered much of what is now central Mexico.
description_plain: Your child can explain how the Aztec built and controlled a strong empire in what is now central Mexico.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain the effects of geographic features and climate on the agricultural practices and settlement of the Aztec and Incan civilizations.
description_plain: Your child understands how geography and climate affected farming and where the Aztec and Inca civilizations lived.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe the existence of diverse networks of Indigenous North American cultures, including varied languages, customs, and economic and political structures.
description_plain: Your child can describe the many different Native American cultures in North America, including their languages, customs, and ways of governing and trading.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain the effects of geographic features on Indigenous North American cultures (Northeast, Southeast, and Plains), including clothing, housing, and agriculture.
description_plain: Your child understands how geography affected the clothing, homes, and farming of Native American groups in the Northeast, Southeast, and Plains regions.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify and locate the geographic features of the Americas, including the Andes Mountains, Appalachian Mountains, Great Plains, Pacific Ocean Mountains, Gulf of Mexico, Rocky Mountains, Atlantic Ocean, Mississippi River, Amazon River, South America, Caribbean Sea, North America, Yucatan Peninsula, and the Central Mexican Plateau.
description_plain: Your child can find and name important geographic features in the Americas, like mountains, rivers, oceans, and regions.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe the geographic, political, economic, and cultural structures of Indigenous civilizations of the Americas.
description_plain: Your child will describe the geography, government, economy, and culture of Native American civilizations in the Americas.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe Galileo Galilei's theories and improvement of scientific tools, including the telescope and microscope.
description_plain: Your child can describe how Galileo improved scientific tools like the telescope and microscope and his ideas about science.

subject: Social Studies
description: Compare and contrast heliocentric and geocentric theories of the Greeks (geocentric) and Copernicus (heliocentric).
description_plain: Your child understands the difference between the old idea that Earth was the center of the universe and the new idea that the sun is the center.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain the significant causes of the Protestant Reformation, including the selling of indulgences and Martin Luther's 95 Theses.
description_plain: Your child can explain what caused the Protestant Reformation, including the selling of indulgences and Martin Luther's 95 Theses.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain how Johannes Gutenberg's printing press affected the growth of literacy and diffusion of knowledge.
description_plain: Your child understands how Johannes Gutenberg's printing press helped more people learn to read and spread new ideas.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain the development of Renaissance art, including the significance of Leonardo da Vinci, Michelangelo, William Shakespeare, and systems of patronage.
description_plain: Your child can describe Renaissance art and important artists like Leonardo da Vinci, Michelangelo, and Shakespeare, and how wealthy people supported artists.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify the importance of Florence, Italy and the Medici Family in the early stages of the Renaissance.
description_plain: Your child understands why Florence, Italy, and the Medici family were important at the start of the Renaissance.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain how the location of the Italian Peninsula affected the movement of resources, knowledge, and culture throughout Italy's independent trade cities.
description_plain: Your child can explain how Italy's location helped trade, sharing knowledge, and culture between its cities.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe the origins, accomplishments, and geographic diffusion of the Renaissance as well as the historical developments of the Protestant Reformation and Scientific Revolution.
description_plain: Your child will describe where the Renaissance started, what it achieved, how it spread, and learn about the Protestant Reformation and Scientific Revolution.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain the importance of the Malian king Mansa Musa and his pilgrimage to Mecca.
description_plain: Your child understands why the Malian king Mansa Musa was important and what happened during his trip to Mecca.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe the role of the Trans-Saharan caravan trade in the changing religious and cultural characteristics of West Africa and in the exchange of salt, gold, and enslaved people.
description_plain: Your child can describe how trade across the Sahara Desert changed West Africa's religion, culture, and economy, including trading salt, gold, and enslaved people.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe the growth of the kingdoms of Ghana, Mali, and Songhai, including cities such as Djenne and Timbuktu as centers of trade, culture, and learning.
description_plain: Your child understands how the kingdoms of Ghana, Mali, and Songhai grew and how cities like Djenne and Timbuktu became centers for trade, culture, and learning.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify and locate the geographic features of West Africa, including the Atlantic Ocean, Niger River, Djenne, the Sahara, Gulf of Guinea, and Timbuktu.
description_plain: Your child can find and name important geographic features in West Africa, like the Atlantic Ocean, Niger River, Sahara Desert, and cities like Timbuktu.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe the geographic, political, economic, and cultural structures of Medieval West African Kingdoms.
description_plain: Your child will describe the geography, government, economy, and culture of Medieval West African kingdoms.

subject: Social Studies
description: Summarize the contributions of Islamic scholars in the areas of art, medicine, science, and mathematics.
description_plain: Your child can summarize how Islamic scholars contributed to art, medicine, science, and math.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe the diffusion of Islam, its culture, and the Arabic language throughout North Africa and Southwest Asia.
description_plain: Your child understands how Islam, its culture, and the Arabic language spread across North Africa and Southwest Asia.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify and locate the geographic features of Southwest Asia and North Africa, including the Arabian Peninsula, the Persian Gulf, Arabian Sea, Red Sea, Black Sea, and the Caspian Sea.
description_plain: Your child can find and name important geographic features in Southwest Asia and North Africa, like the Arabian Peninsula and nearby seas.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe the geographic, political, economic, and cultural structures of Southwest Asia and North Africa.
description_plain: Your child will describe the geography, government, economy, and culture of Southwest Asia and North Africa.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe the significance of the Hundred Years' War, including the roles of Henry V in shaping English culture and language and Joan of Arc in promoting a peaceful end to the war.
description_plain: Your child can explain why the Hundred Years' War was important and the roles of Henry V and Joan of Arc in it.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe the economic and social effects of the spread of the Black Death (Bubonic Plague) from Central Asia to China, the Middle East, and Europe, and its effect on the global population.
description_plain: Your child understands how the Black Death spread from Central Asia to other parts of the world and how it affected people and populations.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain how the Crusades affected Christian, Muslim, and Jewish populations in Europe.
description_plain: Your child can explain how the Crusades affected Christians, Muslims, and Jews in Europe.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe the significance of the Magna Carta, including limiting the power of the monarch, the rule of law, and the right to trial by jury.
description_plain: Your child understands why the Magna Carta was important for limiting the king's power and giving people rights like fair trials.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe the development of feudalism and manorialism and their role in the medieval European economy.
description_plain: Your child can describe how feudalism and manorialism developed and how they shaped the medieval European economy.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain how Charlemagne shaped and defined medieval Europe, including the creation of the Holy Roman Empire, and the establishment of Christianity as the religion of the Empire.
description_plain: Your child understands how Charlemagne shaped medieval Europe by creating the Holy Roman Empire and making Christianity the empire's religion.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe the role of monasteries in the preservation of knowledge and the spread of the Catholic Church throughout Europe.
description_plain: Your child can explain how monasteries helped save important knowledge and helped spread the Catholic Church across Europe.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify and locate geographic features of Europe, including the Alps, Atlantic Ocean, North European Plain, English Channel, Ural Mountains and the Mediterranean Sea.
description_plain: Your child can find and name important places in Europe like the Alps, Atlantic Ocean, and Mediterranean Sea on a map.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe the geographic, political, economic, and cultural structures of Europe during the Middle Ages.
description_plain: Your child understands what Europe was like during the Middle Ages, including its land, government, economy, and culture.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe the origin and spread of major world religions as they developed throughout history.
description_plain: Your child can tell how major world religions started and spread over time.

subject: Social Studies
description: Use geographic representations and historical information to explain how physical geography influenced the development of civilizations and empires.
description_plain: Your child can use maps and history to show how land features affected how civilizations and empires grew.

subject: Social Studies
description: Create and use geographic representations to locate and describe places and geographic characteristics, including hemispheres; landforms such as continents, oceans, rivers, mountains, deserts; cardinal and intermediate directions; latitude and longitude, climate, and environment.
description_plain: Your child can make and use maps to find and describe places, landforms, directions, climate, and environments.

description_plain: Your child can compare and explain different events and changes in world history.

description: Explain connections between ideas, events, and developments in world history.
description_plain: Your child can explain how ideas and events in world history are connected.

description_plain: Your child can put events in order and explain how things changed or stayed the same over time.

description_plain: Your child understands and uses important words and phrases related to social studies.

description_plain: Your child can figure out where information comes from, who wrote it, and who it was meant for.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain the structure, powers, and functions of the branches of the United States federal government (legislative, executive, and judicial), and describe the qualifications, roles, and responsibilities of elected and appointed government officials.
description_plain: Your child can explain how the three parts of the U.S. government work and what elected and appointed leaders do.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain the structure and processes of the United States government as outlined in the Constitution of the United States, including the branches of government, how a bill becomes a law at the federal level, and the process for amending the United States Constitution.
description_plain: Your child can explain how the U.S. government is set up by the Constitution, how a bill becomes a law, and how the Constitution can be changed.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the key principles of government established by the Constitution of the United States, including federalism (enumerated, reserved, and concurrent powers), individual rights, judicial review, limited government, popular sovereignty and consent of the governed, rule of law, separation of powers, and a system of checks and balances.
description_plain: Your child understands important ideas in the Constitution like sharing power, protecting rights, and how the government checks itself.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain how and why the Constitution of the United States was amended to include the Bill of Rights, and analyze the guarantees of civil rights and individual liberties protected in each of the first ten amendments.
description_plain: Your child can explain why the Bill of Rights was added to the Constitution and what rights it protects.

subject: Social Studies
description: Evaluate the arguments of Federalists and Anti-Federalists on the ratification of the Constitution expressed in the Federalist Papers and the writings of the Anti-Federalists.
description_plain: Your child can explain the arguments for and against the Constitution from people called Federalists and Anti-Federalists.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain the significance of the Commerce Clause, including its role in establishing a constitutional relationship between Native Americans and the U.S. government.
description_plain: Your child can explain why the Commerce Clause is important, including how it affects the relationship between Native Americans and the U.S. government.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain the importance of ideas expressed in the Preamble to the Constitution of the United States, including the purpose and responsibilities of government and the concept of self-government.
description_plain: Your child understands the main ideas in the Preamble of the Constitution about the government's purpose and self-government.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain how the ideas of leading figures and Founding Fathers contributed to the Constitutional Convention and development of the U.S. government, including John Adams, Benjamin Franklin, Alexander Hamilton, Thomas Jefferson, James Madison, George Mason, William Patterson, Roger Sherman, George Washington, and James Wilson.
description_plain: Your child can explain how important leaders helped create the U.S. government and the Constitution.

subject: Social Studies
description: Evaluate the major issues debated at the Constitutional Convention, including the key characteristics and features of the Articles of Confederation, the division and sharing of power between the federal and state governments (federal system), the Great Compromise, and slavery (Three-Fifths Compromise).
description_plain: Your child can explain big issues debated when the Constitution was made, like how power is shared and compromises about slavery.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain the ideas and events leading to the ratification of the Constitution of the United States, including inadequacies of the Articles of Confederation and Shays Rebellion.
description_plain: Your child can explain what led to the Constitution being approved, including problems with the Articles of Confederation and events like Shays' Rebellion.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe the development of various state Constitutions, and the effects of early abolitionists on the development of state government, including Olaudah Equiano, Benjamin Banneker, and Elizabeth Freeman.
description_plain: Your child can describe how early state governments were made and how early abolitionists helped shape them.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain the purpose and importance of the Articles of Confederation.
description_plain: Your child understands what the Articles of Confederation were and why they were important.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the development of the U.S. political system through the ratification of the U.S. Constitution.
description_plain: Your child can explain how the U.S. political system grew and changed when the Constitution was created.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain the role of espionage during the American Revolution, including the actions of spies for the colonies (Nathan Hale, Culper Spy Ring, John Clark, Enoch Crosby, Nancy Hart, and James Armistead Lafayette) and spies for Britain (Benedict Arnold).
description_plain: Your child can explain how spies helped during the American Revolution for both the colonies and Britain.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain the role of Spain and Spanish colonial Louisiana during the American Revolution and effects of the conflict on the colony, including the roles of Bernardo de Galvez, Battle of Lake Pontchartrain (1779), and Battle of Baton Rouge (1779).
description_plain: Your child can explain Spain's role and events in Spanish Louisiana during the American Revolution.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain the contributions of women to the American Revolution, including those of Abigail Adams, Mercy Otis-Warren, Mary Ludwig Hays, Deborah Sampson, Phillis Wheatly, and Betsy Ross.
description_plain: Your child can explain how women helped during the American Revolution and name some important women.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain the significance and outcome of key battles and turning points during the American Revolution, including the Battles of Lexington and Concord (1775), creation of the Continental Army and appointment of George Washington as Commander in Chief (1775), Battles of Trenton and Princeton (1776–1777), Battle of Saratoga (1777), encampment at Valley Forge (1777–1778), Franco-American alliance (1778), Battle of Yorktown (1781), and the Treaty of Paris of 1783.
description_plain: Your child can explain important battles and events that changed the course of the American Revolution.

subject: Social Studies
description: Compare and contrast the American colonies and British in the American Revolution, including leadership, military power, recruitment, alliances, population, and resources, and evaluate their strengths and weaknesses.
description_plain: Your child can compare the American colonies and Britain during the Revolution, including their leaders, armies, and resources.

subject: Social Studies
description: Compare and contrast viewpoints of Loyalists and Patriots, and evaluate their arguments for and against independence from Britain.
description_plain: Your child can explain the different views of Loyalists and Patriots about independence from Britain.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain efforts to mobilize support for the American Revolution by individuals and groups, including the Minutemen and Committees of Correspondence and Sons of Liberty (Samuel Adams, John Hancock, Paul Revere).
description_plain: Your child can explain how groups and people like the Minutemen and Sons of Liberty helped support the American Revolution.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain how key ideas expressed in historical works influenced the American Revolution, including "taxation without representation is tyranny" (John Otis), John Dickinson's Letters from a Farmer in Pennsylvania, Patrick Henry's "Give Me Liberty or Give Me Death" speech, Thomas Paine's Common Sense, and the Declaration of Independence: "all men are created equal, … endowed by their Creator with certain unalienable rights, … among these are life, liberty, and the pursuit of Happiness," and "the consent of the governed."
description_plain: Your child can explain how important writings and speeches inspired the American Revolution.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the role and importance of key events and developments leading to the American Revolution, including end of Salutary Neglect by King George III, French and Indian War, Proclamation of 1763, Acts of 1764–1773 (Sugar Act, Stamp Act, Quartering Act, Townshend Acts, Tea Act), Boston Massacre and the death of Crispus Attucks, Boston Tea Party, Coercive (Intolerable) Acts, First Continental Congress, Restraining Acts, the seizure of firearms, and Second Continental Congress.
description_plain: Your child can explain key events and laws that led to the American Revolution.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain the causes and effects of the French and Indian War.
description_plain: Your child can explain what caused the French and Indian War and what happened because of it.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the historical and religious factors that influenced the development of government in the United States, including those from ancient Greece; the Roman Republic; the Judeo-Christian tradition; English rule of law and the Magna Carta; Enlightenment philosophies; and the Great Awakening.
description_plain: Your child can explain how history and religion influenced how the U.S. government was formed.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the causes, course, and consequences of the American Revolution.
description_plain: Your child can explain what caused the American Revolution, what happened during it, and what effects it had.

subject: Social Studies
description: Compare and contrast French and Spanish colonial Louisiana and colonial Louisiana and British colonies.
description_plain: Your child can tell how French and Spanish colonial Louisiana were alike and different, and also compare colonial Louisiana to the British colonies.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe the contributions and achievements of Gens de Couleur Libres in colonial Louisiana.
description_plain: Your child understands the important roles and achievements of free people of color in colonial Louisiana.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain the influence of France and Spain on government in Louisiana, with an emphasis on the Napoleonic Code, the Code Noir, and the contributions of Jean-Baptiste Le Moyne de Bienville, John Law, King Louis XIV, and Alejandro O'Reilly.
description_plain: Your child can explain how France and Spain influenced Louisiana’s government, including laws like the Napoleonic Code and Code Noir, and the work of important leaders.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe the characteristics of colonial Louisiana, including physical geography, climate, economic activities, culture and customs, and government, and analyze their importance to the growth and development of Louisiana.
description_plain: Your child describes what colonial Louisiana was like, including its land, weather, jobs, culture, and government, and why these things helped it grow.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe the factors that influenced migration within and to Louisiana by various groups, including French, Spanish, Africans, Acadians, Germans, Canary Islanders/Islenos, and Haitians, and explain how individuals and groups interacted and contributed to the development of Louisiana.
description_plain: Your child can explain why different groups like the French, Spanish, Africans, and others moved to Louisiana and how they worked together to build the state.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain the significance of events that influenced pre-colonial and colonial Louisiana, including the founding of Natchitoches and New Orleans, the Treaty of Fontainebleau, and the Third Treaty of San Ildefonso.
description_plain: Your child understands why important events like the founding of Natchitoches and New Orleans and certain treaties mattered for Louisiana’s early history.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the growth and development of colonial Louisiana.
description_plain: Your child analyzes how colonial Louisiana grew and developed over time.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze cooperation, competition, and conflict among groups in North America from the late 1500s to the mid-1700s, including Dutch, English, French, Spanish, and Native Americans including the 1621 Autumn Harvest Celebration, French and Native American trade of fur, Bacon's Rebellion, and King Philip's (Metacom) War.
description_plain: Your child can explain how different groups in North America, like the Dutch, English, French, Spanish, and Native Americans, worked together, competed, or fought from the late 1500s to mid-1700s.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain the experiences and perspectives of various people groups living in colonial North America, including large landowners, farmers, artisans, women, children, indentured servants, enslaved people, and Native Americans.
description_plain: Your child understands what life was like for different people in colonial North America, including landowners, farmers, women, children, servants, enslaved people, and Native Americans.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the causes, interactions, and consequences related to triangular trade, including the forced migration of Africans through the transatlantic trade of enslaved people and experiences of the Middle Passage.
description_plain: Your child can explain the causes and effects of the triangular trade, including the forced movement of Africans and the hardships of the Middle Passage.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify the locations of the colonies and lands inhabited by Native Americans, and explain how location, environment, and resources affected changes and development over time.
description_plain: Your child can find where the colonies and Native American lands were and explain how their locations and resources affected their growth and changes.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain the contributions of key individuals and groups to the foundation of the colonies, including Pilgrims, Puritans, Quakers, John Smith, Roger Williams, Anne Hutchinson, William Penn, Edward Winslow, William Bradford, John Winthrop, John Rolfe, and Pocahontas.
description_plain: Your child knows about important people and groups who helped start the colonies, like the Pilgrims, Puritans, Quakers, and leaders such as John Smith and Pocahontas.

subject: Social Studies
description: Compare and contrast the New England, Middle, and Southern colonies, including their physical geography, religion, education, economy, and government.
description_plain: Your child compares the New England, Middle, and Southern colonies by looking at their land, religion, schools, economy, and government.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain the importance of the founding and development of the Plymouth settlement, including practice of self-government established by the Mayflower Compact, religious freedom, and contributions of Native Americans, including Chief Massasoit and Squanto, the leadership of William Bradford.
description_plain: Your child explains why the Plymouth settlement was important, including its self-government, religious freedom, and help from Native Americans.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain the importance of the founding and development of Jamestown, including representative government established through the House of Burgesses, private ownership of land, introduction of slavery, and arrival of women and families.
description_plain: Your child understands why Jamestown was important, including its government, land ownership, start of slavery, and arrival of women and families.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the development of the settlements and colonies in the late sixteenth century through the seventeenth century.
description_plain: Your child analyzes how settlements and colonies developed from the late 1500s through the 1600s.

subject: Social Studies
description: Compare and contrast the motivations, challenges, and achievements related to exploration and settlement of North America by the British, Dutch, French, and Spanish, including the search for wealth, freedom, and a new life.
description_plain: Your child compares why and how the British, Dutch, French, and Spanish explored and settled in North America, including their goals and challenges.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain the significance of the land claims made in North America by European powers after 1600, including England, France, the Netherlands, Portugal, Russia, Spain, and Sweden and their effects on Native Americans.
description_plain: Your child explains why European countries claimed land in North America after 1600 and how this affected Native Americans.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze European exploration and colonization of North America.
description_plain: Your child analyzes how Europeans explored and settled in North America.

subject: Social Studies
description: Use maps to identify absolute location (latitude and longitude) and describe geographic characteristics of places in Louisiana, North America, and the world.
description_plain: Your child uses maps to find exact locations using latitude and longitude and describes features of places in Louisiana, North America, and the world.

subject: Social Studies
description: Use geographic representations and historical data to analyze events and developments in U.S. history from 1580 to 1791, including environmental, cultural, economic, and political characteristics and changes.
description_plain: Your child uses maps and historical information to study events in U.S. history from 1580 to 1791, including changes in environment, culture, economy, and politics.

subject: Social Studies
description: Compare and contrast events and developments in U.S. history from 1580 to 1791.
description_plain: Your child compares and contrasts important events in U.S. history from 1580 to 1791.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze connections between ideas, events, and developments in U.S. history within their global context from 1580 to 1791.
description_plain: Your child analyzes how ideas, events, and developments in U.S. history from 1580 to 1791 connect to the wider world.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain ideas, events, and developments in the history of the United States of America from 1580 to 1791 and how they progressed, changed, or remained the same over time.
description_plain: Your child explains important ideas and events in U.S. history from 1580 to 1791 and how they changed or stayed the same over time.
