subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze how national events and amendments to the U.S. Constitution influenced Louisiana from the 1860s to 1877, including changes to the Louisiana Constitution.
description_plain: Your child understands how national events and changes to the U.S. Constitution affected Louisiana and its state constitution from the 1860s to 1877.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze how Black Codes affected the lives of Black Americans, including the restriction rights to own and lease property, conduct business, bear arms, and move freely through public spaces.
description_plain: Your child understands how Black Codes limited the rights of Black Americans to own property, run businesses, have weapons, and move freely.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain how the presidential election of 1876 and the Compromise of 1877 led to the end of Reconstruction, and analyze short-term effects of the collapse of Reconstruction, including the decline of Black Americans in elected offices and loss of enforcement of the Fourteenth and Fifteenth Amendments.
description_plain: Your child can explain how the 1876 presidential election and the Compromise of 1877 ended Reconstruction and how this caused fewer Black Americans to hold office and less protection of their rights.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain the roles of Black politicians in Southern states during Reconstruction, including Oscar Dunn and P.B.S. Pinchback.
description_plain: Your child knows about Black politicians like Oscar Dunn and P.B.S. Pinchback and their roles during Reconstruction in Southern states.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe the role and motivations of carpetbaggers and scalawags during Reconstruction.
description_plain: Your child can describe who carpetbaggers and scalawags were and why they were involved in Reconstruction.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain the rise of violence and intimidation of Black Americans by groups, including the Ku Klux Klan, White League and Red Shirts and describe the significance of the Opelousas and Colfax Massacres.
description_plain: Your child understands how groups like the Ku Klux Klan used violence to scare Black Americans and knows about important events like the Opelousas and Colfax Massacres.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain how federal action affected individual rights and freedoms during the Reconstruction era, including through the Thirteenth Amendment, Freedmen's Bureau, Civil Rights Bill of 1866, Reconstruction Act of 1867, Fourteenth Amendment, Fifteenth Amendment, and analyze the challenges, achievements, and effectiveness of each.
description_plain: Your child can explain how laws and actions during Reconstruction, like the Thirteenth Amendment and Freedmen's Bureau, affected people's rights and freedoms, including their successes and challenges.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the development and effects of tenant farming and the sharecropping system in the postwar South.
description_plain: Your child understands how tenant farming and sharecropping worked in the South after the Civil War and how they affected people's lives.

subject: Social Studies
description: Compare and contrast plans for Reconstruction, including Lincoln's Ten Percent Plan, President Johnson's Plan, and the Radical Republican Plan for Reconstruction.
description_plain: Your child can compare different plans for rebuilding the country after the Civil War, like Lincoln's Ten Percent Plan and the Radical Republicans' plan.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the major events, key people, and effects of Reconstruction.
description_plain: Your child can explain the important events, people, and effects of the Reconstruction period.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe the significance of Lincoln's assassination, and how it affected the nation.
description_plain: Your child understands why Lincoln's assassination was important and how it changed the nation.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze Lincoln's Gettysburg Address and Second Inaugural Address, and explain how the ideas expressed affected the course of the war and show how ideas about equality changed over time.
description_plain: Your child can analyze Lincoln's speeches and explain how his ideas influenced the Civil War and changed views about equality.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe the roles and contributions of key individuals in the Civil War, including Jefferson Davis, Robert E. Lee, Thomas Stonewall Jackson, PGT Beauregard, Mary Walker, Clara Barton, Ulysses S. Grant, William Tecumseh Sherman, Robert Smalls, and the Louisiana Tigers.
description_plain: Your child knows about important people from the Civil War, like Jefferson Davis, Robert E. Lee, Clara Barton, and others, and what they did.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the purpose, significance, and consequences of the Emancipation Proclamation.
description_plain: Your child can explain why the Emancipation Proclamation was made, why it mattered, and what happened because of it.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the role of Louisiana in the Civil War and how the conflict affected Louisiana and its people, including the importance of its ports and the occupation of New Orleans.
description_plain: Your child understands Louisiana's role in the Civil War and how the war affected the state and its people, including its ports and New Orleans.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe the roles and experiences of soldiers, women, enslaved people, and freed people during the Civil War.
description_plain: Your child can describe what soldiers, women, enslaved people, and freed people experienced during the Civil War.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe the importance and outcomes of the major military engagements of the Civil War, including Manassas, Shiloh, Capture of New Orleans, Antietam, Gettysburg, Vicksburg, Siege of Port Hudson, Sherman's March to the Sea, and the surrender at Appomattox Court House.
description_plain: Your child knows about major Civil War battles and events, like Gettysburg and Sherman's March, and what happened because of them.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe the events leading to, significance of, and reaction to the Battle of Fort Sumter, including Lincoln's call for 75,000 volunteers.
description_plain: Your child can explain what led to the Battle of Fort Sumter, why it was important, and how Lincoln asked for volunteers afterward.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain Louisiana's decision to secede from the Union and its effects, including the state seizure of federal properties in Louisiana (the United States Arsenal and Barracks at Baton Rouge; United States Branch Mint).
description_plain: Your child understands why Louisiana left the Union and what happened when the state took control of federal buildings.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain why the Confederate states seceded from the Union.
description_plain: Your child can explain the main reasons why the Southern states left the United States.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the causes, course, and consequences of the Civil War.
description_plain: Your child can explain the causes, important events, and results of the Civil War.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze Lincoln's First Inaugural Address, and explain how the ideas expressed affected the cause and course of the Civil War.
description_plain: Your child can analyze Lincoln's First Inaugural Address and explain how his ideas affected the start and progress of the Civil War.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain the causes of and reactions to rebellions and raids, including the German Coast Uprising, Nat Turner's Rebellion, and John Brown's Raid on Harpers Ferry and subsequent trial.
description_plain: Your child understands the causes and reactions to rebellions like Nat Turner's Rebellion and John Brown's Raid.

subject: Social Studies
description: Compare and contrast various arguments on the issue of slavery and state's rights, including those expressed in the Lincoln-Douglas debates and during the 1860 presidential campaign.
description_plain: Your child can compare different opinions about slavery and states' rights, including those from the Lincoln-Douglas debates.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe the reasons for the formation of the Republican Party in 1854 and its founding platform.
description_plain: Your child knows why the Republican Party was formed in 1854 and what it stood for.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze major events, legislation, and court decisions from 1800 to 1861 that led to increasing sectionalism, including the Missouri Compromise of 1820, North Carolina v. Mann (1830), the Nullification Crisis (1831–1833), the Compromise of 1850, the Fugitive Slave Acts (1793, 1850), the Kansas-Nebraska Act (1854), and the Dred Scott decision (1857).
description_plain: Your child can explain important events and laws from 1800 to 1861 that increased tensions between the North and South.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain the ideas, key people, and events related to the growth of sectionalism and rising tension prior to the Civil War.
description_plain: Your child understands the ideas, people, and events that caused growing divisions before the Civil War.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain restrictions placed on the trade of enslaved people prior to the Civil War, including the Northwest Ordinance of 1787 and the Act Prohibiting Importation of Slaves of 1807.
description_plain: Your child can explain laws that limited the trade of enslaved people before the Civil War.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe the purpose, challenges, routes, and successes of the Underground Railroad and the key role played by Harriet Tubman.
description_plain: Your child knows about the Underground Railroad, its challenges and routes, and Harriet Tubman's important role.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the historical works and ideas of influential abolitionists, including Frederick Douglass' speech "The Constitution of the United States: Is It Pro-Slavery or Anti-Slavery?" and Harriet Beecher Stowe's Uncle Tom's Cabin.
description_plain: Your child can analyze the ideas and works of abolitionists like Frederick Douglass and Harriet Beecher Stowe.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain the effects of abolition efforts by key individuals and groups, including Sojourner Truth, William Lloyd Garrison, and the Quakers.
description_plain: Your child understands how people like Sojourner Truth and groups like the Quakers worked to end slavery.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain the development of education and prison reform movements, including those led by Horace Mann and Dorothea Lynde Dix.
description_plain: Your child can explain how education and prison reforms started, including the work of Horace Mann and Dorothea Dix.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the key people, ideas, and events of the women's rights movement and woman's suffrage movement of the early to mid-1800s, including the Seneca Falls Convention, National Women's Rights Conventions, Susan B. Anthony, Elizabeth Cady Stanton, Lucretia Mott, Sojourner Truth, Mary Church Terrell, and Margaret Fuller.
description_plain: Your child knows about important people and events in the early women's rights movement, like Susan B. Anthony and the Seneca Falls Convention.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze role and importance of social and political reform movements of the nineteenth century.
description_plain: Your child can explain the role and importance of social and political reform movements in the 1800s.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe push and pull factors for immigration to the United States in the early to mid-1800s, and explain how migration within and to the United States affected rural and urban areas.
description_plain: Your child can describe why people moved to and within the U.S. in the early 1800s and how this affected cities and rural areas.

subject: Social Studies
description: Compare and contrast the economies of the North and the South during the early to mid-1800s.
description_plain: Your child can compare the economies of the North and South in the early 1800s.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain how steamboats influenced Louisiana's economic growth and the significance of Captain Henry Miller Shreve in steamboat navigation.
description_plain: Your child understands how steamboats helped Louisiana's economy grow and why Captain Henry Miller Shreve was important for steamboat travel.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the development of the agrarian economy in the South, including Louisiana, and explain how advancements in technology, such as the cotton gin and multiple-effect evaporator for sugar, contributed to an increase in enslaved labor.
description_plain: Your child will learn how farming grew in the South, especially Louisiana, and how new machines like the cotton gin increased the use of enslaved workers.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain the causes and effects of the first Industrial Revolution in the United States, including advancements in technology, increased manufacturing, changing labor conditions, growing transportation systems, and urbanization.
description_plain: Your child can explain what caused the first Industrial Revolution in the U.S. and how new technology, factories, jobs, transportation, and cities changed life.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe the causes, course, and consequences of the Mexican-American War, including the Battle of the Alamo, Battle of San Jacinto, annexation of Texas, the Mexican Cession and Zachary Taylor's role in the war and subsequent election to the presidency.
description_plain: Your child will understand why the Mexican-American War happened, what happened during it, and its results, including important battles and leaders.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain the motivation and means of migration West, the experiences of the settlers, and resulting changes in the West, including the Gold Rush (1848–1855), trails (Oregon Trail, Mormon Trail, and Santa Fe Trail), first transcontinental telegraph, and the transcontinental railroad.
description_plain: Your child can explain why people moved west, what their journeys were like, and how events like the Gold Rush and building railroads changed the West.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze key events and developments that contributed to westward expansion, including the Oregon Treaty (1846), annexation of Texas (1845), Treaty of Guadalupe Hidalgo (1848), Gadsden Purchase (1853), the Pony Express (1860), Pacific Railway Act (1862), and Homestead Act (1862).
description_plain: Your child will learn about important events and laws that helped the U.S. grow westward, like treaties, land purchases, and new ways to communicate and travel.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the causes and effects of Indian Removal policies of the early to mid-1800s, including the Indian Removal Act of 1830, Trail of Tears, and Seminole Wars, and explain the role of key figures, including Andrew Jackson, Chief John Ross, and Chief Osceola.
description_plain: Your child can explain why Native Americans were forced to move in the 1800s, what happened during these removals, and the roles of leaders like Andrew Jackson and Chief Osceola.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the ideas and motivations that contributed to westward expansion, including Manifest Destiny, and its political, social, and economic effects.
description_plain: Your child will understand the ideas that made people want to move west, like Manifest Destiny, and how this affected politics, society, and the economy.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the effects of Marbury v. Madison (1803), McCulloch v. Maryland (1819), Gibbons v. Ogden (1824), and Worcester v. Georgia (1832).
description_plain: Your child can explain important court cases that shaped the government and laws in the early 1800s.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the purpose of the Monroe Doctrine (1823), with emphasis on its policies of both isolationism and protection of American interests in the Western Hemisphere, and how it influenced U.S. foreign policy and interactions with other nations.
description_plain: Your child will learn about the Monroe Doctrine and how it guided the U.S. to stay out of European conflicts while protecting its interests in the Americas.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe the Era of Good Feelings (1815–1825), including Henry Clay's American System, Treaty of 1818, Adams-Onis Treaty of 1819, and the development of transportation networks.
description_plain: Your child can describe the Era of Good Feelings, including important plans for the country, treaties, and improvements in transportation.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the growth and development of the United States from the early to mid-1800s.
description_plain: Your child will study how the United States grew and changed from the early to mid-1800s.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain the events leading to and surrounding Louisiana statehood, including the Neutral Strip, the West Florida controversy, and the capture of the Spanish Fort at Baton Rouge, as well as key figures including Julien de Lallande Poydras.
description_plain: Your child can explain the events and people involved in Louisiana becoming a state, including conflicts and important leaders.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain the importance and effects of the Battle of New Orleans to Louisiana, and describe the roles played by General Andrew Jackson and Jean Lafitte.
description_plain: Your child will understand why the Battle of New Orleans was important for Louisiana and learn about the roles of General Andrew Jackson and Jean Lafitte.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the interests and motivations of Native American groups aligned with the United States and with Britain during the War of 1812, including Chief Tecumseh.
description_plain: Your child can explain why some Native American groups sided with the U.S. or Britain during the War of 1812 and learn about leaders like Chief Tecumseh.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain key events, turning points and outcomes of the War of 1812, including blockades, Battle of Lake Erie (1813), Burning of Washington (1814), Battle of New Orleans (1814), Battles of Baltimore and Lake Champlain (1814), penning of the Star Spangled Banner, and the Treaty of Ghent (1814).
description_plain: Your child will learn about key events and outcomes of the War of 1812, including battles, the writing of the Star-Spangled Banner, and the peace treaty.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain the events leading to the War of 1812, including Britain's war with Napoleonic France, impressment, and blockades, and analyze the political and economic effects on the United States.
description_plain: Your child can explain what led to the War of 1812, including problems with Britain, and how the war affected the U.S. politically and economically.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the causes, course of, and consequences of the War of 1812.
description_plain: Your child will study the causes, events, and results of the War of 1812.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the major events of Thomas Jefferson's presidency, including the Louisiana Purchase, Lewis and Clark expeditions, Dunbar-Hunter Expedition of Ouachita River, Red River Expedition, and Twelfth Amendment.
description_plain: Your child can describe important events during Thomas Jefferson's presidency, like buying Louisiana, exploring new lands, and changes in government rules.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain how the U.S. government addressed foreign and domestic challenges during the late 1700s to the mid-1800s and how related policies and legislation influenced the development of the United States.
description_plain: Your child will learn how the U.S. government handled challenges at home and abroad in the late 1700s to mid-1800s and how laws shaped the country.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe the role of the Electoral College in presidential elections, including how it aims to ensure representation of less populated states.
description_plain: Your child can explain how the Electoral College works in choosing the president and why it helps smaller states have a voice.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain how the disagreements between Thomas Jefferson and Alexander Hamilton resulted in the emergence of the Federalist and Democratic-Republican political parties, including views on foreign policy, Alien and Sedition Acts, economic policy, National Bank, funding and assumption of the revolutionary debt.
description_plain: Your child will understand how disagreements between Thomas Jefferson and Alexander Hamilton led to the first political parties and different ideas about government and economy.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain the significance of the election of 1800.
description_plain: Your child can explain why the election of 1800 was important in U.S. history.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze key events of the presidency of John Adams including the Alien and Sedition Act and the XYZ affair.
description_plain: Your child will learn about key events during John Adams's presidency, including the Alien and Sedition Acts and the XYZ Affair.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain the influence of precedents set by the presidency of George Washington, and analyze the advice in and effects of his Farewell Address.
description_plain: Your child can explain how George Washington set examples as the first president and why his Farewell Address was important advice for the country.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain the causes and events of the Whiskey Rebellion, including the response from the Washington administration and its relationship to enforcement of the government's right to tax.
description_plain: Your child will understand the causes and events of the Whiskey Rebellion and how the government showed it could enforce taxes.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the influence of key events, ideas, and people on the economic, political, and social development of the United States from 1791–1850s.
description_plain: Your child will study important events, ideas, and people that shaped the U.S. from 1791 to the 1850s.

subject: Social Studies
description: Evaluate counterclaims.
description_plain: Evaluate counterclaims.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze causes and effects.
description_plain: Analyze causes and effects.

subject: Social Studies
description: Compare and contrast content and viewpoints.
description_plain: Your child can compare different ideas and points of view.

subject: Social Studies
description: Demonstrate an understanding of social studies content.
description_plain: Your child understands social studies topics.

subject: Social Studies
description: Construct and express claims that are supported with relevant evidence from primary and/or secondary sources, social studies content knowledge, and clear reasoning and explanations to:
description_plain: Your child can make and explain opinions using facts from history and other sources.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain how the availability of sources affects historical interpretations.
description_plain: Your child can explain how having different sources changes how we understand history.

subject: Social Studies
description: Compare and contrast multiple sources and accounts.
description_plain: Your child can compare and find differences and similarities in multiple sources and stories.

subject: Social Studies
description: Evaluate claims, counterclaims, and evidence.
description_plain: Your child can judge if claims and evidence are strong or weak.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze social studies content.
description_plain: Your child can study and understand social studies information.

subject: Social Studies
description: Use a variety of primary and secondary sources to:
description_plain: Your child will use different kinds of sources, like documents and stories, to learn about history.

subject: Social Studies
description: Use maps to identify absolute location (latitude and longitude) and describe geographic characteristics of places in Louisiana, North America, and the world.
description_plain: Your child can use maps to find exact places using latitude and longitude and describe features of places in Louisiana, North America, and the world.

subject: Social Studies
description: Use geographic representations and historical data to analyze events and developments in U.S. history from 1791 to 1877, including environmental, cultural, economic, and political characteristics and changes.
description_plain: Your child will use maps and historical facts to study events in U.S. history from 1791 to 1877, including changes in the environment, culture, economy, and politics.

subject: Social Studies
description: Compare and contrast events and developments in U.S. history from 1791 to 1877.
description_plain: Your child can compare and explain events and changes in U.S. history from 1791 to 1877.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze connections between ideas, events, and developments in U.S. history within their global context from 1791 to 1877.
description_plain: Your child will understand how ideas and events in U.S. history from 1791 to 1877 connect to the rest of the world.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain ideas, events, and developments in the history of the United States of America from 1791 to 1877 and how they progressed, changed, or remained the same over time.
description_plain: Your child can explain important ideas and events in U.S. history from 1791 to 1877 and how things changed or stayed the same over time.

subject: Social Studies
description: explain counterclaims
description_plain: explain counterclaims

subject: Social Studies
description: analyze causes and effects
description_plain: analyze causes and effects

subject: Social Studies
description: compare and contrast content and viewpoints
description_plain: Your child compares and contrasts different ideas and points of view.

subject: Social Studies
description: demonstrate an understanding of social studies content
description_plain: Your child shows they understand social studies topics.

subject: Social Studies
description: Construct and express claims that are supported with relevant evidence from primary and/or secondary sources, social studies content knowledge, and clear reasoning and explanations to
description_plain: Your child makes and explains claims using evidence from sources and social studies knowledge.

subject: Social Studies
description: distinguish between historical facts and historical interpretations
description_plain: Your child can tell the difference between facts and opinions about history.

subject: Social Studies
description: compare and contrast multiple sources and accounts
description_plain: Your child compares and contrasts different sources and stories about history.

subject: Social Studies
description: evaluate claims, counterclaims, and evidence
description_plain: Your child evaluates different claims and the evidence for and against them.

subject: Social Studies
description: analyze social studies content
description_plain: Your child analyzes social studies information.

subject: Social Studies
description: Use a variety of primary and secondary sources to
description_plain: Your child uses many types of sources to learn about history and social studies.

subject: Social Studies
description: explain the meaning of words, phrases, and content-specific vocabulary
description_plain: Your child can explain the meaning of important words and phrases used in social studies.

subject: Social Studies
description: determine the origin, author's point of view, intended audience, and reliability
description_plain: Your child can figure out where a source comes from, what the author's opinion is, who the source is for, and if it can be trusted.

subject: Social Studies
description: distinguish between primary, secondary, and tertiary sources
description_plain: Your child can tell the difference between original sources, secondhand accounts, and summaries or collections of information.

subject: Social Studies
description: Examine sources in order to
description_plain: Examine sources in order to

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe important issues of the 2008 presidential election and the significance of the election of Barack Obama.
description_plain: Your child will learn about important issues in the 2008 presidential election and why Barack Obama's election was significant.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe the effects of natural disasters on Louisiana and the United States, including hurricanes Katrina and Rita.
description_plain: Your child can explain how natural disasters like hurricanes Katrina and Rita affected Louisiana and the United States.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain how relationships between the United States and Middle East affected events and developments during the modern era, including Persian Gulf Wars, 1993 World Trade Center bombing, terrorist attacks on September 11, 2001, the War on Terrorism, and the establishment of the Department of Homeland Security.
description_plain: Your child can explain how the United States' relationships with the Middle East influenced events like the Persian Gulf Wars, 9/11 attacks, and the creation of the Department of Homeland Security.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain how events and developments of the modern era have affected American society.
description_plain: Your child understands how important events in recent history have changed American society.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe the importance of key ideas, events, and developments of the modern era.
description_plain: Your child can describe why key ideas and events from modern times are important.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain events and policies leading to the end of the Cold War and collapse of the Soviet Union under the leadership of President Reagan, including political and economic pressures, policies of glasnost and perestroika, and the fall of the Berlin Wall.
description_plain: Your child can explain the events and policies that ended the Cold War, like President Reagan's leadership, the fall of the Berlin Wall, and changes in the Soviet Union.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain how advances in technology and media during the mid- to late twentieth century changed society and public perception, including newspapers and television, the space race, and the nuclear arms race.
description_plain: Your child understands how new technology and media, like TV and the space race, changed society during the mid-1900s.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain how events during the Cold War affected American society, including the Second Red Scare and McCarthyism.
description_plain: Your child can explain how Cold War events like the Red Scare and McCarthyism affected life in America.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe the role of organizations and alliances during the Cold War, including the United Nations, NATO, and the Warsaw Pact.
description_plain: Your child can describe the roles of groups like the United Nations, NATO, and the Warsaw Pact during the Cold War.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze Cold War crises and conflicts and how they contributed escalating tensions, including the Berlin Blockade and Airlift, Korean War, Suez Crisis, U-2 Incident, Cuban Missile Crisis, Bay of Pigs Invasion, Berlin Crisis of 1961, and Vietnam War, Soviet-Afghan War.
description_plain: Your child can explain how Cold War conflicts like the Berlin Airlift, Korean War, Cuban Missile Crisis, and Vietnam War increased tensions between countries.

subject: Social Studies
description: Evaluate the effectiveness of U.S. policies, programs, and negotiation efforts in accomplishing their intended goals, including the Marshall Plan, containment and related doctrines, mutual assured destruction, détente, Strategic Arms Limitation Talks (SALT I and II), and Strategic Defense Initiative (Star Wars program).
description_plain: Your child can talk about how well U.S. plans like the Marshall Plan and arms control talks worked during the Cold War.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain how the ideologies of communism in the Soviet Union and capitalism in the United States influenced the Cold War and global tensions from 1945–1989.
description_plain: Your child understands how the ideas of communism and capitalism caused the Cold War and global conflicts from 1945 to 1989.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain the causes, course, and consequences of the Cold War.
description_plain: Your child can explain what caused the Cold War, what happened during it, and what effects it had.

subject: Social Studies
description: Evaluate legislation and amendments passed in response to the civil rights movement, including the Twenty-Fourth Amendment, Civil Rights Act of 1964, Voting Rights Act of 1965, and Civil Rights Act of 1968.
description_plain: Your child can talk about important laws and changes made during the civil rights movement, like the Civil Rights Act and Voting Rights Act.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the role of the Supreme Court in advancing civil rights and freedoms during the 1950s and 1960s, including the court cases of Brown v. Board of Education (1954), Boynton v. Virginia (1960), and Bailey v. Patterson (1962).
description_plain: Your child understands how the Supreme Court helped advance civil rights through cases like Brown v. Board of Education.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain reactions to the civil rights movement by opposing individuals and groups, including George Wallace and Leander Perez.
description_plain: Your child can explain how some people and groups, like George Wallace, opposed the civil rights movement.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain the role and importance of key individuals and groups of the civil rights movement, including the Congress of Racial Equality (CORE), Dr. Martin Luther King Jr., Rosa Parks, the Southern Christian Leadership Conference (SCLC), the Student Nonviolent Coordinating Committee (SNCC), Medgar Evers, Shirley Chisholm, Fannie Lou Hamer, and Malcolm X.
description_plain: Your child knows about important people and groups in the civil rights movement, like Dr. Martin Luther King Jr., Rosa Parks, and CORE.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze works of civil rights leaders, including Dr. King's "Letter from Birmingham Jail" and his "I Have a Dream" speech, and explain how the ideas expressed in the works influenced the course of the civil rights movement.
description_plain: Your child can explain how speeches and writings by civil rights leaders, like Dr. King's 'I Have a Dream,' influenced the movement.

subject: Social Studies
description: Evaluate the effectiveness of methods (civil disobedience, boycotts, sit-ins, marches, drives) during the civil rights movement, including during the 1960 Greensboro sit-ins, 1963 demonstrations in Birmingham, 1963 March on Washington, 1964 Freedom Summer, and 1965 Selma Marches.
description_plain: Your child can evaluate how effective different civil rights actions were, like sit-ins, marches, and boycotts.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the cause, course, and outcome of efforts to desegregate transportation, including the Baton Rouge Bus Boycott, Montgomery Bus Boycott, and Freedom Rides.
description_plain: Your child can explain the efforts to end segregation on buses, including the Montgomery Bus Boycott and Freedom Rides.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the importance of the Brown v. Board of Education (1954) decision and subsequent efforts to desegregate schools, including those of the Little Rock Nine at Central High School in Arkansas, Ruby Bridges at William Frantz Elementary in Louisiana, and James Meredith at the University of Mississippi.
description_plain: Your child understands the importance of Brown v. Board of Education and how students like the Little Rock Nine helped desegregate schools.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze how the murder of Emmett Till affected support for the civil rights movement.
description_plain: Your child can explain how the murder of Emmett Till increased support for the civil rights movement.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain the origins and goals of the civil rights movement of the 1950s and 1960s, and how segregation (de jure and de facto) affected African Americans and influenced the movement.
description_plain: Your child understands the start and goals of the civil rights movement and how segregation affected African Americans.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze events during and immediately after World War II leading to the civil rights movement, including Executive Order 8022 and Executive Order 9981.
description_plain: Your child can explain events during and after World War II that helped start the civil rights movement, like Executive Orders ending discrimination in the military.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze causes, major events, and key leaders of the Civil Rights Movement from 1954 to 1968.
description_plain: Your child can analyze the causes, major events, and leaders of the civil rights movement from 1954 to 1968.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain how key decisions from Allied conferences during World War II, including the Atlantic Charter, Tehran, Yalta, and Potsdam, affected the course of the war and postwar world.
description_plain: Your child can explain how important World War II meetings between Allied leaders affected the war and the world after.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe the importance of the Manhattan Project and development of atomic bombs, and analyze the decision to use them.
description_plain: Your child understands the importance of the Manhattan Project and the decision to use atomic bombs.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain the events that led to, and the conditions of the surrender of the Axis Powers in Europe and Asia, and describe the United States' critical role in the Allied victory.
description_plain: Your child can explain how World War II ended in Europe and Asia and the important role the U.S. played.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain how life in the United States changed during and immediately after World War II, with an emphasis on wartime production and the workforce, rationing, conservation, victory gardens, financing through war bonds, propaganda campaigns, and the Servicemen's Readjustment Act (GI Bill).
description_plain: Your child understands how life changed in the U.S. during and after World War II, including work, rationing, and support for soldiers.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze how Louisiana contributed to the war effort during World War II and the effects of the war on Louisiana, including the role of the Louisiana Maneuvers, Higgins Boats in the success of the Allies, and prisoner of war (POW) camps in Louisiana.
description_plain: Your child can describe how Louisiana helped during World War II and how the war affected the state.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain the sacrifices and contributions of U.S. soldiers during World War II such as the Tuskegee Airmen, the 442nd Regimental Combat team, the 101st Airborne, Cajun "Frenchies", the Women's Army Corps (WAC), and the Navajo Code Talkers.
description_plain: Your child knows about the sacrifices and contributions of U.S. soldiers and groups like the Tuskegee Airmen and Navajo Code Talkers during World War II.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain the causes and effects of Japanese internment in the United States during World War II.
description_plain: Your child can explain why Japanese Americans were put in internment camps during World War II and how it affected them.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe the Tuskegee Study conducted on Black Americans from the 1930s to 1972.
description_plain: Your child understands the Tuskegee Study and how Black Americans were treated unfairly from the 1930s to 1972.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain the causes and consequences of the Holocaust, including antisemitism, Nuremberg Laws restricting civil rights, resistance efforts, experiences of people including Anne Frank, concentration camp system, liberation of camps by the Allies, and Nuremberg trials.
description_plain: Your child can explain the causes and effects of the Holocaust, including how people suffered and how the Allies stopped it.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe the roles and importance of key figures of World War II, including leaders from the United States (Franklin D. Roosevelt, Harry S. Truman, Dwight D. Eisenhower, George Patton, Douglas MacArthur), Great Britain (Sir Winston Churchill), France (Charles de Gaulle), the Soviet Union (Joseph Stalin), Germany (Adolf Hitler), Italy (Benito Mussolini), and Japan (Michinomiya Hirohito, Hideki Tojo).
description_plain: Your child knows about important World War II leaders from the U.S., Britain, France, the Soviet Union, Germany, Italy, and Japan.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain the significance of major military actions and turning points during World War II in the Atlantic Theater (Battle of The Atlantic, Operation Torch, Battle of Normandy/Operation Overlord, Battle of The Bulge, Battle of Berlin) and the Pacific Theater (Battle of Bataan and Bataan Death March, Doolittle Raid, Battle of the Coral Sea, Battle of Midway, Battle of Leyte Gulf, Battle of Iwo Jima, Battle of Okinawa).
description_plain: Your child can explain major battles and turning points in World War II in both Europe and the Pacific.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe the role of alliances during World War II, including the Allies and Axis Powers.
description_plain: Your child can describe the roles of the Allies and Axis Powers during World War II.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe the causes of World War II, and analyze events that led to U.S. involvement in World War II, with emphasis on the attack on Pearl Harbor.
description_plain: Your child understands what caused World War II and why the U.S. entered the war, especially after Pearl Harbor.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe the acts of aggression leading to World War II in both Europe and Asia, and explain the effectiveness of policies and reactions, including the policy of appeasement towards Nazi Germany.
description_plain: Your child can explain aggressive actions by countries before World War II and how other countries responded, including trying to avoid war.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain the rise and spread of militarism and totalitarianism internationally, examining the similarities and differences between the ideologies of Imperial Japan, fascist Italy and Nazi Germany, and the communist Soviet Union, as well as the origins and effects of violence and mass murder in the 1930s and 1940s as demonstrated by the Nanjing Massacre, the Holodomor, the Holocaust, and treatment of political opponents and prisoners of war during World War II.
description_plain: Your child understands how militarism and totalitarian governments grew before World War II and the violence that happened, like the Holocaust and Nanjing Massacre.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe the causes, course, and consequences of World War II.
description_plain: Your child can describe the causes, events, and effects of World War II.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the purpose and effectiveness of the New Deal, including the Civilian Conservation Corps (CCC), Tennessee Valley Authority (TVA), Agricultural Adjustment Act, National Recovery Administration, Public Works Administration, Glass-Steagall Act, Federal Deposit Insurance Corporation (FDIC), Securities Exchange Act (SEC), National Housing Act, Works Progress Administration (WPA), and the Social Security Act (SSA).
description_plain: Your child can explain what the New Deal was and how programs like the CCC, TVA, and Social Security helped people during the Great Depression.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe the government response to the Great Depression, comparing the reaction of the Hoover and Roosevelt administrations.
description_plain: Your child can compare how Presidents Hoover and Roosevelt responded differently to the Great Depression.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe the causes and effects of the Dust Bowl, including agricultural practices, drought, and migration.
description_plain: Your child understands what caused the Dust Bowl and how it affected farming, weather, and people moving to new places.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain the effects of the Great Depression on people, including rising unemployment, foreclosures, growth of "Hoovervilles," and soup kitchens.
description_plain: Your child can describe how the Great Depression caused unemployment, home losses, and the rise of places like Hoovervilles and soup kitchens.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain the causes of the Great Depression, with an emphasis on how bank failures, buying stock on margin, overextension of credit, overproduction, high tariffs and protectionism, and the 1929 stock market crash contributed to the economic crisis.
description_plain: Your child can explain the main reasons the Great Depression happened, like bank failures, risky stock buying, and the 1929 stock market crash.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the causes and effects of the Great Depression.
description_plain: Your child can explain what caused the Great Depression and how it affected people and the country.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze factors leading to and consequences of social and economic tensions in the early twentieth century, including the 1918 influenza outbreak, recession and inflation, labor strikes, resurgence of the Ku Klux Klan, Chicago riot of 1919, and the Tulsa Massacre.
description_plain: Your child can explain events like the 1918 flu, labor strikes, and racial violence that caused social and economic problems in the early 1900s.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain the causes and effects of migration and population shifts in the United States during the early twentieth century, including the Great Migration.
description_plain: Your child understands why many people moved to new places in the early 1900s, including the Great Migration of African Americans.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze causes and effects of changes to the Louisiana Constitution over time, with emphasis on revisions from 1879 to 1974.
description_plain: Your child can explain how and why Louisiana's Constitution changed between 1879 and 1974.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze Louisiana politics in the early twentieth century, including the role of Huey Long's career in both Louisiana and national politics.
description_plain: Your child can describe Louisiana politics in the early 1900s and how Huey Long influenced the state and the nation.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe the causes of the Great Mississippi River Flood of 1927, and explain how the disaster and government response affected Louisianans.
description_plain: Your child can explain what caused the Great Mississippi River Flood of 1927 and how it affected people and government actions in Louisiana.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain how various factors affected Louisiana's economy during the early twentieth century, including booms in the timber, oil, and gas industries.
description_plain: Your child understands how industries like timber, oil, and gas helped Louisiana's economy grow in the early 1900s.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain the causes and effects of social and cultural changes of the 1920s and 1930s on the United States, and describe the influence of notable figures of the Harlem Renaissance (Louis Armstrong, Duke Ellington, Ella Fitzgerald, Langston Hughes, Zora Neale Hurston, Sargent Claude Johnson, Augusta Savage) and cultural figures (Amelia Earhart, Ernest Hemingway, Jacob Lawrence, Jesse Owens, and Babe Ruth).
description_plain: Your child can explain how life and culture changed in the 1920s and 1930s and learn about important people from the Harlem Renaissance and other cultural figures.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain the importance of the woman's suffrage movement and events leading to the passage of the Nineteenth Amendment, including the role of key figures such as Susan B. Anthony, Lucy Burns, Carrie Chapman Catt, Alice Paul, Elizabeth Cady Stanton, Lucy Stone, and Ida B. Wells.
description_plain: Your child understands why women fought for the right to vote and learns about key leaders who helped pass the Nineteenth Amendment.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain how advances in transportation, technology, and media during the early twentieth century changed society and culture in the United States, including the automobile, radio, and household appliances.
description_plain: Your child can explain how new inventions like cars, radios, and appliances changed how people lived in the early 1900s.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe the causes and consequences of Prohibition and the Eighteenth Amendment, including bootlegging and organized crime, and the repeal with the Twenty-First Amendment.
description_plain: Your child can describe why Prohibition started, how it led to illegal activities, and how it ended with the Twenty-First Amendment.

subject: Social Studies
description: Differentiate between the benefits and detriments of capitalism and communism, and explain how the concepts affected society during the early 1900s, including the Bolshevik Revolution and the first Red Scare.
description_plain: Your child can explain the differences between capitalism and communism and how these ideas affected the U.S. during the early 1900s.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the political, social, cultural and economic effects of events and developments during the early twentieth century.
description_plain: Your child can explain how politics, society, culture, and the economy changed in the early 1900s.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain the reaction of the U.S. Senate to the Treaty of Versailles and League of Nations, and describe the return to isolationism after the war.
description_plain: Your child understands why the U.S. Senate rejected the Treaty of Versailles and how the U.S. chose to stay out of world affairs after World War I.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe the goals of leaders at the Paris Peace Conference, comparing Woodrow Wilson's Fourteen Points, and the Treaty of Versailles.
description_plain: Your child can compare the goals of leaders at the Paris Peace Conference and explain Woodrow Wilson's Fourteen Points and the Treaty of Versailles.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain how military strategies and advances in technology affected warfare and the course of World War I, including trench warfare, airplanes, machine guns, poison gas, submarines, and tanks.
description_plain: Your child can explain how new weapons and strategies like trench warfare and tanks changed fighting during World War I.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain how the U.S. government directed public support and responded to dissent during World War I, including through the use of wartime propaganda, Committee on Public Information, Espionage Act, Sedition Act, and Schenck v. United States (1919).
description_plain: Your child understands how the U.S. government encouraged support for World War I and handled people who disagreed, using laws and propaganda.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze how the United States mobilized for war and ways the American people contributed to the war effort on the home front and abroad, with an emphasis on military service, role of women and minority groups, liberty bonds, and victory gardens.
description_plain: Your child can explain how the U.S. prepared for World War I and how people helped at home and in the military, including women and minorities.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the events leading to U.S. involvement in World War I, including German submarine warfare, the sinking of the Lusitania, and the Zimmerman Telegram.
description_plain: Your child understands the events that led the U.S. to join World War I, like submarine attacks and secret messages.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain the reasons for the initial U.S. policy of neutrality and isolationism.
description_plain: Your child can explain why the U.S. first tried to stay neutral and avoid getting involved in World War I.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe the causes of World War I, including militarism, alliances, imperialism, nationalism, and the assassination of Archduke Franz Ferdinand.
description_plain: Your child can describe the main causes of World War I, like military build-up, alliances, and the assassination of Archduke Franz Ferdinand.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the causes, course and consequences of World War I.
description_plain: Your child can explain what caused World War I, what happened during the war, and what effects it had afterward.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze foreign policy achievements of Theodore Roosevelt, including the construction of the Panama Canal and use of the Great White Fleet.
description_plain: Your child can explain how Theodore Roosevelt helped the U.S. grow stronger by building the Panama Canal and showing off the Navy with the Great White Fleet.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the ideas and events leading to the Spanish-American War and the short- and long-term outcomes, including the terms of the Treaty of Paris (1898), U.S. acquisition of Spanish territories, and emergence of the United States as a world power.
description_plain: Your child understands why the Spanish-American War started, what happened during the war, and how it made the U.S. a world power.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the events leading to and effects of the U.S. acquisition of Hawaii.
description_plain: Your child can explain how and why the U.S. took control of Hawaii and what effects this had.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the causes and effects of conflict between Native Americans and the U.S. government and settlers during the late nineteenth century and early twentieth century, including the Battle of Little Bighorn and Wounded Knee and subsequent treaties.
description_plain: Your child understands the conflicts between Native Americans and the U.S. government in the late 1800s, including important battles and treaties.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze how lives of Native Americans changed as a result of westward expansion and U.S. policies, including extermination of the buffalo, reservation system, Dawes Act, and assimilation.
description_plain: Your child can explain how Native American lives changed because of westward expansion, including loss of buffalo and new government policies.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze Frederick Turner's "The Significance of the Frontier in American History."
description_plain: Your child understands Frederick Turner's idea about how the American frontier shaped the country's history.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain the motivations for migration to and settlement of the West by various groups, including Exodusters, and how their motivations relate to the American Dream.
description_plain: Your child can explain why different groups moved west to settle, including African American Exodusters, and how this relates to the American Dream.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze ideas and events related to the expansion of the United States during the late nineteenth century and early twentieth century.
description_plain: Your child can explain important ideas and events about how the United States expanded in the late 1800s and early 1900s.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain the goals and strategies used by civil rights leaders of the late 1800s and early 1900s, and analyze differing viewpoints of key figures and groups, including W.E.B. DuBois and the Niagara Movement, Booker T. Washington, NAACP, Mary Church Terrell, and Ida B. Wells.
description_plain: Your child can explain the goals and methods of civil rights leaders in the late 1800s and early 1900s and how their ideas were different.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain the emergence of the Jim Crow system and how it affected Black Americans.
description_plain: Your child understands how the Jim Crow laws started and how they affected Black Americans.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the events leading to Plessy v. Ferguson (1896) and the consequences of the decision, including changes to the Louisiana Constitution.
description_plain: Your child can explain the Plessy v. Ferguson court case and how it affected laws in Louisiana.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain the origins and development of Louisiana public colleges and universities, including land grant institutions, Historically Black Colleges and Universities, and regional universities.
description_plain: Your child understands how public colleges and universities started in Louisiana, including schools for Black students and regional universities.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe important ideas and events of presidential administrations during the late 1800s and early 1900s, with emphasis on Theodore Roosevelt's administration and his support for trust busting, regulation, consumer protection laws, and conservation.
description_plain: Your child can describe important ideas and events during late 1800s and early 1900s presidencies, especially Theodore Roosevelt's work on breaking up trusts and protecting consumers and nature.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the government's response to the rise of trusts and monopolies, including the passage of the Interstate Commerce Act of 1887, the Sherman Antitrust Act of 1890, and the Clayton Antitrust Act of 1914.
description_plain: Your child can explain how the government responded to big businesses controlling markets by passing laws like the Interstate Commerce Act and the Sherman and Clayton Antitrust Acts.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the causes and outcomes of the Progressive movement and the role of muckrakers, including the Meat Inspection Act, Pure Food and Drug Act, Seventeenth Amendment, Thomas Nast, Ida Tarbell, Upton Sinclair, and Jacob Riis.
description_plain: Your child can describe what caused the Progressive movement, how writers called muckrakers helped, and the laws and changes that came from it, like food safety laws and the 17th Amendment.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe the reasons for and effects of the rise of Populism in the United States and Louisiana during the late 1800s, including the role of the Grange, Farmers' Alliance, and People's Party.
description_plain: Your child understands why the Populist movement grew in the late 1800s and how groups like the Grange and Farmers' Alliance worked to help farmers in the U.S. and Louisiana.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe the working conditions and struggles experienced by the labor force that led to the labor movement (child labor, hours, safety, wages, standard of living), and evaluate the effectiveness of efforts to improve conditions.
description_plain: Your child can describe the hard working conditions people faced, like long hours and unsafe jobs, and explain how workers tried to improve their lives.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain the causes and effects of immigration to the United States during the late 1800s and early 1900s, and compare and contrast experiences of immigrants.
description_plain: Your child can explain why many people moved to the U.S. in the late 1800s and early 1900s and compare what different immigrant groups experienced.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain how industrialization influenced the movement of people from rural to urban areas and the effects of urbanization.
description_plain: Your child understands how the growth of factories and industries caused people to move from farms to cities and what effects this had on communities.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the social, political, and economic changes that developed in the United States during the late nineteenth and early twentieth century.
description_plain: Your child can explain the important social, political, and economic changes that happened in the U.S. during the late 1800s and early 1900s.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain how industrialists and corporations revolutionized business and influenced the U.S. economy and society, with an emphasis on business practices (vertical and horizontal integration, formation of monopolies/trusts), development of major industries (oil, steel, railroad, banking), and the role of entrepreneurs, including Andrew Carnegie, J.P. Morgan, John D. Rockefeller, Cornelius Vanderbilt, and Madam C.J. Walker.
description_plain: Your child can explain how business leaders and companies changed how business worked, including how big companies formed and grew in industries like oil and steel.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain the social and economic effects of innovations in technology, transportation, and communication during the late 1800s and early 1900s, including the expansion of railroads, electricity, and telephone.
description_plain: Your child understands how new inventions and improvements in transportation and communication, like railroads and the telephone, affected society and the economy.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze factors that contributed to and effects of the growth of the industrial economy, including capitalism and the growth of free markets, mass production, agricultural advancements, the government's laissez-faire economic policy, and the rise of corporations.
description_plain: Your child can explain what helped the industrial economy grow, like free markets, mass production, farming improvements, and the rise of big companies.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the causes and effects of technological and industrial advances during the late nineteenth century and the early twentieth century.
description_plain: Your child can explain the causes and effects of new technology and industry growth during the late 1800s and early 1900s.

description: Analyze the Declaration of Independence and evaluate how the ideas expressed reflected the values and principles of the founders' and influenced development of the United States, with an emphasis on "inalienable rights" as inherent in all people by virtue of their being human meaning that they cannot be surrendered to the government; the rights to "life, liberty, and the pursuit of happiness" being the freedom to live, to protect rights, and to seek happiness as long as it does not violate the rights of others; and the concept of "consent of the governed" and how this differed from rule under a monarch.
description_plain: Your child understands the ideas in the Declaration of Independence about rights and government by the people.

Board of Education (1954); Baker v. Carr (1962); Engel v. Vitale (1962); Gideon v. Wainwright (1963); Miranda v. Arizona (1966); Loving v. Virginia (1967); Tinker v. Des Moines (1969); New York Times Co. v. United States (1971); Wisconsin v. Yoder (1972); Roe v. Wade (1973); United States v. Nixon (1974); Shaw v. Reno (1993); United States v. Lopez (1995); Bush v. Gore (2000); McDonald v. Chicago (2010); Citizens United v. Federal Election Commission (2010).
description_plain: Your child can explain important Supreme Court decisions, why they were made, and how they affected politics, society, and the economy.
